artist:abbey-lincoln.notes: Birth/death years (Aug 6 1930 - Aug 14 2010), vocals, We Insist! with Max Roach ('she sang on Max Roach's landmark civil rights-themed recording We Insist!'), civil-rights activism, and highly regarded late-career Verve albums ('a crowning achievement in Lincoln's career') all confirmed. 'Glamorous supper club singer' framing is editorial; early professional club singing in LA/Honolulu and the Marilyn Monroe-dress film appearance support it.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Abdullah Ibrahim (born Dollar Brand)
wrongClaims.problem: Dollar Brand was his earlier stage name, not his birth name; he was born Adolph Johannes Brand
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Abdullah Ibrahim (born Adolph Johannes Brand on 9 October 1934), previously known as Dollar Brand

artist:abdullah-ibrahim.notes: Right entity; single minor error. Everything else checks: born 1934, South African pianist, Mannenberg as anti-apartheid anthem ('the theme tune of the anti-apartheid movement'), Cape Town multicultural influences. Simple repair: 'formerly known as Dollar Brand'.

artist:abraham-burton.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead/biography: born March 17, 1971 in New York City, raised in Greenwich Village, Hartt School 1989-1993. Saxophone and living status confirmed.

artist:ahmad-jamal.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: 1930-2023, pianist, At the Pershing recorded 1958 at Pershing Hotel ('stayed on the Ten Best-selling charts for 108 weeks' supports best-seller claim), Poinciana on that album, influence on Miles Davis ('especially through its influence on Miles Davis... a seminal force'), minimalist style ('unusually minimalist and restrained style'). Unverifiable: the direct quote attributed to Jamal ('what you leave out is as important as what you put in') is not in evidence; 'Poinciana a jukebox hit' not in evidence; 'recording and performing into his 90s' not checkable (extract truncates in the 1970s; dates do confirm he lived to 92).

artist:al-di-meola.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: Return to Forever 1974, solo career from 1976, Elegant Gypsy, Friday Night in San Francisco, World Sinfonia trilogy. Born July 22, 1954; guitar; living status confirmed.

artist:albert-ayler.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: 1936-1970, tenor saxophone (switched from alto in the army), wide vibrato ('his wide vibrato was similar to that of gospel saxophonists'), Spiritual Unity and Ghosts both in evidence, march-like themes, Coltrane connection (signed to Impulse 'at the urging of Coltrane'). Unverifiable: the direct quote 'the father' attributed to John Coltrane is not in evidence; 'mysterious death in New York's East River' cannot be checked (extract truncates before his death; shortDescription confirms 1970 only). Punk/noise influence is editorial.

artist:alexander-von-schlippenbach.notes: Confirmed: born 7 April 1938, German jazz pianist and composer, founded the Globe Unity Orchestra at 28 (consistent with 'In 1966'), long-running trio with Evan Parker and Paul Lovens. 'A format inspired by both Duke Ellington and total freedom' and 'one of the longest-running groups in improvised music' are editorial characterizations not contradicted by evidence (trio discography spans 1960s-2010s).

artist:alice-coltrane.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: 1937-2007, pianist and harpist, own path after John's death July 17 1967, Swamini Turiyasangitananda, ashram founding (Vedantic Center 1975, Shanti Anantam 1983), Hindu devotional albums, return to jazz before death (Translinear Light 2004). Unverifiable: album titles 'Journey in Satchidananda' and 'Ptah, the El Daoud' are not in the extract (it names A Monastic Trio, Universal Consciousness, World Galaxy); 'taking over his band' is not stated; 'introduced the harp as a jazz instrument' outruns the evidence, which says only 'one of the few harpists in the history of jazz'.

artist:allan-holdsworth.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead, including the full band list (Soft Machine, U.K., The Tony Williams Lifetime, Pierre Moerlen's Gong, Bruford, Level 42, Planet X). Born 6 August 1946, died 15 April 2017; guitarist, violinist and composer confirmed.

artist:ambrose-akinmusire.notes: Confirmed: born 1982, trumpeter/composer, Blue Note artist (debut 2011), On the Tender Spot of Every Calloused Moment exists (2020, Grammy-nominated). Hip-hop connection supported by the To Pimp a Butterfly feature. 'Blending the tradition of Miles Davis and Freddie Hubbard with hip-hop, spoken word, and contemporary classical' and the album's racial-justice themes are editorial characterizations, not contradicted.

artist:amm.notes: Confirmed: founded 1965 in London; initial lineup Keith Rowe (guitar), Lou Gare (saxophone), Eddie Prevost (drums); avoidance of conventional melody/harmony/rhythm; influential in improvised music. birthYear 1965 = founding year (group entity). 'Radical politics' partially supported (Cardew and Rowe committed to socialism/Maoism); 'abstract expressionism' influence and post-punk/lowercase influence are editorial, not in evidence. Ours says 'electroacoustic improvisation group' where wiki says 'free improvisation group' - consistent given documented amplification/contact-mic practice, not a contradiction.

artist:andrew-hill.notes: STORED LINK PROBLEM: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Hill resolves to a disambiguation page ('Topics referred to by the same term'), not the artist page; the site is shipping a link that strands users on a disambiguation list. Correct target is 'Andrew Hill (jazz musician)'. The disambiguation line 'Andrew Hill (jazz musician) (1931-2007), American jazz pianist and composer' confirms our birthYear, deathYear, and instruments, so our prose appears to describe the right entity - but its substantive claims (1960s Blue Note albums, 'Point of Departure', academic years, 2000s comeback) cannot be checked against this page. Re-fetch evidence from the jazz-musician page before repair.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Andrew Wideman Bey Jr. (October 28, 1939 - April 26, 2025) was an American jazz singer and pianist.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Andy Bey is a vocalist and pianist possessing one of the deepest, most resonant baritone voices in jazz
wrongClaims.problem: Present tense / living status: he died April 26, 2025 in Englewood, New Jersey per evidence; the dataset ships deathYear null and a present-tense bio
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: He died in Englewood, New Jersey on April 26, 2025, at the age of 85.

artist:andy-bey.notes: Right entity. Other facts check: born Oct 28 1939, baritone voice ('four-octave baritone voice'), late-career HighNote recordings (2013, 2014). 'Recordings for Savant' is not in the evidence discography (no Savant titles listed; American Song was Savoy Jazz) - possibly a Savoy/Savant mix-up; unverified, not contradicted. Repair: set deathYear 2025 and shift bio to past tense; double-check the Savant claim.

artist:anthony-braxton.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: born 1945, key early AACM member, For Alto 1969 as 'the first full-length album of solo saxophone music', diagram-based compositional titles, operas (Trillium series), saxophonist/composer. Unverifiable: 'works for 100 orchestras' - evidence shows only 'compositions for four orchestras'; possibly a garbled magnitude, but absence is not contradiction, so flagging for review rather than as error. Instruments 'clarinet' and 'flute' not named in extract (he is described as a multi-instrumentalist who 'played over ten instruments').

artist:archie-shepp.notes: Confirmed: born May 24 1937, living, central avant-garde figure, political/Afrocentric work (Fire Music with its Malcolm X elegy, Attica Blues as response to the Attica riots), links to tradition and R&B ('recording blues, ballads, spirituals... tributes to... Charlie Parker and Sidney Bechet, while at other times dabbling in R&B'). Evidence calls him 'saxophonist' without specifying tenor/soprano - minor, not contradicted. Sound descriptions are editorial.

artist:arnold-schoenberg.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed nearly verbatim: 1874-1951, Austrian and American composer/music theorist/teacher, developing variation, the emancipation of the dissonance, motivic processes ('row-derived intervallic motives (or cells)'). Unverifiable: the 'writer' descriptor and the quoted concept 'unity of musical space' do not appear in the extract (bio likely lifted from an older Wikipedia lead where both appeared). 'Avant-garde' vs evidence's 'modernist' is characterization, not contradiction. A classical composer in the jazz artists dataset - presumably an intentional context entry.

artist:art-blakey.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: 1919-1990, drummer, Jazz Messengers led 35 years, 'incubator for young talent', alumni Wayne Shorter / Lee Morgan / Freddie Hubbard / Wynton Marsalis all in the evidence list, 'archetypal hard bop group', active until death. Unverifiable: Branford Marsalis as an alumnus (not in the evidence's explicitly non-exhaustive 'including...' list) and the 'signature press roll' detail. Both plausible; flag is a verification gap, not suspicion of corruption.

artist:art-ensemble.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: grew out of the AACM, all five named members with matching instruments (Mitchell sax, Bowie trumpet, Favors bass, Jarman sax, Moye drums/percussion from 1970), theatrical practice (costumes, face paint), breadth of styles. Unverifiable: the motto 'Great Black Music: Ancient to the Future' is only partially supported - evidence has a 1982 documentary titled 'Great Black Music - The Art Ensemble of Chicago' but the 'Ancient to the Future' half appears nowhere. birthYear 1967 is a defensible founding convention (Jarman joined in 1967; the 'Art Ensemble of Chicago' name itself dates from France 1969) - not contradicted, worth a reviewer's eye.

artist:art-pepper.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: alto saxophonist, occasional tenor/clarinet/bass clarinet, West Coast jazz, prominence in Stan Kenton's big band. Born September 1 1925, died June 15 1982 confirmed.

artist:art-tatum.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: widely regarded as one of the greatest jazz pianists, extraordinary technique, stride roots, reharmonization/voicing/bitonality. Born October 13 1909, died November 5 1956 confirmed. (The known 'In Private' corruption case concerned a Tatum album entry, not this artist bio - this bio is sound.)

artist:arthur-blythe.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: 1940-2017, alto saxophonist, 'straddling the avant garde and traditionalist jazz', Columbia Records 1978-1987 (covers 'early 1980s Columbia recordings'), moved to New York mid-1970s. Unverifiable: nickname 'Black Arthur' (evidence gives full name Arthur Murray Blythe, no nickname); album titles 'Lenox Avenue Breakdown' and 'Illusions' (extract names The Grip and Metamorphosis instead); 'key figure in New York's loft jazz scene' (loft scene never mentioned).

artist:arturo-sandoval.notes: Bio is the Wikipedia lead nearly verbatim: born 1949, influences Parker/Brown/Gillespie, met Gillespie 1977, defection during United Nations Orchestra tour, citizenship 1998, For Love or Country (2000) with Andy Garcia. One typo, not counted as factual error: ours reads 'United Nation Orchestra' where evidence reads 'United Nations Orchestra' - recommend cosmetic fix.

artist:arve-henriksen.notes: Bio is verbatim from Wikipedia: born 22 March 1968 in Stranda Municipality, Trondheim Musikkonservatorium jazz program, band history (Bodega Band, Luft, Veslefrekk, Close Enough, Nutrio, Trio Midt-Norge, Piggy Bop) all confirmed. Trumpet confirmed; 'vocals' supported by 'He also sings; his unique wordless vocalising was central to Chiaroscuro'.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: recorded only two albums for Blue Note before vanishing from the jazz scene entirely
wrongClaims.problem: He did not vanish after the Blue Note dates: he formed a Chicago trio in 1963, recorded two further leader albums for Argo (1964-65), and kept playing Chicago clubs until failing health
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: After his release, he returned to Chicago and formed his own trio in 1963.  He recorded two more albums for Argo.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Little is known about his life before or after these sessions
wrongClaims.problem: Evidence documents his life in detail both before (gospel upbringing, touring as pianist, 1952 debut recording, Vee-Jay sides, Milwaukee residency) and after (Argo albums, Squeeze Club residency, death from bronchial pneumonia 1971)
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: He spent his early career travelling across the United States, Canada and Cuba, as pianist with the bands of King Kolax, Joe Houston, Johnny Otis and Big Jay McNeely, among others.

artist:baby-face-willette.notes: Right entity; the 'mysterious enigma' skeleton is the problem, not the identity. Confirmed: birth/death years 1933/1971 (note: evidence flags source variance - 'other sources state 1934 or 1937' - ours matches the primary 1933), Hammond organ, exactly two Blue Note leader albums (Face to Face and Stop and Listen, both 1961). Repair: keep the two-Blue-Note-albums fact, drop 'vanishing entirely' and 'little is known' (he moved to Argo and Chicago club work); 'one of jazz's most enigmatic figures' is editorial but now unsupported.

artist:ben-webster.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: American jazz tenor saxophonist, performed in the US and Europe, recordings with Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday, Johnny Hodges. Born March 27 1909, died September 20 1973 confirmed.

artist:benny-goodman.notes: All claims verified: 1909-1986, clarinetist/bandleader, 'King of Swing', January 16, 1938 Carnegie Hall concert described as 'the single most important jazz or popular music concert in history', and 'led one of the first integrated jazz groups, his trio and quartet'.

artist:betty-carter.notes: Dates (1929-1998), vocals, and improvisation/scatting claims verified. Two substantive claims cannot be checked: 'She ran her own label for years' and 'mentored countless young players through her working bands' -- the extract truncates around 1961, before either topic. Background knowledge says both are true (Bet-Car Records, her bands as a training ground), but that is not citable here; no contradiction found.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: His 1992 album 'Celebration' on Verve Records
wrongClaims.problem: Celebration was released in 1991 on World Circuit, not 1992 on Verve; Verve signed him only after Celebration
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: His 1991 album Celebration, which featured Courtney Pine among a number of British players as guests, was nominated for a Mercury Music Prize. After this, Mseleku was taken up by Verve Records for several albums.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: His 1992 album 'Celebration' on Verve Records
wrongClaims.problem: discography confirms label and year: World Circuit, 1991
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Celebration (World Circuit, 1991)

wrongClaims.ourClaim: featured Joe Henderson and Pharoah Sanders
wrongClaims.problem: per evidence, Celebration's guests were Courtney Pine and British players; Joe Henderson appeared on the first of the later Verve albums, not on Celebration; Pharoah Sanders is not mentioned anywhere in the evidence
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: The first of these featured a number of American players, including Joe Henderson, Abbey Lincoln and Elvin Jones.

artist:bheki-mseleku.notes: Right entity (1955-2008, South African, pianist/saxophonist, self-taught, spiritual themes all match), but the album sentence conflates Celebration (World Circuit, 1991, Courtney Pine) with his subsequent Verve recordings (Joe Henderson et al.). 'Struggles with health and addiction': health supported (diabetic, bipolar diagnosis); addiction not mentioned in evidence. Repair should rewrite the Celebration sentence.

artist:bill-evans.notes: Core skeleton fully verified: 1929-1980, piano, Kind of Blue with Miles Davis (modal jazz), trio with Scott LaFaro and Paul Motian, LaFaro's death in a 1961 car crash. Unverifiable claims: 'struggles with drug addiction' (extract truncates before this; widely documented but not citable here); influence specifically on 'Keith Jarrett, Brad Mehldau, Fred Hersch' (evidence says only 'continue to influence jazz pianists today'). 'Debussy and Ravel' maps plausibly to evidence's 'impressionist harmony' but the names are not in the extract. No contradictions.

artist:bill-frisell.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (ECM 1980s, Downtown Scene, John Zorn, Paul Motian's groups until Motian's death in 2011). Born March 18, 1951; alive (deathYear null correct).

artist:billie-holiday.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; 1915-1959 verified. Data-quality flag (not a contradiction): instruments field is ['musician'], a non-instrument placeholder -- evidence supports 'vocals' ('American jazz singer'). Recommend field cleanup.

artist:billy-cobham.notes: All claims verified: born 1944 in Colon, Panama, raised in Brooklyn; Horace Silver then Miles Davis before co-founding Mahavishnu Orchestra with John McLaughlin (1971); Birds of Fire (1973) and solo debut Spectrum (1973); influence on rock/metal drummers (Bill Bruford, Danny Carey of Tool); multiple bass drums and early electric drum kit support the double-bass/electronic claims.

artist:billy-harper.notes: Verified: born 1943, tenor saxophonist, Coltrane-influenced ('one of a generation of Coltrane-influenced tenor saxophonists'), Lee Morgan sideman (The Last Session; present at Morgan's death per I Called Him Morgan), formed his own group in 1979, spiritual-jazz framing supported by the Capra Black description ('profoundly spiritual effort').

artist:bireli-lagrene.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead. Born 4 September 1966; alive; guitar verified.

artist:blue-mitchell.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (Riverside/Mainstream/Blue Note, most recorded trumpeter with jazz organ, 27 sideman recordings). 1930-1979 verified.

artist:bobbi-humphrey.notes: Verified: born 1950, flute, first female instrumentalist signed by Blue Note (1971), Blacks and Blues with the Mizell Brothers, jazz/funk/soul blend. One substantive claim exceeds evidence scope: ours says the album 'became one of Blue Note's biggest sellers' (label-wide), while evidence says it 'was one of her biggest selling albums for Blue Note' (artist-scoped, peaked no. 84 Billboard 200). Not a contradiction, but the label-wide superlative cannot be checked; repair could rescope to match evidence.

artist:bobby-hutcherson.notes: Verified: 1941-2016, vibraphone and marimba, 1960s Blue Note work with Jackie McLean (One Step Beyond, his first Blue Note date), Eric Dolphy, and Andrew Hill. Avant-garde-plus-lyricism framing is editorial and consistent with the post-bop/free jazz session history.

artist:bobby-timmons.notes: Bio is essentially verbatim the Wikipedia lead (two Jazz Messengers stints, Cannonball Adderley in between, 'Moanin''/'Dat Dere'/'This Here', early-mid 1960s trios). 1935-1974 verified. Minor field note: instruments lists 'composer' (a role, not an instrument) alongside piano; both supported by 'American jazz pianist and composer'.

artist:bobo-stenson.notes: Verified: born 1944, Swedish pianist, long-running trio, deep ECM association (many ECM titles in discography), worked with Jan Garbarek (co-led Witchi-Tai-To and Dansere) and joined Charles Lloyd's quartet in 1988 (five Lloyd ECM albums listed). 'Nordic aesthetics' language is editorial.

artist:booker-little.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (Max Roach, John Coltrane, Eric Dolphy; influenced by Sonny Rollins and Clifford Brown; died aged 23). 1938-1961 verified.

artist:brad-mehldau.notes: Verified: born 1970, trio since early 1990s, Art of the Trio series praised, pop/rock/classical absorption, simultaneous melodies in separate hands influencing other musicians, Joshua Redman quartet mid-1990s, Pat Metheny collaboration, solo piano formats. Unverified illustrative examples: 'Radiohead' and 'Nick Drake' are not named in the extract (the underlying claim -- pop interpretations expanding repertoire -- is directly supported). Note: evidence complicates the 'Bill Evans's lyricism' framing (critics reassessed the Evans comparison), but ours phrases it as editorial interpretation, not flagged.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Branford Marsalis (born August 26, 1960) is an American saxophonist, composer, and bandleader.

artist:branford-marsalis.notes: Bio prose is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified (Branford Marsalis Quartet, classical soloist work, Buckshot LeFonque, Tonight Show Band 1992-1995); born 1960, alive. The defect is the instruments field: it lists only the roles 'composer' and 'bandleader' and omits his actual instrument, saxophone -- consistent with a parse artifact that dropped the first token of 'saxophonist, composer, and bandleader'. Repair: set instruments to saxophone (he plays soprano and tenor per the extract).

artist:jack-mcduff.notes: Verified: 'Brother' Jack McDuff, 1926-2001, organist, soul jazz era prominence, gave George Benson his first break (young Benson in his groups), 1960s recordings on Prestige and then Atlantic. Minor: 'Hammond B-3' model is not specified in the extract (evidence says only 'organist'); standard shorthand, not flagged as substantive.

artist:bud-powell.notes: Verified: 1924-1966 (evidence explicitly notes the 1922 birth-certificate date was incorrect; ours matches the correct 1924), 1945 police beating followed by institutionalizations, attacking right-hand approach breaking from stride's left-hand style, late-1940s/early-1950s recordings with Parker/Stitt/Roach important to modern jazz piano. 'Influencing every jazz pianist from Bill Evans to Herbie Hancock' is editorial hyperbole; evidence names Walter Davis Jr., Toshiko Akiyoshi, Barry Harris instead -- not contradicted, not flagged.

artist:buddy-bolden.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (cornetist, key figure in New Orleans ragtime/'jass' becoming jazz). 1877-1931 verified.

artist:buster-smith.notes: Verified: 1904-1991, Texas-born alto saxophonist and clarinetist, mentor to the young Charlie Parker, Kansas City-area scene via the Blue Devils, only leader album The Legendary Buster Smith (Atlantic, 1959). Unverifiable: 'co-wrote One O'Clock Jump with Count Basie' -- the tune is never mentioned in the evidence; this credit is historically contested (often officially credited to Basie alone), so it needs a real source before shipping. Also 'guitarist' in instruments: evidence shows only bass guitar, taken up after his 1960s auto accident; his father was the guitarist. No contradictions.

artist:cal-tjader.notes: Verified: 1925-1982, vibraphone plus drums/bongos/congas/timbales (percussion), Dave Brubeck Octet/Trio and George Shearing sideman, own Latin combos from 1954 to his death (~three decades), prolific on Fantasy and Verve, Soul Sauce (1964) his biggest success. HIGH-PRIORITY REVIEW: ours says Soul Sauce 'won a Grammy' -- evidence instead says 'His Grammy Award in 1980 for his album La Onda Va Bien capped off a career', strongly implying his Grammy was for La Onda Va Bien, not Soul Sauce; background knowledge agrees Tjader's only Grammy was 1980 La Onda Va Bien. The extract truncates mid-discussion of Soul Sauce and never explicitly denies it a Grammy, so per the rules this is unverifiable, not factual_error -- but repair should treat the Grammy attribution as almost certainly wrong. 'One of the best-selling Latin jazz albums ever recorded' also unverifiable (evidence only artist-scoped).

artist:cannonball-adderley.notes: Verified: 1928-1975, alto sax, moved from Florida to New York in 1955, hailed as heir to Charlie Parker's mantle ('new Bird'), blues-rooted style, Kind of Blue with Miles Davis, quintet with brother Nat, 'Mercy, Mercy, Mercy' (1966 soul jazz hit), died at 46 after a stroke. Minor unverified color: 'hosting educational concerts and TV programs' -- educator role supported (narrated The Child's Introduction to Jazz, 1961), but TV hosting specifically is not in the extract.

artist:carla-bley.notes: Verified: 1936-2023, composer/pianist/organist/bandleader, jazz opera Escalator over the Hill (triple LP), co-led the Jazz Composers' Orchestra and started its JCOA label ('helped found' consistent), big band work, compositions performed by many artists. Minor unverified: 'self-taught' -- evidence says her piano-teacher father encouraged her to learn piano and does not address her training as a composer; commonly described as self-taught as a composer, but not checkable here. Not contradicted.

artist:carmen-mcrae.notes: Verified: 1920-1994, vocals and piano (early jobs as pianist with Benny Carter's band and at Minton's), Billie Holiday as primary influence ('disciple' supported; always sang at least one Lady Day song), developed her own style (Chicago years), behind-the-beat phrasing and ironic lyric interpretation support the diction/phrasing/wit description. Minor unverified color: 'her between-song commentary became legendary' is not in the extract; reputational flourish, not contradicted.

artist:cassandra-wilson.notes: All checkable claims verified: born 1955, jazz singer (vocals), blending with blues/country/folk per the Giddins quote ('incorporating blues, country, and folk music into her work'), two Grammy Awards. 'Roots music' is a fair umbrella for the country/blues repertoire described (Robert Johnson, Hank Williams). Rest is editorial.

artist:cecil-taylor.notes: Core facts verified: 1929-2018, pianist, free jazz pioneer, 'Bela Bartok and Karlheinz Stockhausen notably influenced his music', technique 'compared to percussion', MacArthur Fellowship 1993, long pieces (59-minute solo performance cited). Unverifiable from the extract (truncates mid-2000s): Kyoto Prize, working as a dishwasher between gigs, performing into his 80s. No contradictions.

artist:cedar-walton.notes: Verified: 1934-2013, joined Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers in the early 1960s as pianist-arranger (same day as Freddie Hubbard), 'Bolivia' and 'Firm Roots' both named as jazz standards, in-demand sideman (Prestige house rhythm section), career from 1958 recording debut to 2013 death supports 'over five decades'. 'Most celebrated lineups' and 'musician's musician' are editorial.

artist:charles-gayle.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; 1939-2023 matches. All five instruments confirmed: tenor sax (primary reputation), alto sax and bass clarinet and piano ('notably the piano (his original instrument), violin, bass clarinet and alto saxophone'), bass (lede lists 'bassist').

artist:charles-lloyd.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born March 15, 1938 matches birthYear 1938; tenor saxophone and flute confirmed; Moran/Rogers/Harland quartet since 2007 confirmed verbatim.

artist:charles-mingus.notes: Most claims verified: 1922-1979, upright bassist/composer/bandleader, 'major proponent of collective improvisation', Mingus Ah Um (1959) and The Black Saint and the Sinner Lady (1963) named as seminal, autobiography Beneath the Underdog, political themes ('racism, discrimination, and (in)justice'), classical elements, volcanic temper (fired by Ellington after backstage fight). Unverifiable from the extract (truncates ~1953): gospel element, stopping performances to berate sidemen, workshop approach to rehearsals. No contradictions.

artist:charlie-christian.notes: Bio tracks the Wikipedia lede nearly verbatim: 1916-1942, first electric guitarists, key figure in bebop and cool jazz, Benny Goodman Sextet and Orchestra Aug 1939-June 1941. The 'rhythm instrument into a leading solo voice' clause matches 'helped bring the guitar out of the rhythm section and into the forefront as a solo instrument'.

artist:charlie-haden.notes: Verified: 1937-2014, 'original member of the ground-breaking Ornette Coleman Quartet', 'liberating bassists from a strictly accompanying role', Liberation Music Orchestra combining free jazz and political music (Spanish Civil War material), duo recordings with Pat Metheny. Unverifiable: duet recordings with Keith Jarrett specifically -- evidence documents Jarrett trio/quartet/quintet membership and names Metheny/Hank Jones/Kenny Barron as duo partners, but no Jarrett duets (list is non-exhaustive, so not a contradiction).

artist:charlie-parker.notes: Verified: 1920-1955 (died at 34), nicknamed 'Bird', leading figure in development of bebop, Kansas City jam-session origins, Gillespie association, growing drug addiction. Unverifiable from the extract (truncates ~1944): Savoy and Dial recordings, 'Ko-Ko'/'Ornithology'/'Confirmation', revolutionizing jazz at 52nd Street clubs (extract shows Harlem venues), heroin specifically (extract says 'drug addiction'). No contradictions.

artist:chet-baker.notes: Verified: 1929-1988, trumpet and vocals, joined Gerry Mulligan Quartet in 1952 and attracted considerable attention, 'My Funny Valentine' hit associated with him for life, imprisoned in Lucca (Italy) on narcotics charges, cool jazz fame, career resurgence after obscurity implied by lede. Unverifiable: 'pianoless' quartet detail and death from a fall in Amsterdam (death date May 13, 1988 matches but the extract truncates in 1963 and never states cause/place). No contradictions.

artist:chick-corea.notes: Verified: 1941-2021, Miles Davis band late 1960s / birth of jazz fusion, Return to Forever with Hispanic/Latin styles, 'Spain', 'La Fiesta' and 'Windows' listed as jazz standards, collaborations with Herbie Hancock (duet concerts) and Bela Fleck (The Enchantment). Unverifiable: devoted Scientologist, death from cancer (extract gives only the date), 'brought fusion to arenas'. No contradictions.

artist:chick-webb.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia page, including the 'year of his birth is disputed' caveat. birthYear 1905 matches the page's primary date (supported by Britannica, AllMusic, census; grave marker says 1909, NYT said 1907 -- the bio itself flags the dispute). Died June 16, 1939; drums confirmed.

artist:chris-mcgregor.notes: Verified: 1936-1990 South African jazz pianist/bandleader/composer, founded and led the Blue Notes, created Brotherhood of Breath in 1969 incorporating 'leading English improvisors', synthesis of South African black traditional music and jazz; 'Years in exile' section confirms exile. Unverifiable from extract: that McGregor was white and the Blue Notes multi-racial, apartheid as the cause of exile, London specifically as the band's base (England implied by 'leaving England'), 'township jazz' label. No contradictions.

artist:chris-potter.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede and fully verified (born Jan 1, 1971; Red Rodney 1992-1993, Motian 1994-2009, Holland 1999-2007, Douglas 1998-2003). Single gap: instruments field lists bass clarinet, which the extract never names -- it only says 'multi-instrumentalist' and documents saxophone. Consistent but not checkable. No contradictions.

artist:christian-sands.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born May 22, 1989 matches birthYear 1989; pianist confirmed; Be Water (Mack Avenue, 2020) Grammy nomination for 'Be Water II' in Best Instrumental Composition confirmed.

artist:clark-terry.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; 1920-2015 matches; swing and bebop trumpeter, flugelhorn pioneer, composer and educator all confirmed.

artist:clifford-brown.notes: Verified: 1930-1956, 'died at the age of 25 in a car crash, leaving behind four years' worth of recordings', Brown/Roach Quintet with classic albums (Clifford Brown & Max Roach, Study in Brown, At Basin Street), Gillespie comparison ('Clifford Brown - the New Dizzy'). Unverifiable from extract: 'Brownie' nickname, clean living (no drugs/alcohol), influence on Lee Morgan/Freddie Hubbard/Wynton Marsalis. No contradictions.

artist:coleman-hawkins.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede, including the Berendt and Chilton quotes; 1904-1969 matches; tenor saxophonist confirmed (instruments list 'saxophone' + 'tenor saxophone' is redundant but not wrong).

artist:count-basie.notes: Verified: 1904-1984, 'American jazz pianist, organist, bandleader, and composer' verbatim, 'led the group for almost 50 years', minimalist piano style, rhythm-section emphasis, 'One O'Clock Jump' as his standard. Unverifiable: 'April in Paris' as one of his iconic recordings -- not in the extract (extract truncates in the 1930s; not a contradiction since the bio claims a recording, not a composition). No contradictions.

artist:danilo-perez.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born December 29, 1965 matches; Panamanian pianist, composer, educator, social activist all confirmed.

artist:darcy-james-argue.notes: Verified: born 1975 in Vancouver (Canadian-born), moved to Brooklyn 2003 (Brooklyn-based), 18-piece Secret Society, Infernal Machines Grammy nomination for Best Large Jazz Ensemble Album. Unverifiable from extract: 'has been called the most exciting large jazz ensemble of the 21st century' (no such attribution), fusion with indie rock/electronica/minimalism, steampunk-influenced themes, conductor credit (bandleader confirmed, conducting not stated). No contradictions.

artist:darius-milhaud.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; 1892-1974 matches; Les Six, polytonality, jazz and Brazilian influences, and the full student list (Bacharach, Brubeck, Glass, Reich, Kurtag, Stockhausen, Xenakis) all appear verbatim in evidence.

artist:dave-brubeck.notes: Verified: 1920-2012, unusual time signatures, quartet with Paul Desmond, Time Out (1959) 'the first jazz album to sell over one million copies' featuring 'Take Five' and 'Blue Rondo a la Turk', student of Darius Milhaud, counterpoint and contrasting tonalities, Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award 1996. Unverifiable: 1950s college campus tours (extract truncates at quartet formation); 'performed into his 90s' only loosely supported -- he died at 91 and the page says he 'continued releasing music until his death', not performing. No contradictions.

artist:dave-holland.notes: Stored URL resolves to a DISAMBIGUATION page ('Topics referred to by the same term'), not the bassist's article -- the site is shipping users a list that includes a rock drummer, a Klansman and a rugby player. Correct target per the disambiguation entry is the article titled 'Dave Holland (bassist)' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dave_Holland_(bassist)). The one-line entry 'Dave Holland (bassist) (born 1946), British jazz bassist and composer' corroborates birthYear 1946 and bass, but none of the bio claims (Miles Davis, Bitches Brew, Circle with Chick Corea, ECM albums, big band, educator) can be checked against it. No contradictions; prose is plainly about the bassist, not another Dave Holland.

artist:dave-weckl.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born January 8, 1960 matches; jazz fusion drummer, Dave Weckl Band leader, Modern Drummer Hall of Fame 2000 all confirmed. 'Percussion' in instruments treated as consistent with drummer (drums are percussion; extract also shows his Latin Percussion instructional videos).

artist:david-murray.notes: Stored wikipedia URL returned page: null — no evidence document available, nothing could be checked. All bio claims (birthYear 1955, 1970s loft-jazz scene, co-founded World Saxophone Quartet, 100+ albums as leader, Gonsalves/Ayler lineage) remain unchecked. Nothing suggests an entity mismatch; the stored URL targets the disambiguated title David_Murray_(musician). Needs re-fetch or alternate source.

artist:david-s-ware.notes: Bio is near-verbatim the Wikipedia lede/biography opening. All claims confirmed: dates (November 7, 1949 – October 18, 2012 -> 1949/2012), Plainfield NJ birth, Scotch Plains upbringing, UConn music camp taught by Ron Carter, Charlie Mariano, and Alan Dawson, school bands and New Jersey All-State band.

artist:david-sanborn.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Dates confirmed (July 30, 1945 – May 12, 2024 -> 1945/2024), alto saxophonist, jazz/pop/R&B blend, saxophone at age 11, first solo album Taking Off (1975) all confirmed.

artist:dennis-chambers.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Born May 9, 1959 (birthYear 1959 ok, living per evidence), jazz fusion/funk drummer, Modern Drummer Hall of Fame 2001 confirmed.

artist:derek-bailey.notes: Dates 1930/2005 confirmed (29 January 1930 – 25 December 2005). 'British' consistent with evidence 'English'. Non-idiomatic free improvisation confirmed verbatim concept. 'He founded Incus Records' — evidence: 'Bailey founded the record label Incus with Tony Oxley, Evan Parker, and Michael Walters', 'the first musician-owned independent label in the UK'; ours' 'one of the first musician-owned labels' compatible, though co-founders are omitted (legitimate editorial compression, not error). Book title rendered as 'Improvisation: Its Nature and Practice in Music' vs evidence's shorter 'Improvisation: Its Nature and Practice' — no contradiction, evidence appears to abbreviate.

artist:dexter-gordon.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: dates 1923/1990 (February 27, 1923 – April 25, 1990), tenor saxophone, large sound and behind-the-beat phrasing ('characterized as being "large"... tendency to play behind the beat'), early bebop influence ('among the most influential early bebop musicians', early influence on Coltrane and Rollins), Round Midnight Oscar nomination ('nominated for the Academy Award for Best Actor... Round Midnight'). Unverifiable: 'spent years in European exile before a triumphant comeback' — the extract truncates at his 1959 Folsom release and never reaches the 1960s-70s; zero coverage of the European period. Background knowledge (Copenhagen/Paris 1962-76, 1976 US comeback) supports the claim but is not citable. Re-check against the full article.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: collaborating with Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo to create groundbreaking fusions like 'Manteca' and 'A Night in Tunisia.'
wrongClaims.problem: Ours presents 'A Night in Tunisia' as created through the Chano Pozo collaboration. Evidence dates the song's creation to 1942 in the Earl Hines band context and never associates Pozo with it — the song predates and is independent of the Afro-Cuban collaboration.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: "A Night in Tunisia", written in 1942 while Dizzy was with Earl Hines's band, is noted for its syncopated bass line - a feature common in today's music.

artist:dizzy-gillespie.notes: Right entity; everything else checkable is confirmed: dates 1917/1993 (October 21, 1917 – January 6, 1993), trumpet virtuoso, bebop development with Charlie Parker, harmonic/rhythmic innovation, bent horn / pouched cheeks / light-hearted personality, taught and influenced many musicians. Unverifiable from the truncated extract (ends early 1945): 'Manteca' as a Pozo collaboration and the State Department-sponsored tours — both consistent with common knowledge but uncovered here. Repair suggestion: attach Pozo to 'Manteca' only and treat 'A Night in Tunisia' as a separate 1942 Gillespie composition.

artist:django-reinhardt.notes: Dates 1910/1953 confirmed (23 January 1910 – 16 May 1953). Romani guitarist, caravan (wagon) fire, Grappelli partnership, gypsy jazz all confirmed. Two near-superlatives kept as editorial, not flagged: 'the first major jazz voice to emerge from Europe' vs evidence 'one of the first major jazz talents to emerge in Europe'; 'only two functioning fingers on his fretting hand' is the standard simplification — evidence says fourth and fifth fingers of the left hand were badly burned, he played primarily with index and middle, and used the two injured fingers 'only for chord work' (so they retained partial function).

artist:don-cherry.notes: Stored URL 'Don Cherry (musician)' resolved to 'Don Cherry (trumpeter)' — same entity (the jazz trumpeter, not the hockey commentator), link fine. Confirmed: dates 1936/1995 (November 18, 1936 – October 19, 1995), long tenure in Ornette Coleman's bands, world-music direction ('pioneer in world fusion'), piano (acted as pianist in Art Farmer's group). 'Pocket trumpet' specifically and 'percussion' in the instruments field are not named in the extract — only generically supported by 'trumpeter' and 'multi-instrumentalist'; no contradiction.

artist:don-ellis.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Dates 1934/1978 confirmed (July 25, 1934 – December 17, 1978), trumpeter/drummer/composer/bandleader, time-signature experimentation, The French Connection (1971) and The Seven-Ups (1973) scores all confirmed.

artist:donald-byrd.notes: Right entity, no contradictions, almost everything confirmed: dates 1932/2013 (December 9, 1932 – February 4, 2013), hard bop to funk/soul transition, Herbie Hancock's Blue Note debut on Byrd's Royal Flush plus the mentor relationship, Black Byrd / Places and Spaces with the Mizell Brothers, commercial success ('Blue Note's best-selling album'), Howard University teaching and law degree (J.D. 1976), mentoring (the Blackbyrds). Unverifiable: 'Wayne Shorter' as a future star featured on Byrd's Blue Note sessions — Shorter appears nowhere in the evidence, and background knowledge does not recall Shorter as a Byrd sideman (not citable; flag for source check, possible personnel error). Also '1990s hip-hop sampling' vs evidence 'samples for acid jazz artists such as Us3' — close, not contradicted.

artist:donald-harrison.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Born June 23, 1960 (birthYear 1960 ok); living per evidence (2026 Grammy nomination mentioned). Saxophonist and Big Chief of The Congo Square Nation Afro-New Orleans Cultural Group confirmed word for word.

artist:dorothy-ashby.notes: Dates 1932/1986 confirmed (August 6, 1932 – April 13, 1986), harp confirmed. 'Proved the harp could swing / pioneering jazz harp in the 1950s and 1960s' matches 'established the harp as an improvising jazz instrument... proving the harp could play bebop' and the 1957-1970 session run. Soul/Afrocentric range supported ('not just bop, but soul, Brazilian, African, Middle Eastern'); sampling by crate-digging producers confirmed (Pete Rock, Rahzel, Ugly Duckling; continued high-profile sampling).

artist:dudu-pukwana.notes: Dates 1938/1990 confirmed (18 July 1938 – 30 June 1990), alto saxophone confirmed (switched in 1956). Blue Notes membership, exile/emigration with the band in 1964 and settling in London (he died in London), township-rhythm-meets-free-improvisation groups (Spear's In the Townships; free sessions with Mengelberg/Bennink and John Stevens), and British-scene presence (Brotherhood of Breath, Ronnie Scott's) all confirmed.

artist:duke-ellington.notes: Skeleton comprehensively confirmed: dates 1899/1974 (April 29, 1899 – May 24, 1974), led his orchestra 1924 to death (~50 years), 1000+ compositions ('wrote or collaborated on more than one thousand compositions'), mastery of the three-minute 78-rpm miniature, multiple extended suites, Cotton Club fame, 'America's most important composer' reputation, posthumous Pulitzer. Unchecked illustrative details (extract truncates before naming them): 'Mood Indigo', 'Black, Brown and Beige', and writing for sidemen Johnny Hodges / Harry Carney / Cootie Williams — the extract names Tizol, Strayhorn, Blanton, Webster instead. No contradictions; the general claims these examples illustrate (miniatures, suites, orchestra-as-instrument) are confirmed.

artist:eddie-lang.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Dates 1902/1933 confirmed (October 25, 1902 – March 26, 1933). Father-of-jazz-guitar credit, duets with Lonnie Johnson / Carl Kress / Joe Venuti, Paul Whiteman Orchestra rhythm guitar, favoured Bing Crosby accompanist all confirmed word for word.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Eduardo Palmieri (December 15, 1936 – August 6, 2025) was an musician, bandleader, and composer of Puerto Rican heritage.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: A ten-time Grammy winner
wrongClaims.problem: Evidence states nine Grammy Awards total, not ten.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Palmieri has won a total of 9 Grammy Awards during his career, most recently for his 2006 album Simpático.

artist:eddie-palmieri.notes: Right entity throughout. Stale living-status: ours has deathYear null and present-tense bio ('is a pianist'); evidence records death on August 6, 2025 — primary repair is deathYear 2025 plus past-tensing the bio. Grammy count: ours 'ten-time' vs evidence '9 Grammy Awards' (counts elsewhere vary depending on Latin Grammy inclusion, but the pack evidence says 9). Confirmed: birthYear 1936 (December 15, 1936), Monk and McCoy Tyner influences ('Influenced by Thelonious Monk and McCoy Tyner'; Tyner 'a mentor and inspiration'), Latin jazz/salsa stature. 'Harlem River Drive' appears in evidence as a band he founded (1970); ours calls it an album — the eponymous album is not covered by the extract (not contradicted).

artist:egberto-gismonti.notes: Born 5 December 1947 (birthYear 1947 ok, living per evidence). Brazilian composer/guitarist/pianist confirmed; classical training (Boulanger/Barraqué) plus Brazilian traditions confirmed; extensive ECM catalog confirmed; Amazon evocation directly supported ('sojourn in the Xingu region of the Amazon basin... documented musically').

artist:ella-fitzgerald.notes: Right entity; bio is the Wikipedia lede nearly verbatim EXCEPT ours inserts 'absolute pitch' into the attribute list — the current extract reads 'purity of tone, impeccable diction, phrasing, timing, intonation, and a horn-like improvisational ability' with no absolute pitch. Absence is not contradiction, so the claim is unverifiable (likely scraped from an older revision). Dates 1917/1996 (April 25, 1917 – June 15, 1996) and the sobriquets First Lady of Song / Queen of Jazz / Lady Ella confirmed. Data-quality flag for repair: instruments field is ["composer"] only — composer is supported ('singer, songwriter and composer') but vocals, her primary instrument, is missing.

artist:elvin-jones.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede: Coltrane quartet late 1960 to late 1965, My Favorite Things / A Love Supreme / Ascension / Live at Birdland, post-1966 trio and Jazz Machine, brothers Hank and Thad — all confirmed. Dates 1927/2004 confirmed (September 9, 1927 – May 18, 2004). Data-quality flag for repair: instruments field is ["musician"] — not contradicted but malformed; evidence says 'American jazz drummer of the post-bop era', should be ["drums"].

artist:enrico-pieranunzi.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia opening (born 5 December 1949, Italian jazz pianist, classical-jazz blend, parents Renata Brillantini and Alvaro Pieranunzi, father a jazz guitarist). birthYear 1949 ok, living per evidence.

artist:enrico-rava.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede (Italian jazz trumpeter; started on trombone, switched to trumpet after hearing Miles Davis). Born 20 August 1939 (birthYear 1939 ok, living per evidence).

artist:eric-dolphy.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: dates 1928/1964 (June 20, 1928 – June 29, 1964; death at 36 consistent with those dates), alto sax / bass clarinet / flute, wide intervals and register leaps ('dramatic leaps from the lower to the upper register'), bass clarinet establishment in jazz, deep Mingus and Coltrane partnerships, initial critical hostility ('anti-jazz' branding). Unverifiable (extract truncates mid Booker Little section): 'Out to Lunch!' and its stature; 'worked extensively with... Ornette Coleman'; death in Berlin from undiagnosed diabetes. Background knowledge is consistent with all three (not citable); note 'extensively' may overstate the Ornette association even so — flag for source check.

artist:erroll-garner.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede, including 'first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra' and Play Misty for Me (1971). Dates 1921/1977 confirmed (June 15, 1921 – January 2, 1977). Caution for any future rewrite: later in the same extract a truncated sentence begins 'In 1954, Garner composed "Misty", first recordin...' — the source may internally place the first recording in 1954; ours matches the lede verbatim so no contradiction is chargeable here, but verify the Misty recording date against the full article before editing that sentence.

artist:esperanza-spalding.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: born October 18, 1984, bassist/singer/composer, Berklee instructor beginning at age 20, Best New Artist Grammy as her first Grammy (evidence ties it to Chamber Music Society, 2010), Emily's D+Evolution as a funk-rock concept project (alter-ego framing consistent with her middle name Emily). Unverifiable: the '2011... upset Justin Bieber' framing (neither the year nor Bieber appears in the extract) and the entire 'Songwrights Apothecary Lab' claim including the music-therapy-principles angle (extract coverage ends around 2019's 12 Little Spells / Harvard). Background knowledge is consistent with both (not citable).

artist:evan-parker.notes: Birth year 1944, British, soprano+tenor saxophone, unaccompanied soprano solos using circular breathing and multiphonics, and five-plus decades of prolific collaboration all confirmed by the extract. 'Founding figure of European free improvisation' is consistent with 'a pivotal figure in the development of European free jazz and free improvisation'. 'Dense, swirling galaxies of sound' is editorial opinion, not flagged.

artist:fats-waller.notes: Bio is near-verbatim of the Wikipedia lead. 1904-1943, pianist/organist/composer/singer, international touring, and Grammy Hall of Fame inductions for 'Ain't Misbehavin'' (1984) and 'Honeysuckle Rose' (1999) all confirmed. 'Laid much of the basis for modern jazz piano' is a slight editorial extension of 'widely influential among musicians'; the Harlem stride framing is supported (James P. Johnson, 'a leading figure of the burgeoning Harlem stride style', was his mentor).

artist:fletcher-henderson.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead. 1897-1952, pianist/bandleader/arranger, big band and swing significance, Duke Ellington comparison, Dixieland-to-swing bridge, and the 'Smack' nickname all confirmed word-for-word.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: extraordinary four-octave range
wrongClaims.problem: Evidence states her vocal range is six octaves, not four
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Purim has a rare six-octave voice.

artist:flora-purim.notes: Entity is right: born 1942, Brazilian jazz-fusion singer, Return to Forever and Airto Moreira work, and 'Butterfly Dreams' (1973 first US solo album) all confirmed. The octave count contradicts the evidence; published sources vary on this figure, so the repair should either follow the cited page (six) or drop the number. 'Open Your Eyes, You Can Fly' is not named in the extract (it only says she 'released a string of albums for the Milestone label' in the 1970s) - unverified, plausible.

artist:fred-hersch.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead (including the '17-time Grammy nominee' with the odd 'nominée' spelling, the Village Vanguard solo-week first, and 75+ original compositions). Born 1955 confirmed.

artist:freddie-hubbard.notes: Confirmed: 1938-2008, trumpet, hard bop; sideman on Out to Lunch!, Maiden Voyage, and Ascension; own album Ready for Freddie (Blue Note, 1961); 1970s commercial success with Red Clay (1970) plus the critics-vs-commercialism debate; serious lip injury in 1992 that limited his later playing. Unverifiable: 'Hub-Tones' (not in extract); the grouping of Coltrane's 'Ascension' under 'Blue Note classics' (the extract never states Ascension's label; background knowledge says it was Impulse!, so the repair-writer should re-source this likely label slip - not counted as an error since the evidence does not show it); claimed influence on Woody Shaw and Roy Hargrove (evidence shows Shaw only as a 1985-87 co-leader peer, and never mentions Hargrove).

artist:gary-bartz.notes: Confirmed: born 1940; joined Miles Davis's band mid-1970 (Isle of Wight, Cellar Door dates released on Live-Evil); formed Ntu Troop 'which combined jazz, funk, and soul' (the Afrocentric/African-American-consciousness framing is editorial). Unverifiable: 'His 1970s Prestige albums have been widely sampled by hip-hop producers' (no Prestige releases or sampling mentioned in the extract); 'active and vital performer well into his 80s' (latest dated activity in the extract is 2019, before he turned 80). Instruments field says alto saxophone; evidence says only 'saxophonist' - not contradicted.

artist:geri-allen.notes: Confirmed: 1957-2017, pianist; work with Ornette Coleman (two 1996 Sound Museum albums); own groups (Printmakers trio debut 1984); 'premature death' consistent with dying of cancer two weeks after her 60th birthday. Unverifiable: 'her work with Betty Carter' - Betty Carter appears nowhere in the extract. 'Leading jazz pianist of her generation' and the Cecil Taylor/Herbie Hancock synthesis line are editorial opinion, not flagged.

artist:gerry-mulligan.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead: 'Jeru' nickname, baritone saxophone with light airy tone in the cool jazz era, arranger for Claude Thornhill, Miles Davis, and Stan Kenton. 1927-1996 confirmed.

artist:gil-evans.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead: Canadian-American, one of jazz's greatest orchestrators, role in cool/modal/free/fusion, best known for the Miles Davis collaborations. 1912-1988 confirmed.

artist:gonzalo-rubalcaba.notes: Bio is verbatim Wikipedia text: born May 27, 1963, Havana; birth name Gonzalo Julio Gonzalez Fonseca; great-grandmother's surname adopted professionally as by father Guillermo and grandfather Jacobo; 1983 France/Africa tour with Orquesta Aragon. All confirmed.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Moncur died from cardiac arrest on June 3, 2022, his 85th birthday, at his home in Newark, New Jersey.

artist:grachan-moncur.notes: Entity is right: born 1937, trombonist; Blue Note composing confirmed (contributed the bulk of compositions to Jackie McLean's One Step Beyond and Destination... Out!, plus own albums Evolution 1963 and Some Other Stuff 1964). The dataset lists him as living, but he died June 3, 2022 - deathYear must be set to 2022. 'Influenced a generation of avant-garde trombonists' is editorial/unverified.

artist:grant-green.notes: Confirmed: 1935-1979; single-note horn-like style ('playing single notes rather than chords', 'mimicked that of a saxophonist'); one of Blue Note's most recorded ('made more appearances on Blue Note albums as leader or sideman than anyone else' 1961-65); Idle Moments described as among his best; hip-hop sampling supported ('one of the most sampled guitarists'); death at 43 from a heart attack confirmed (born June 1935, died January 31, 1979). 'Warm, slightly distorted tone' is editorial description (evidence quotes a 'punchy, biting tone' - different wording, not a contradiction).

artist:greg-osby.notes: Bio is verbatim Wikipedia text: born August 3, 1960, St. Louis; Howard then Berklee; New York 1982; played with Jaki Byard, Jim Hall, Muhal Richard Abrams, Andrew Hill, Jack DeJohnette, Dizzy Gillespie, Herbie Hancock; joined DeJohnette's Special Edition in 1985. All confirmed.

artist:hampton-hawes.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead: 1928-1977, American jazz pianist, author of Raise Up Off Me, which won the Deems Taylor Award for music writing in 1975 (article body calls it the ASCAP Deems Taylor Award). All confirmed.

artist:hank-mobley.notes: Confirmed: 1930-1986, tenor saxophone; Leonard Feather's 'middleweight champion of the tenor saxophone' nickname; Jazz Messengers membership (founding era and 1959 return); Miles Davis membership in 1961. Unverifiable: 'Blue Note Records' most prolific artist' and 'over 25 albums as leader' - the extract gives no count or ranking. Two cautions for the repair-writer: (1) ours glosses the middleweight metaphor as between Coltrane's hardness and Stan Getz's coolness, while the evidence glosses it as 'neither as aggressive as John Coltrane nor as mellow as Lester Young' - compatible descriptions, not a strict contradiction, but worth aligning with the cited version; (2) 'essential member of... the Miles Davis Quintet' overstates relative to the evidence's 'Mobley was briefly a member of the Miles Davis band in 1961', though 'essential' is editorial.

artist:henry-threadgill.notes: Confirmed: born 1944; saxophonist/flautist/composer; 2016 Pulitzer Prize for Music (In for a Penny, In for a Pound); AACM origin (original member of the Experimental Band, the AACM precursor, under Muhal Richard Abrams); groups Air, the Sextett, Very Very Circus, and Zooid all listed in evidence. The stylistic-blend sentence (New Orleans marching bands, free jazz, funk, contemporary classical) is editorial characterization; his marching-band background is in the evidence.

artist:herbie-hancock.notes: Confirmed: born 1940; joined Davis's Second Great Quintet May 1963 and left summer 1968 (matches '1963-68'); helped redefine the jazz rhythm section; dozens of Blue Note sessions under his own name; Head Hunters as his best-known, most influential album; 'Rockit' as a 1980s hit single. Unverifiable: Head Hunters 'became one of the first jazz albums to go platinum' (no chart/certification data in extract); 'Empyrean Isles' (extract names Takin' Off, My Point of View, Speak Like a Child, The Prisoner, Inventions and Dimensions, but not Empyrean Isles or the Maiden Voyage album as such); 'Rockit' dated 1983 and the MTV/hip-hop-scratching framing; 'A Buddhist practitioner'. No contradictions found.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Pascoal died from multiple organ failure in Rio de Janeiro, on 13 September 2025, at the age of 89.

artist:hermeto-pascoal.notes: Entity is right: born 22 June 1936, Brazilian multi-instrumentalist; 'o Bruxo' nickname confirmed (evidence translates it 'the Sorcerer'; ours' 'The Wizard' is an acceptable alternative rendering, not an error); music with teapots and unconventional objects/animals confirmed; baiao/forro roots supported (Luiz Gonzaga idol, Latin Grammy for a forro album). He died 13 September 2025 - deathYear must be set and the bio's present tense ('is... He plays') updated; the death likely postdates dataset creation. 'Slaves Mass' is not named in the extract - unverified.

artist:horace-silver.notes: Confirmed: 1928-2014; hard bop pioneer with gospel/blues/R&B elements; co-founded the Jazz Messengers with Art Blakey; 'Song for My Father' (his most successful album); 'Sister Sadie' named among his songs that became standards; later spirituality turn (United States of Mind series). Unverifiable: 'Nica's Dream' (not in the extract); the specific proteges 'Blue Mitchell, Junior Cook, and Joe Henderson' (evidence confirms he developed young talents over four decades but names none of these three). No contradictions.

artist:hugh-masekela.notes: Confirmed: 1939-2018; trumpeter/flugelhornist; 'the father of South African jazz' supports 'brought South African jazz to the world'; 'Grazing in the Grass' was a number-one US pop hit in 1968; exile of roughly 30 years confirmed (left after the 1960 Sharpeville massacre, returned to South Africa in the early 1990s). 'Continued performing until his death' is a generic closing claim the truncated extract does not directly confirm - low stakes, noted for completeness.

artist:ibrahim-maalouf.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead: French-Lebanese trumpeter/producer/arranger/composer; in 2022 the first Lebanese instrumentalist nominated at the Grammy Awards, for Queen of Sheba with Angelique Kidjo. Born 5 November 1980 confirmed.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: founded by pianist Misha Mengelberg and drummer Han Bennink
wrongClaims.problem: Ours equates the orchestra with the Instant Composers Pool (parenthetical plus birthYear 1967), but the 1967 founding had three founders - saxophonist Willem Breuker is omitted
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: In 1967 saxophonist Willem Breuker, pianist Misha Mengelberg and drummer Han Bennink founded the ICP label in Amsterdam.

artist:icp-orchestra.notes: Entity match is good: the stored URL (Instant_Composers_Pool) covers both the label and the orchestra; Dutch improvised-music ensemble and the 1967 founding year are confirmed. Nuance for the repair-writer: after Breuker left in 1974, Mengelberg and Bennink carried the ICP Tentet/Orchestra ('Mengelberg and Bennink intensified their collaboration and, widening their own musical project as the Instant Composers Pool Tentet'), so 'founded by Mengelberg and Bennink' is defensible for the orchestra as it became known - but paired with birthYear 1967 the founding claim should include Breuker or be rephrased (e.g. 'grew out of the Instant Composers Pool, founded 1967'). Dadaist humor / performances-as-theater is editorial (loosely supported by Mengelberg's Fluxus involvement and 'musical games'). The instruments list matches members named in evidence (piano Mengelberg, drums Bennink, trombone Wierbos, violin Oliver, saxophone Delius).

artist:j-dilla.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead: James Dewitt Yancey, J Dilla / Jay Dee, Detroit underground scene, Slum Village, founding Soulquarians member, Jaylib with Madlib (Champion Sound), Donuts released three days before his death, produced the Pharcyde's Labcabincalifornia. 1974-2006 confirmed. Note for reviewers: he is a hip-hop producer, not a jazz musician, but that is a curatorial inclusion choice, not a data error.

artist:j-j-johnson.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead: born James Louis Johnson, also known as Jay Jay Johnson, American jazz trombonist, composer, and arranger. 1924-2001 confirmed.

artist:jackie-mclean.notes: Page is unambiguously our Jackie McLean (1931-2006, alto saxophonist). birthYear/deathYear/instrument all match. Bio claims verified: distinctive tone ('bittersweet', 'piercing', 'searing'), bebop/hard-bop start (tutored by Monk, Powell, Parker; early recordings 'in the hard bop school'), later embrace of the avant-garde (Let Freedom Ring, 1962, began his avant-garde period). 'Distinctive cry' and 'emotional directness' are editorial.

artist:jaco-pastorius.notes: Dates match (Dec 1, 1951 - Sep 21, 1987). Bio claims verified: fretless bass, harmonics, lyrical solos ('fretless bass, lyrical solos, bass chords and innovative use of harmonics'), Weather Report membership 1976-1981, solo albums, early death at 35 from injuries after a beating. 'Revolutionized', 'changed music forever', 'unique genius' are editorial; evidence supports the stature claims ('widely regarded as one of the greatest and most influential bassists of all time').

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Willie James "Blood" Ulmer (February 8, 1940 – June 3, 2026) was an American jazz, free funk and blues guitarist and singer.
Ulmer died on June 3, 2026, at the age of 86.

artist:james-blood-ulmer.notes: Right entity. Two field errors: birth year is 1940 per evidence, not 1942; and the page records his death on June 3, 2026 (ten days before today), so deathYear null and the present-tense bio ('is a guitarist and vocalist') are outdated -- the dataset almost certainly predates the death, but it now needs updating. Bio content otherwise verified: Ornette Coleman association and harmolodic link (first electric guitarist to record/tour extensively with Coleman; album 'Harmolodic Guitar with Strings'), and both named albums are in the discography: Tales of Captain Black (Artists House, 1979), Are You Glad to Be in America? (Rough Trade, 1980).

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: He has been prominent as a performer and recording artist on the jazz scene since the late 1980s, focusing on saxophones, flute and clarinets.

artist:james-carter.notes: Right entity; bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified (born Jan 3, 1969; virtuosity on saxophones and woodwinds; cousin of Regina Carter). The instruments field is corrupted: a single token 'bass clarinetsaxophonesflutes' with lost delimiters. This is a formatting/data-corruption defect, not a wrong-entity or wrong-fact problem -- the intended instruments (bass clarinet, saxophones, flutes) are consistent with the evidence ('saxophones, flute and clarinets'; bass clarinet specifically is not named but 'clarinets' plural is compatible). Repair: split into a proper array.

artist:james-francies.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; every claim verified: born 1995, Houston upbringing, move to New York (New School), debut as leader on Blue Note in 2018 (Flight). Instruments piano/keyboards match 'jazz pianist, keyboardist'.

artist:james-jamerson.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: dates (Jan 29, 1936 - Aug 2, 1983), uncredited Motown bassist of the 1960s/early 1970s, Rock and Roll Hall of Fame 2000, 23 Billboard Hot 100 number-ones and 56 R&B number-ones as a session musician. Note for editors: he is a Motown session bassist with jazz roots rather than a jazz artist per se, but that is a curation choice, not a factual error.

artist:james-p-johnson.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: dates (Feb 1, 1894 - Nov 17, 1955), stride piano pioneer, key figure in ragtime-to-jazz evolution alongside Jelly Roll Morton, influence on Basie/Ellington/Tatum/Monk, and Fats Waller as his student.

artist:jan-garbarek.notes: Right entity; born 4 March 1947, Norwegian saxophonist, no death. Bio claims verified: free-jazz background ('By 1973 he had turned his back on the harsh dissonances of avant-garde jazz'), Gregorian-chant connection (Officium with the Hilliard Ensemble, 'one of ECM's biggest-selling albums of all time'), strong ECM association. Tone descriptions are editorial but echo the evidence ('sharp-edged tone, long, keening, sustained notes'). One gap: the extract says only 'saxophonist' -- it never names tenor or soprano specifically, so the species-level instruments field is confirmed at genus level only.

artist:jason-moran.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead sentence; verified: born January 21, 1975, jazz pianist, composer, educator, multimedia/theatrical installation work (IN MY MIND, museum commissions).

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: The group was formed in the mid-1950s when Brand and Mackay Davashe began working together initiated a collaboration with Gwangwa and Masekela.

artist:jazz-epistles.notes: Right entity. Low-confidence flag: if birthYear means formation year, the evidence says 'formed in the mid-1950s', contradicting 1959 -- but that evidence sentence is itself garbled and may describe a precursor collaboration; 1959 is the year they recorded Jazz Epistle, Verse 1, and the page's '56-year hiatus' before the 2016 reunion implies activity ended 1960. Human should decide the intended semantics. Not flagged: 'recorded only one album' (consistent: recorded the album 'just before breaking up'); 'Sharpeville massacre ended their brief existence' is a simplification -- evidence has them breaking up around the 1959 recording, yet also ties the members' dispersal to post-Sharpeville exile; 'South Africa's first bebop group' vs evidence 'first important (albeit short-lived) bebop band' is a minor wording gap. Featuring Abdullah Ibrahim (Dollar Brand) and Hugh Masekela verified. Instruments list omits Jonas Gwangwa's trombone (omission, not contradiction).

artist:jeanne-lee.notes: Right entity; dates match (Jan 29, 1939 - Oct 25, 2000). 'The Newest Sound Around' with Ran Blake verified as her first record, released 1962 (RCA Victor; recorded 1961). 'Poet' verified ('jazz singer, poet and composer'); 'dancer' supported (studied dance at Bard, created choreography, her ensemble fused poetry, music and dance). Collaborations with free improvisers across Europe and America verified (Gunter Hampel, Carla Bley, Braxton, Shepp, Waldron). 'Landmark', 'expanded the boundaries', 'influencing generations' are editorial.

artist:jelly-roll-morton.notes: Right entity. Bio verified: 'jazz's first great arranger' matches 'Morton was jazz's first arranger, proving that a genre rooted in improvisation could retain its essential characteristics when notated'; Red Hot Peppers recordings for Victor from 1926 (late 1920s) verified. Caution on birthYear: the evidence is internally inconsistent -- the lead says 'c. September 20, 1890' (matches ours), but the shortDescription says '(1885-1941)' and the biography text says born 'around 1885' (he claimed 1884). Ours matches the page's primary lead date, so not flagged as an error, but a reviewer may want 'c. 1890'. deathYear 1941 verified.

artist:jerry-jemmott.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: Gerald Stenhouse Jemmott, born March 22, 1946, bass guitarist, chief session bassist of late 1960s/early 1970s soul, blues, and jazz dates, two Grammy Awards.

artist:jim-hall.notes: Right entity; dates match (Dec 4, 1930 - Dec 10, 2013). Verified: Bill Evans partnership ('formed a studio partnership with Bill Evans... appearing on five albums with Evans from 1962 to 1966' -- 'duo recordings' specifically is a fair gloss, supported by the partnership framing), Sonny Rollins (1961-62, 1964), Art Farmer (1962-64), Paul Desmond (six albums as featured soloist; Concierto). 'Influenced virtually every jazz guitarist who followed' and 'subtle, restrained' are editorial.

artist:jimmy-smith.notes: Right entity; dates match (Dec 8, 1928 - Feb 8, 2005). Verified: Hammond B-3 popularizer, bebop-plus-blues style ('combined bluesy licks with bebop-based single note runs'), prolific Blue Note run from 1956 (about 40 sessions in eight years, so late 1950s and 1960s holds), influence on a generation of organists (McGriff, McDuff, Holmes, DeFrancesco...). Caution for repair-writer: 'single-handedly brought the Hammond B-3 organ into jazz' is editorial hyperbole -- evidence notes the organ was already used in jazz by Fats Waller, Count Basie, and Wild Bill Davis, and that Smith 'helped to popularize' it. Treated as opinion, not a factual error, but the wording could be softened.

artist:jiro-inagaki.notes: The stored Wikipedia URL returned no page (page: null), so nothing could be entity-matched or fact-checked: birthYear 1933, deathYear 2016, saxophone, Soul Media, the 1970s jazz-funk framing, and the 'Funky Stuff' album are all unverified. Judge's background knowledge (not citable) raises doubt about deathYear 2016 -- Inagaki may have remained active after that date -- so prioritize finding a working source for the death year specifically. The wikiLinkVerdict is unsure because we cannot see what the stored URL resolves to.

artist:joe-harriott.notes: Right entity; no contradictions. Verified: dates (15 July 1928 - 2 January 1973), Kingston, Jamaica birth, alto saxophone, free-form pioneer in Britain, albums Free Form (Jazzland, 1960) and Abstract (Columbia UK, 1962), and the Ornette Coleman comparison ('often compared to Ornette Coleman's roughly contemporary breakthrough in the United States'). Unverifiable substantive claims: (1) 'the two were unaware of each other's work' -- the evidence neither supports nor denies mutual unawareness, and it is a strong claim; (2) 'Indo-Jazz Suites with violinist John Mayer' -- the extract truncates before any mention of John Mayer or the Indo-Jazz works; the lead's 'global music fusion' supports the direction only. 'Preceded many American free jazz recordings' and 'far less recognition' are loose/editorial (the latter is broadly supported by the noted hostility of the British jazz establishment).

artist:joe-henderson.notes: Right entity; no contradictions. Verified: dates (April 24, 1937 - June 30, 2001), tenor saxophone, 1960s Blue Note era as leader and sideman (nearly 30 albums, 1963-1968), Kenny Dorham association, Horace Silver ('Song for My Father'), Andrew Hill (Black Fire, Point of Departure), and the 1991 Verve signing. Unverifiable substantive claims: the Grammys for his tributes to Billy Strayhorn and Miles Davis -- the extract truncates right after the Verve signing and never reaches the 1990s albums or awards. 'Smoky, distinctive sound' and 'inexorable logic' are editorial.

artist:joe-lovano.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified: born December 29, 1952, multi-instrumentalist best known on tenor, recorded on alto clarinet, flute and drums, Grammy Award, Down Beat poll mentions, wife Judi Silvano, longtime Paul Motian trio with Bill Frisell. One gap: 'tarogato' in the instruments field is not named in the extract -- the lead's 'amongst other instruments' leaves it consistent but unconfirmed.

artist:joe-pass.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: dates (January 13, 1929 - May 23, 1994), recorded and performed with Oscar Peterson, Duke Ellington, and Ella Fitzgerald (six Pablo albums with Fitzgerald), solo guitar renown via Virtuoso (Pablo, 1974).

artist:joel-ross.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified: born 1995, Chicago-raised vibraphonist living in Brooklyn, started on drums at his father's church, high school (Chicago High School for the Arts) and college (New School) education, signed with Blue Note (KingMaker, 2019). This was a known past collision target (Diana Ross facts), but the current content is about the correct Joel Ross -- no trace of the old contamination.

artist:joey-calderazzo.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: born February 27, 1965, pianist, brother of Gene Calderazzo, extensive work in Michael Brecker's quintet (from 1987) and the Branford Marsalis Quartet (succeeding Kenny Kirkland in 1998), own bands as leader.

artist:john-coltrane.notes: Right entity; no contradictions, but the extract truncates around 1957, leaving several substantive claims uncheckable: 'Giant Steps' and 'My Favorite Things' as albums, the 'sheets of sound' coinage, the classic quartet personnel (McCoy Tyner, Jimmy Garrison, Elvin Jones), liver cancer as cause of death, and soprano saxophone (only tenor is explicit: 'switched to playing tenor saxophone in 1947'). Verified: dates (Sept 23, 1926 - July 17, 1967; age 40 at death is consistent with the dates), hard bop start and the Miles Davis / Thelonious Monk association ('1955-1957: Miles and Monk period'), modal pioneering, forefront of free jazz, spiritual dimension, and A Love Supreme as his most acclaimed album. The unverified claims are standard Coltrane biography, but the pack evidence cannot confirm them.

artist:john-mclaughlin.notes: Right entity; no contradictions. Verified: born 4 January 1942, guitarist, played on Miles Davis's In a Silent Way and Bitches Brew before forming the Mahavishnu Orchestra ('His 1970s electric band'), fusion of jazz/rock with Indian and classical influences, Shakti founded 1974 as a seminal Indian-jazz fusion band. Unverifiable substantive claims: Mahavishnu formed specifically 'in 1971' (evidence gives no formation year), 'devoted practitioner of Sri Chinmoy's teachings' (Sri Chinmoy is never mentioned in the extract; the 'Mahavishnu' alias is consistent but not probative), and influence 'from Al Di Meola to progressive metal players' (Di Meola absent from the extract). 'Spiritually charged', 'lightning-fast' are editorial; his spiritual orientation generally is supported.

artist:john-patitucci.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead plus opening biography sentences; all claims verified: born December 22, 1959, Brooklyn, electric bass at 10, performing/composing at 12, acoustic bass at 15, piano by 16, R&B radio and grandfather's jazz records. Instruments double bass / bass guitar verified ('Patitucci switches between double bass and electric bass').

artist:john-taylor.notes: Stored URL resolves to 'John Taylor (jazz)', British jazz pianist (1942-2015) -- our entity. All checkable claims confirmed: birth 1942, death 2015, piano, British; trio Azimuth (formed 1977 with Norma Winstone and Kenny Wheeler, several ECM recordings); extensive Kenny Wheeler collaboration. 'Central to the ECM aesthetic' and 'finest European jazz' are editorial, consistent with the ECM work in evidence.

artist:john-zorn.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead (genre list, 'deliberately resists category' and Rolling Stone quotes all match exactly). Born September 2, 1953 confirmed; deathYear null consistent with '(born 1953)' short description; 'composer' confirmed.

artist:johnny-hodges.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully confirmed: b. July 25 1907, d. May 11 1970, alto sax with Ellington, refused soprano after 1940, Benny Carter comparison. Sole unchecked item: instruments field lists 'clarinet', which the evidence never attributes to Hodges (his early instruments were drums and piano, then soprano sax). Unsupported, not contradicted -- absence is not contradiction. Repair-writer should source or drop the clarinet entry; everything else is clean.

artist:jon-batiste.notes: Bio is near-verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: b. 1986 (Nov 11) confirmed; collaborator list is an exact subset of the wiki list (wiki adds Doja Cat); Stay Human as Late Show house band 2015 to 2022 confirmed verbatim. Instruments 'composer' (Soul score, film scores, symphony) and 'bandleader' confirmed. Minor: ours says 'singer...composer' where the wiki lead says 'musician...record producer'; 'singer' is not explicitly stated in the extract but is a profession descriptor, not contradicted.

artist:jon-faddis.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: b. July 24 1953; trumpet player, conductor, composer, educator; Dizzy Gillespie mentorship 'along with pianist Stan Kenton and trumpeter Bill Catalano' matches the wiki text exactly. Instruments [trumpet, composer] confirmed.

artist:julian-lage.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead. Confirmed: b. December 25 1987; 2000 Grammy Awards performance at 12; Stanford Jazz Workshop faculty at 15; debut Sounding Point (EmArcy, 2009); Blue Note signing 2021; all five listed Blue Note releases incl. Scenes from Above (2026) appear in both the lead and the discography. Guitar confirmed.

artist:julius-hemphill.notes: Confirmed: b. Jan 24 1938, d. April 2 1995; 'performed mainly on alto saxophone, less often on soprano and tenor saxophones and flute' supports alto/flute/composer; co-founded the Black Artists' Group in St. Louis (verbatim 'co-founded'); World Saxophone Quartet founding (wiki: 'founder of the World Saxophone Quartet, a group he formed in 1976' -- ours' 'co-founded' is compatible, not contradicted); Dogon A.D. in discography (Mbari, 1972). 'One of the great avant-garde jazz recordings', 'theatrical', 'uncompromising' are editorial.

artist:kamasi-washington.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: b. Feb 18 1981; raised in Los Angeles; sax on Kendrick Lamar's To Pimp a Butterfly and on Flying Lotus's You're Dead!; The Epic released May 2015 as his debut solo recording; West Coast Get Down founding member supports the 'community of like-minded players' framing. Unchecked substantive claims: The Epic being a 'triple album' (format not stated in evidence); 'jazz-musician father' (father never mentioned); 'tenor' saxophone specifically (evidence says only 'saxophonist'); Coltrane/Pharoah Sanders lineage is a stylistic claim not in evidence.

artist:keith-jarrett.notes: Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: b. May 8 1945; The Koln Concert (1975) 'best-selling piano recording in history' (ours' wider 'best-selling solo jazz and piano album' is not contradicted); the 1970s 'American quartet' (Haden/Motian/Redman) named in evidence; 2018 stroke ending performances; jazz+classical span.
