Unchecked substantive claims: 'Standards Trio with Gary Peacock and Jack DeJohnette' -- the extract truncates in the early 1970s and Gary Peacock never appears in it; 'demanding concert requirements and onstage vocalizations' not in evidence.

artist:ken-vandermark.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead (ours says 'saxophonist, and clarinetist' where wiki lead says 'reedist'; wiki then specifies 'plays tenor saxophone, clarinet, bass clarinet, and baritone saxophone', confirming both instruments). b. September 22 1964, Chicago scene since the 1990s, 1999 MacArthur Fellowship all confirmed.

artist:kenny-burrell.notes: Confirmed: b. July 31 1931 in Detroit; work on Prestige, Blue Note and other top labels; Midnight Blue (1963, with Stanley Turrentine) cited by critics as a highlight; Ellington devotion ('Ellingtonia' UCLA course, Ellington called him his favorite guitarist, recorded Ellington tributes); Director of Jazz Studies at UCLA since 1996. 'Quintessential Blue Note guitarist', 'one of the most recorded guitarists in jazz history' and 'hundreds of sessions' are editorial-scale characterizations consistent with the evidence's 'prolific recording career' and studio-musician demand.

artist:kenny-clarke.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: Kenneth Clarke Spearman, 'Klook', bebop drumming innovator, ride-cymbal timekeeping, bass-drum irregular accents. b. January 9 1914, d. January 26 1985, drums all confirmed.

artist:kenny-dorham.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead, including the Gary Giddins 'virtually synonymous with underrated' quote. b. August 30 1924, d. December 5 1972, trumpet confirmed.

artist:kenny-garrett.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully confirmed: b. October 9 1960; Duke Ellington Orchestra (under Mercer Ellington, 1978); Miles Davis band; solo career since 1985; 'primary instruments are alto and soprano saxophone and flute'. Sole unchecked item: instruments field also lists 'piano', which the evidence never attributes to Garrett. Unsupported, not contradicted. Repair-writer should source or drop the piano entry; everything else is clean.

artist:kenny-wheeler.notes: Confirmed: b. January 14 1930 (Toronto), d. September 18 2014; trumpet and flugelhorn; 'Canadian-British' consistent with 'Canadian... based in the U.K. from the 1950s onwards'. ECM albums with Keith Jarrett (Gnu High, 1975 -- 'one of the few albums to feature Keith Jarrett as a sideman') and with Dave Holland (Angel Song, ECM 1997, with Holland on bass) both confirmed. 'Masterpieces' and 'European chamber jazz aesthetic' are editorial; the chamber-jazz framing is supported by the Azimuth description.

artist:king-oliver.notes: Confirmed: cornet player and bandleader; pioneering use of mutes; 'the mentor and teacher of Louis Armstrong' (ours' 'most important mentor' superlative is editorial but directly supported by Armstrong's own quote); Creole Jazz Band's 1923 recordings for Gennett/Okeh/Paramount/Columbia. Dates 1881-1938 match the short description; wiki notes 1881 is self-claimed and debated (some sources say 1884/85) -- our 1881 follows the page's headline dates. Nuance: ours says 'from New Orleans'; evidence: born in Aben, Louisiana (near Donaldsonville), 'moved to New Orleans in his youth' -- loose phrasing, not contradicted, but a precision-minded repair could say 'Louisiana-born, raised in New Orleans'.

artist:kurt-rosenwinkel.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: guitarist, composer, bandleader, producer, educator, keyboardist, record label owner. b. October 28 1970 confirmed. Instruments [guitar, piano] supported: piano lessons at nine and 'keyboardist' in the lead.

artist:lage-lund.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia article (it even reproduces the source's grammar slip 'founded a jazz trio and performing in local clubs'). Confirmed: b. December 12 1977; Norwegian, lives in New York; skateboarding ambition; guitar at 13; Berklee scholarship and Wally's Cafe performances. Guitar confirmed.

artist:larry-young.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: organist and occasional pianist, Jimmy Smith soul-jazz influence, later experimental/modal Hammond B-3 approach. b. October 7 1940, d. March 30 1978, organ confirmed. Stored URL is the disambiguated 'Larry Young (musician)' page about our organist.

artist:lars-jansson.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia article: Swedish jazz pianist and composer b. 1951; Orebro upbringing, boredom at community music school, relative's Miles Davis/Ben Webster/Mose Allison records, 1960s organ interest -- all confirmed word-for-word. Stored URL is the disambiguated 'Lars Jansson (composer)' page and it is about our pianist.

artist:lee-konitz.notes: One-sentence bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead. b. October 13 1927, d. April 15 2020, alto saxophone confirmed.

artist:lee-morgan.notes: Right entity, no provable contradictions. Confirmed: b. July 10 1938, d. February 19 1972; 'joined Dizzy Gillespie's big band at 18' (verbatim); The Sidewinder title track cracked the pop chart in 1964; Search for the New Land (1964); heroin problems (Blakey said to have introduced him); shot by Helen Moore at Slugs' Saloon and bled to death. Unchecked: 'recording his first Blue Note album at 19' -- evidence says 'Morgan began recording for Blue Note in 1956' (he turned 18 July 1956), which suggests 18, but the same page also says he 'started to record as leader in the late '50s' and never dates the first album, so the 19 claim is neither confirmable nor verbatim-contradicted; flag for repair review. Ours' 'common-law wife' vs evidence's 'live-in girlfriend' is compatible phrasing; 'murder' is consistent with her shooting him, his death, and her arrest/imprisonment.

artist:lennie-tristano.notes: One-sentence bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: pianist, composer, arranger, and teacher of jazz improvisation. b. March 19 1919, d. November 18 1978, piano confirmed.

artist:leon-thomas.notes: The stored URL (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Thomas_(musician)) returned page: null -- no evidence document survived, nothing checkable. All claims pending verification: 1937-1999 dates, vocals, Pygmy-inspired yodeling technique, work with Pharoah Sanders on 'Karma'. Needs a re-fetch of the page (the null may be a fetch failure or a moved/disambiguated title) before any verdict on content; the stored link itself also remains unverified.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: was an American jazz tenor saxophonist and occasional clarinetist

artist:lester-young.notes: Right entity; the bio itself is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully confirmed (b. Aug 27 1909, d. March 15 1959, 'Pres'/'Prez', tenor saxophonist and occasional clarinetist). The error is in the structured instruments field: it lists ONLY 'clarinet', omitting tenor saxophone -- the instrument the evidence (and our own bio text) identifies him by; short description is 'American jazz saxophonist (1909-1959)' and the lead calls him 'President of the tenor saxophone'. Clarinet is not false as a secondary instrument, but as the sole instruments entry it misidentifies his instrument to users. Looks like an extraction slip that kept 'occasional clarinetist' and dropped 'tenor saxophonist'. Repair: instruments should be ['tenor saxophone', 'clarinet'] per the evidence.

artist:lonnie-liston-smith.notes: Entity confirmed. birthYear 1940 (born December 28, 1940), piano/keyboards, formative work with Pharoah Sanders and Miles Davis, and forming the Cosmic Echoes all verify against the page lead. Unverifiable: the claim that his album 'Expansions' became a jazz-funk classic and a DJ/sampling favorite - the extract truncates around 1974, before any mention of Expansions (1975). No contradictions found; deathYear null is consistent with the page (born 1940, no death reported).

artist:lou-donaldson.notes: Entity confirmed; 1926-2024 (November 1, 1926 - November 9, 2024), alto saxophone, Charlie Parker influence in his formative years, and 'Alligator Bogaloo' dated 1967 (listed as a signature song) all verify. Unverifiable: 'became one of Blue Note's biggest sellers' - no label or sales information for that album in the extract (Blue Note appears only regarding the Art Blakey Birdland sessions he played on). 'Helping to create the soul jazz subgenre' treated as editorial framing of the evidenced 'soulful, bluesy approach'. No contradictions.

artist:louis-armstrong.notes: Entity confirmed; born New Orleans 1901, died 1971, trumpet/vocals, work with King Oliver and Fletcher Henderson, the shift from collective improvisation to solo performance, gravelly voice, beloved-entertainer status, and cross-genre influence all verify. Unverifiable: 'formed his legendary Hot Five and Hot Seven groups in the 1920s' (extract truncates before his recording career; universally documented but not citable from this evidence) and 'pioneered scat singing' (evidence says only 'skilled at scat singing'). No contradictions.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Louis Tebogo Moholo (10 March 1940 - 13 June 2025) was a South African jazz drummer.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Louis Moholo-Moholo is a South African drummer and percussionist
wrongClaims.problem: Present-tense bio for an artist the evidence shows died on 13 June 2025
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Moholo died after a lengthy illness in Cape Town, on 13 June 2025, at the age of 85.

artist:louis-moholo-moholo.notes: Everything else verifies: born 1940 in Langa, Cape Town; founding member of the Blue Notes with Chris McGregor, Dyani, Moyake, Feza, Pukwana; emigrated 1964 and settled in London 1965 ('fled apartheid' is fair framing of the exile-community context); Brotherhood of Breath member; played with Cecil Taylor. Stored URL title 'Louis_Moholo-Moholo' resolves to the 'Louis Moholo' page about the same person (he adopted the doubled surname after 2005). Repair: set deathYear 2025, move bio to past tense.

artist:lovie-austin.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead; birth/death years (September 19, 1887 - July 8, 1972) and piano (one of the best female jazz blues piano players; played piano in vaudeville) all match. No issues.

artist:mal-waldron.notes: All substantive claims verify: 1925-2002; Billie Holiday's regular accompanist from April 1957 until her death in July 1959 (supports 'last regular accompanist'); 1963 heroin-overdose breakdown that left him unable to play or remember music, with gradual recovery; permanent move to Europe in the mid-1960s; gravitation toward free jazz; style featuring repetition of notes and motifs. 'Near-fatal', 'anticipated minimalism', and 'influenced many European improvisers' are editorial embellishments, not contradicted by evidence.

artist:manu-dibango.notes: 1933-2020, Cameroonian, saxophone and vibraphone, fusion of jazz/funk/traditional Cameroonian music, Soul Makossa sampling fame, and the Michael Jackson 'Wanna Be Startin' Somethin'' borrowing all verify. 'One of the most sampled songs in history' is consistent with the evidence's 'most sampled African song' and 'most sampled African musician in history'. 'Influencing Afrobeat' is an uncited influence characterization (Fela Kuti appears in evidence only as a collaborator); treated as editorial, not a checkable claim.

artist:marc-cary.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead; born January 29, 1967; pianist; the full collaborator list (Gillespie, Carter, Hargrove, Taylor, Lincoln, Garnett, Badu, Ndegeocello, Harris, Hill, DiFranco, McLean, Q-Tip, McRae) matches exactly. No issues.

fieldIssues.evidence: bass (primary); also clarinet, keyboards, saxophone, guitar
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: He became classically trained as a clarinetist and later learned to play keyboards, saxophone, and guitar in addition to bass.

artist:marcus-miller.notes: Bio prose is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully verifies (worked with Miles Davis, Hancock, Vandross, Shorter, Sanborn; main songwriter/producer on Tutu 1986, Music from Siesta 1987, Amandla 1989; born June 14, 1959). The instruments field is wrong: evidence documents him as a bassist ('Miller has played bass on more than 500 recordings'; signature Fender Jazz Bass section) and never attributes trumpet to him. 'Trumpet' was likely mis-extracted from 'trumpeter Miles Davis' in the bio's first sentence. Repair: instruments should be bass (electric bass) plus optionally bass clarinet/keyboards.

artist:maria-schneider.notes: Entity confirmed; born November 27, 1960; 'composer, conductor' fits the evidenced 'composer and jazz orchestra leader'; Concert in the Garden as her first Grammy-winning album, produced via the fan-funded ArtistShare platform, verifies. Unverifiable: '18-piece orchestra' (no ensemble size in evidence), the phrasing 'first Grammy-winning album released exclusively via the internet' (evidence says first such award-winning album produced by ArtistShare; internet-only release is not stated), and 'Brazilian traditions' as an influence. No contradictions.

artist:marilyn-crispell.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead (Scott Yanow and Jon Pareles quotes, longtime member of Anthony Braxton's quartet in the 1980s-90s, confirmed in body as 1983-1995). Born March 30, 1947; piano. No issues.

artist:mario-bauza.notes: Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead, including the 'Tanga'/first-true-Afro-Cuban-jazz-tune claims. 1911-1993 (April 28, 1911 - July 11, 1993). All three instruments documented in the body: child-prodigy clarinetist, learned alto saxophone in 1930, took up trumpet for the Machin date and became Chick Webb's lead trumpeter. No issues.

artist:mark-turner.notes: Bio matches the Wikipedia lead and body nearly verbatim: born November 10, 1965, in Fairborn, raised in Palos Verdes Estates, intended to become a commercial artist, clarinet in elementary school then alto and tenor saxophones in high school. Ours adds the state glosses 'Ohio' and 'California', which the extract does not state (CSU Long Beach attendance is consistent with a California upbringing); trivial disambiguation, not contradicted. No issues.

artist:takuya-kuroda.notes: Born 1980, trumpet, based in Brooklyn/New York, Rising Son on Blue Note (2014, produced by close friend Jose James) all confirmed. 'Represents the modern face of Blue Note Records' is editorial (his catalog spans CD Baby, P-Vine, Blue Note, Concord, First Word, PPK per evidence); the hard bop/hip-hop/Afrobeat/electronic blend and 'bridge between Japanese and American jazz scenes' are editorial color not covered by the extract.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Taking over Art Blakey's trumpet chair alongside fellow New Orleans native Wynton Marsalis
wrongClaims.problem: Per the evidence Blanchard REPLACED Marsalis in the Jazz Messengers (Marsalis recommended him as his own replacement); they were not in the band together -- 'alongside' misstates the membership relationship (his Messengers partner was Donald Harrison)
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: In 1982, Wynton Marsalis recommended Blanchard as his replacement in Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers and Blakey would appoint Blanchard the band's musical director.

artist:terence-blanchard.notes: Right entity; likely sloppy phrasing for 'succeeding Wynton Marsalis' -- repair is a rewording. Everything else checks: born 1962, New Orleans, Spike Lee collaboration (per-Lee count 'over 20 film scores' not verifiable -- evidence gives 80+ total film/TV scores and 'most of Spike Lee's films'), A Tale of God's Will as Katrina requiem, Fire Shut Up in My Bones as first opera by a Black composer at the Metropolitan Opera opening the 2021-22 season, ongoing mentorship roles. 'composer' appears in the instruments array -- true fact, wrong field semantics; flag for data owner.

artist:terri-lyne-carrington.notes: Born 1965, drummer-composer-producer, worked with jazz greats (Gillespie, Getz, Hancock, Shorter and others), women-in-jazz projects (Berklee Institute of Jazz and Gender Justice; New Standards uplifting women composers) all confirmed. 'Endorsed by Berklee at 11' matches the evidence's 'At the age of 11, she received a full scholarship to Berklee College of Music' -- loose wording, same fact. 'Child prodigy' supported (first major performance at 10 with Clark Terry after starting drums at 7).

artist:terumasa-hino.notes: Born 1942, trumpet and flugelhorn (evidence also adds cornet), still living per evidence (deathYear null consistent). Miles Davis and Freddie Hubbard influence supported (he transcribed their solos as a teenager). 'Japan's most internationally recognized jazz trumpeter' is superlative color; evidence says 'considered one of Japan's finest jazz musicians'. 'Bridges hard bop, fusion, and spiritual jazz' is editorial (evidence describes moves toward funk, free jazz, avant-garde).

artist:blue-notes.notes: Right entity, prose fully consistent: mixed-race South African sextet under apartheid (McGregor the sole white member, police harassment), 1964 departure/exile, members Chris McGregor, Dudu Pukwana, Mongezi Feza, seminal influence on the London/British jazz scene, and the listed instruments (sax, trumpet, piano, bass, drums) all confirmed. The one uncheckable substantive field: birthYear 1960 as formation year -- the evidence gives no formation date (they 'came to prominence at the 1963 National Jazz Festival', initially based in Cape Town). Not contradicted; needs an outside source.

artist:jazz-messengers.notes: 'Founded in 1953 by drummer Art Blakey and pianist Horace Silver' matches one origin account the evidence itself cites ('Some cite the group that included Blakey, Silver, Kenny Dorham, Lou Donaldson and Gene Ramey in 1953 as the original Jazz Messengers'); evidence notes most historians date the name to 1954/55 -- contested historiography, not a contradiction. 'Under Blakey's leadership for over 35 years' confirmed ('existed for over thirty-five years... Blakey led or co-led the group from the outset'). All five named alumni (Shorter, Morgan, Hubbard, Wynton Marsalis, Keith Jarrett) appear in the evidence's alumni list; 'most important training ground' is supported by 'proving ground'/'rite of passage'. Group ended at Blakey's death in 1990 while deathYear is null -- flag for the data owner if the group schema expects a dissolution year.

artist:thelonious-monk.notes: Entity confirmed: stored URL resolves to 'American jazz pianist and composer (1917-1982)'. Verified: birth/death years, piano/composer, angular melodies, dissonances, percussive attack, whole-tone runs, silences, ''Round Midnight'/'Straight, No Chaser'/'Blue Monk' as repertoire standards, house pianist at Minton's Playhouse where bebop was formulated. Unverifiable (extract truncates around 1948): the 'wrongful cabaret card revocation', 'major recognition in the late 1950s with Riverside and Columbia recordings', and influence on avant-garde/art music. No contradictions found.

artist:tina-brooks.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Birth (June 7, 1932), death (August 13, 1974), tenor saxophone, hard bop association all confirmed by the stored-URL page.

artist:tom-harrell.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Born June 16, 1946 (living, deathYear null correct); trumpet/flugelhorn confirmed. All award claims (JJA Trumpeter of the Year 2018, DownBeat awards, SESAC, BMI, Prix Oscar du Jazz, Grammy nomination for Time's Mirror) appear word-for-word in the evidence.

artist:tomasz-stanko.notes: Verified: 1942-2018, Polish trumpeter, free jazz association, Komeda connection (joined Komeda quintet 1963, recorded on Astigmatic), ECM recordings, late-career quartet of young Polish musicians (Wasilewski/Kurkiewicz/16-year-old Miskiewicz) plus the 2007 Scandinavian quintet (Dark Eyes, ECM), international standing. 'Dark, haunting sound' and 'melancholy of Eastern European music' are editorial, not flagged.

artist:tony-williams.notes: Verified: Anthony Tillmon Williams, 1945-1997, drummer, joined Miles Davis's Second Great Quintet at 17, pioneered fusion with The Tony Williams Lifetime, polyrhythms. 'One of the most innovative drummers' / 'influenced generations' are editorial, consistent with hall-of-fame inductions and the Christgau quote in evidence.

artist:tubby-hayes.notes: Verified: 1935-1973, British, tenor saxophone + flute + vibraphone (all three confirmed in evidence), died at 38. Unverifiable: 'led his own band at 16' (evidence says at 16 he JOINED Kenny Baker's sextet; his own octet was formed in 1955 - in tension but not contradicted); 'first British jazz musician to lead his own group at a New York club' (evidence describes the Half Note residency via a Musicians' Union exchange but never says 'first'); 'heart problems' as cause of death (extract truncates before his death). 'Greatest jazz musician Britain ever produced' is an editorial superlative; evidence says 'one of the finest jazz saxophonists to have emerged from Britain'.

artist:tyshawn-sorey.notes: Verified: born 1980, MacArthur Fellowship 2017, drums/trombone (began as classical trombone major)/piano/composer, album 'The Inner Spectrum of Variables' exists (2016, trio plus strings), work spans jazz and classical from solo percussion to opera and orchestral commissions. Editorial characterizations consistent with evidence.

artist:various-artists.notes: Catalog placeholder, not a real artist. The bio's only claim ('Compilation or collection featuring multiple contributing artists') matches the resolved page - 'Various artists' redirects to 'Compilation album', which covers exactly this concept. birthYear 0 is a placeholder artifact a repair-writer may want to null; empty instruments list is expected. Nothing contradicted.

artist:victor-wooten.notes: Bio is near-verbatim the Wikipedia lede. Born September 11, 1964; Flecktones bassist since the group's 1988 formation; SMV with Stanley Clarke and Marcus Miller (evidence adds 2008-2009); Nitro 2017-2019. All confirmed.

artist:vijay-iyer.notes: Bio is an older snapshot of the Wikipedia lede; born October 26, 1971, pianist, NYT quote, MacArthur 2013, Doris Duke, USA Fellowship, Alpert Award all verbatim-confirmed. One staleness: ours says 'a Grammy nomination' where the current page says 'three Grammy nominations' - a subset, not a contradiction, so not flagged as error; repair-writer may update the count.

artist:vinnie-colaiuta.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede; born February 5, 1956; Modern Drummer HoF 1996, Classic Drummer HoF 2014, one Grammy + two nominations, Zappa/Mitchell/Sting all confirmed word-for-word. Data-quality (not a factual error): instruments field contains 'musician' rather than an instrument; evidence identifies him as a drummer - should be 'drums'.

artist:wallace-roney.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede, including dates (May 25, 1960 - March 31, 2020), one Grammy + two nominations, lessons from Clark Terry and Dizzy Gillespie, study with Miles Davis 1985-1991, and the 'only trumpet player Davis personally mentored' line. All confirmed.

artist:wayne-shorter.notes: Verified: 1933-2023, tenor/soprano saxophone + composer, Jazz Messengers from 1959 staying four years (= ours '1959-63'), joined Davis's Second Great Quintet 1964 with last Davis recordings 1970 (= ours '1964-70'), Blue Note leader albums Night Dreamer/JuJu/Speak No Evil, Weather Report co-founder 1970, compositions became enduring standards. 'Creatively vital into his 80s' consistent with the six-decade career and death at 89 in 2023.

artist:weather-report.notes: Verified: active 1970-1986 (matches birthYear/deathYear), founded 1970, co-led by Zawinul and Shorter (both played on Davis's In a Silent Way / Bitches Brew, supporting 'formed from Miles Davis alumni'), evolution from free-improvising/experimental beginnings to a funky groove-oriented sound, 'Birdland' as 1977 hit single, Jaco Pastorius's virtuoso impact, 16-year career, fusion-defining status. Unverifiable: the album title 'Heavy Weather' (1977) - the extract confirms 'Birdland' as a 1977 hit single but never names its parent album. No contradictions. Minor: the page lede says active to 1986 while the body mentions 'break-up in late 1985'; ours matches the lede.

artist:wes-montgomery.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. 1923-1968, guitarist, thumb-plucking technique and octaves all confirmed.

artist:willie-the-lion-smith.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede (full name, 'the Lion' nickname, American jazz and stride pianist). 1893-1973 confirmed (November 23, 1893 - April 18, 1973).

artist:woody-shaw.notes: Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lede. 1944-1989, trumpet/flugelhorn/cornet, composer-arranger-bandleader-educator framing all confirmed word-for-word.

artist:world-saxophone-quartet.notes: Right entity: original membership (Julius Hemphill, Oliver Lake, David Murray, Hamiet Bluiett) confirmed exactly. 'Performing without a rhythm section' is consistent with 'principally recorded and performed as a saxophone quartet' (later sometimes joined by drummers/bassists). The 'Revue' album is unverifiable - the discography is not in the extract. Caveat for human review: founding is sometimes dated late 1976 (first concerts) vs 1977 elsewhere, so the 1976-vs-1977 discrepancy may be a genuine source ambiguity rather than corruption; the evidence as given says 1977. Bio's present tense ('is a groundbreaking ensemble') also conflicts with the 2016 split and 'was' in evidence.

artist:wynton-marsalis.notes: Verified: born 1961, trumpeter/composer/educator, moved to New York in 1979 (Juilliard), Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers 1980-82, own quintets, Blood on the Fields as first jazz Pulitzer (1997), Jazz at Lincoln Center artistic director (helped start the 1987 Classical Jazz series that became J@LC), education/media work (Marsalis on Music, Peabody, CBS), and criticism of his conservative aesthetic consistent with the page's 'Debate on jazz' section. 'Led the neo-traditional movement' and 'jazz's first major permanent institution' are editorial characterizations, not contradicted.

artist:yuki-arimasa.notes: The stored ja.wikipedia URL (titled with the Japanese name) returned no page (page: null) - the article may not exist or the title may be wrong, so the site may be shipping a dead link; flag for link check. No evidence document survived, so nothing is checkable: birthYear 1965, the Bill Evans influence, and 'His Village Vanguard appearance marked a milestone for Japanese jazz' are all unverified.

artist:yusef-lateef.notes: Verified: 1920-2013, tenor saxophone and flute as main instruments plus oboe (rare in jazz), bamboo flute and shehnai (ours 'shanai'), 'played world music before world music had a name', Eastern Sounds (1961), conversion to Islam (Ahmadiyya) in the period after his 1950 return to Detroit (ours 'in 1950' consistent), doctorate (Ed.D. 1975, UMass Amherst). 'Writing extensively on music education' is loosely supported (flute method book, education dissertation, professorships) and not contradicted.

artist:zim-ngqawana.notes: Verified: 1959-2011, South African saxophonist/flautist (+ composer per short description), 'Zimology' attested ('He was later known as Zimology'; album and institute of that name). Unverifiable: 'A student of Bheki Mseleku' - evidence names Archie Shepp and Yusef Lateef as his teachers (UMass) and never mentions Mseleku (absence, not contradiction); 'Xhosa musical traditions' and 'drew equally on free jazz and indigenous African music' are not attested in evidence. Nuance: ours says he called his STYLE Zimology; evidence presents Zimology as a name he was known by.

album:bix-beiderbecke-and-the-chicago-co.notes: MB (search, sim 1.0) matches title/artist: a 1974 Milestone archival LP, secondaryTypes empty but plainly a reissue of 1920s material. 'from 1926' cannot be verified: MB dates the release 1974 and no evidence dates the recordings; wiki bio says Bix first recorded with the Wolverines in 1924, so even as a recording-era year 1926 deserves review. Bio claims in prose (most influential 1920s soloist, lyrical cornet, 'shooting bullets at a bell') are confirmed verbatim by the Bix wiki page, but that page is about the artist, not this album - stored album link points to the artist page (deliberate fallback, not a disambiguation collision; flagged wrong_entity because it is not the album's page). Data glitch: prose quote is truncated mid-word ('at a bel.'). 'contemporary jazz album' boilerplate misdescribes archival material.

album:masterpieces-by-ellington.notes: No confident MB match (candidates only; top candidate title/artist/1951 align). Stored wiki page is about our album and confirms the prose verbatim: first Ellington LP, recorded for Columbia 1950, early 12-inch LP, four tracks, three concert arrangements plus 'The Tattooed Bride'; 1951 release year confirmed. Label field is 'Unknown' (no claim) - evidence says Columbia, available for repair. keyTracks: no MB tracklist; wiki names Mood Indigo / Sophisticated Lady / Solitude / The Tattooed Bride (4 of ours 6); 'Vagabonds' and 'Smada' are not in the extract - wiki says the album 'consisted of four tracks' with unnamed bonus tracks on the 2004 CD reissue, so those two are likely CD bonus material; reviewer should confirm before shipping them as keyTracks.

album:in-walked-bud.notes: No MB evidence (empty search). Stored wiki page (The Amazing Bud Powell) is about our album and confirms the prose verbatim: sessions Aug 8 1949 and May 1 1951, Blue Note, April 1952 release, the quintet (Navarro/Rollins/Potter/Haynes) and trio/solo lineups. Fields: label Blue Note confirmed; year 1951 is the second session's recording year - wiki gives release as April 1952 (10") and the 'Volume 1' recompile as 1956 (BLP 1503), so the year field follows recording-date convention rather than release date; not flagged, but reviewer should note. keyTracks: no MB tracklist; wiki mentions Un Poco Loco, A Night in Tunisia, Bouncing with Bud (not Parisian Thoroughfare). Item id 'in-walked-bud' oddly differs from title; out of scope but worth a look.

album:ambiance-bechet.notes: Embedded MB release confirms everything checkable: Disques Vogue (LD 049), 1952, France, and both tracks named in the prose; all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist. MB credits 'Sidney Bechet All Stars' - same leader, band credit, not a mismatch. 'clarinetist' descriptor and 'dixieland vein' have no evidence either way (Bechet is usually identified with soprano sax as well; no instrument data in pack). No wiki evidence; no URL shipped.

album:bird-and-diz.notes: Embedded MB release (1952 FR pressing) confirms year 1952, label Blue Star (also Mercury MGC-512), artists Bird/Diz, and that 'Passport' and 'Visa' are on the album. All keyTracks match after stripping take suffixes ('My Melancholy Baby' ~ MB 'Melancholy Baby'). Unverifiable from this item's evidence: recording dates (June 6 1950; March/May 1949), 'without Gillespie and with different personnel', and the 'originally issued ... on the Verve subsidiary label Clef Records' claim (MB shows Blue Star/Mercury for this pressing; no Clef). The twin item bird-and-diz-gillespie carries the album's wiki page, which confirms all of these verbatim - repair-writer can rely on that; per-item evidence here cannot.

album:bird-and-diz-gillespie.notes: Stored wiki page is our album and confirms the prose verbatim (recorded primarily June 6 1950 NYC; Passport/Visa recorded 1949 by Parker without Gillespie; issued 1952 in 10" format on Clef). Embedded MB release confirms year 1952 and label Blue Star (FR pressing, with Mercury MGC-512), and all keyTracks match after take-suffix stripping (wiki CD listing also shows Bloomdido, My Melancholy Baby, An Oscar for Treadwell, Mohawk). Listing the duo album under 'Dizzy Gillespie' is the site's per-artist convention, consistent with MB credit 'Bird, Diz'.

album:on-parade.notes: Embedded MB release confirms 1952, Disques Vogue (LD 048), and both tracks named in prose; all 5 keyTracks on tracklist. MB credits Sidney Bechet AND Claude Luter - ours lists Bechet alone; Luter's band backed Bechet on these Vogue dates, so this reads as credit-style variance, but repair-writer may want the co-credit. Stored wiki URL is a hard collision: 'On Parade' resolves to a 1964 Canadian TV variety series - wrong entity, discarded; repoint or drop. Unverifiable prose claims: 'clarinetist', and 'first recorded several months before trumpeter Louis Armstrong' (no supporting evidence in pack). Typo: 'A early jazz outing'.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: It was first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra, and played a prominent part in the 1971 motion picture Play Misty for Me.
wrongClaims.problem: Sentence lifted from Erroll Garner's bio where 'It' refers to his composition 'Misty'; pasted here it falsely asserts the 1952 album Solo Flight was first recorded in 1956 (MB: released 1952, Columbia CL 6209).
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: His instrumental ballad "Misty", his best-known composition, has become a jazz standard. It was first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra, and played a prominent part in the 1971 motion picture Play Misty for Me.

album:solo-flight.notes: Album entity is right: embedded MB release confirms title, Erroll Garner, 1952, Columbia CL 6209, the two named standouts, and all 5 keyTracks. The Misty sentence is the corruption. Also questionable but editorial: 'delivers a bebop program' - the Garner wiki page says he was 'not a bebop musician per se'; not flagged as a wrongClaim (genre framing), but a repair-writer should reword. Stored wiki URL is the artist page, not an album page (fallback, not a collision).

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: artists: ["Lennie Tristano", "Arnold Ross"]

album:holiday-in-piano.notes: MB (search, sim 1.0) confirms title, 1953, Mercury Records (MG 26029), and all 5 keyTracks - but credits the release jointly to Lennie Tristano AND Arnold Ross (likely a split 10" with sides by each pianist). Ours credits Tristano alone and the prose presents the whole program as his; which tracks are whose is not in evidence. MB search is strong-not-infallible, hence moderate confidence. Background note (not flagged): 'bebop program' is odd framing for Tristano, usually filed under cool/Tristano school; no pack evidence either way.

album:jazz-at-massey-hall.notes: Stored wiki page is our album and confirms the prose verbatim: released December 1953 on Debut, recorded 15 May 1953 at Massey Hall, Toronto; the Quintet = Gillespie, Parker, Powell, Mingus, Roach; only time the five recorded as a unit; last recorded meeting of Parker and Gillespie. MB (sim 1.0) confirms The Quintet / Debut DEB-124 and all 4 keyTracks; MB's 1956 date is the 12" edition (wiki shows the original came out as two 10" LPs in Dec 1953), so ours.year 1953 stands. 'Greatest bebop performance ever recorded' is editorial; wiki supports the legend framing ('the greatest jazz concert ever' tag).

album:king-of-the-tenors.notes: Embedded MBID resolves to the original Norgran LP 'The Consummate Artistry of Ben Webster' (1954, MGN 1001) whose release-group is titled 'King of the Tenors' with firstReleaseDate 1953 - confirming ours.year 1953, the Norgran claim, and the original-title claim; all 5 keyTracks on tracklist. Unverifiable from pack evidence: the 1957 Verve reissue claim (consistent with the RG title but not shown), the personnel claim (Oscar Peterson Trio; Benny Carter and Harry 'Sweets' Edison on several tracks), and the label field 'Verve' (MB shows Norgran for the embedded release; 'Verve' matches the reissue convention the prose describes). No contradictions; no wiki evidence; no URL shipped.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Night Train is an album by the Oscar Peterson Trio, released in 1963 by Verve Records.
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA describes a different album (Night Train, 1963, Verve) instead of Oscar Peterson Plays George Gershwin.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: shortDescription: "1963 studio album by the Oscar Peterson Trio"

album:oscar-peterson-plays-george-gershw.notes: Stored wiki link also points to Night Train (Oscar Peterson album), so the site ships a wrong link for this item. The MB search hit is a 1954 Swedish Karusell EP (KEP 201) of the same title with only 4 tracks; entity match is unsure (ours claims a 1953 Clef LP), so the keyTracks mismatch is a weak signal: the two unmatched tracks may exist on the full Clef LP, which is absent from evidence. Year (1953 vs MB EP release group 1954) and label (Clef vs Karusell) are not flagged because the MB doc is a foreign EP issue, not proof about the original release. Repair needs a full prose rewrite about the Gershwin songbook album plus a corrected wiki link.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: The three sessions were subsequently recompiled across The Eminent Jay Jay Johnson, Volume 1 (BLP 1505) and The Eminent Jay Jay Johnson, Volume 2 (BLP 1506).

album:the-eminent-jay-jay-johnson-vol-1.notes: albumDNA is the wiki lead verbatim and entity-correct; the label fieldIssue is a fillable gap (ours asserts 'Unknown'), not a contradiction, and does not change the verdict class. Year field 1953 vs prose/evidence 'released on Blue Note in 1956': 1953 matches the documented June 22, 1953 first session; the site appears to date archival/compiled albums by material year (see also a-night-at-birdland, at-carnegie-hall), so not flagged as a contradiction, but field (1953) and prose ('released ... in 1956') visibly disagree on the page. No MB evidence (search returned nothing).

album:the-great-artistry-of-django-reinh.notes: albumDNA is a verbatim Django Reinhardt artist biography ('Jean Reinhardt ... was a Belgian-born Romani jazz guitarist and composer'), i.e. content about a person while the item is an album (the spec's entity_mismatch tell). The bio facts themselves match the person page, so no wrongClaims; the problem is wrong kind of content. Album fields are solid: embedded MBID resolves to release group 'The Great Artistry of Django Reinhardt' (firstReleaseDate 1953-11, matching year 1953) and all 5 keyTracks are on the MB tracklist. Label 'Clef Records' is unverifiable (the MB doc is a 2016 Jazz Images reissue) but not contradicted. Stored wiki link goes to the artist page, not an album page. Repair: write album-specific prose; fix or drop the wiki link.

album:a-night-at-birdland.notes: Prose matches the wiki page verbatim plus legitimate editorial. Year 1954 corresponds to the original 10-inch LPs ('originally spread out over three 10" LPs as A Night at Birdland Vols. 1-3 (1954)' per wiki); the 12-inch Vols 1 & 2 came in 1956 and the prose states both, so no contradiction. The MB doc is a 2005 Dreyfus compilation of the same February 21, 1954 Birdland material; all 4 keyTracks appear on its tracklist. Label Blue Note confirmed by wiki.

album:chet-baker-sings.notes: All claims verified verbatim against the wiki page (debut vocal album, 1954, Pacific Jazz, Grammy Hall of Fame 2001, My Funny Valentine as signature song) and MB (Pacific Jazz PJLP-11, 1954). All 4 keyTracks on the MB original-LP tracklist. Editorial tail ('Proved that jazz vocals could be introverted...') is opinion, not flagged.

album:lester-young-with-the-oscar-peters.notes: All 4 keyTracks exactly equal the embedded MB release tracklist; year 1954 and label Blue Star match the embedded release (French Blue Star GLP 6988), which is authoritative for this anchor. Wiki notes the US June 1954 originals were Norgran (MGN 5) -- not a contradiction (different-territory issue of the same album), but a curator may prefer Norgran as the canonical label. albumDNA describes the 1997 Verve collation that subsumes #1; its facts (J. C. Heard, three source albums, June 1954) check against the wiki page.

Year, label and all 4 keyTracks verified by embedded MBID (Norgran MG N-6, 1954) and consistent with wiki (MGN 6, June 1954). Same collation-style albumDNA as the #1 item; all facts check against the wiki page.

album:louis-armstrong-plays-wc-handy.notes: albumDNA is verbatim from the wiki page (1954 All Stars release, AllMusic quotes, 1986 altered CD, 1997 restoration with Avakian-Handy interview and rehearsal tracks); all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist; year 1954 matches the release group. Label note: the embedded MB doc is a 1966 Dutch CBS pressing (CBS is Columbia's international imprint and carries the Columbia catalog number CL 591), and wiki references Columbia for the CD reissues, so 'Columbia' is consistent, not flagged.

album:tatum-carter-bellson.notes: albumDNA is Art Tatum career/discography boilerplate ('Pianist Art Tatum recorded commercially from 1932 until near his death...'), not content about this album -- same misplaced-kind pattern as a person bio on an album item. Fields are verified by embedded MBID: 1954, Clef MG C-643, all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist. Stored wiki link is the Art Tatum discography list page, not an album page (it does list 'Tatum - Carter - Bellson (Clef, 1955)'; note wiki's 1955 vs MB's authoritative 1954 -- ours says 1954, kept). Artist field 'Art Tatum' vs MB credit 'Tatum, Carter, Bellson' is site primary-artist convention, not flagged. Repair: album-specific prose; a dedicated album wiki page may not exist.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: A Norgran Records session that documents clarinetist Lester Young working through the bebop vocabulary of the bebop period.
wrongClaims.problem: Misleading instrument framing: the evidence identifies Young primarily as a tenor saxophonist with clarinet only occasional; the pack has no evidence he plays clarinet on this album.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: was an American jazz tenor saxophonist and occasional clarinetist

album:the-president.notes: First albumDNA sentence is the Lester Young person bio verbatim (person content on an album item -- the entity_mismatch tell); second sentence is generic generated filler ('bebop vocabulary of the bebop period' is tautological, and the bio characterizes Young's idiom as relaxed/cool swing, though there is no direct contradiction to cite for that). Fields are verified by embedded MBID: Norgran MGN 1005, 1954, and all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist. Stored wiki link is the artist page, not an album page. Repair: album-specific prose; the leader's instrument should be tenor saxophone unless a source shows otherwise.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: released on Blue Note in October 1956
wrongClaims.problem: The embedded MB release (Blue Note BLP 1518) and its release group date the album 1955, contradicting an October 1956 release; the site's own year field (1955) agrees with MB and contradicts the prose, so one of the two must be wrong either way.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: date: "1955"; releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1955"

album:horace-silver-and-the-jazz-messeng.notes: Caution for repair: embedded MBID is authoritative per spec, but the 'October 1956' wording reads like Wikipedia prose about the 12-inch issue, while MB's 1955 may reflect the 10-inch era; verify externally before rewriting either the prose or the year. Unverified claims (no wiki evidence in pack): the two 1955 10-inch LPs (Horace Silver Quintet Vol. 3 / Vol. 4), session dates November 13, 1954 and February 6, 1955, 'Silver's debut 12"', and the personnel (Mobley, Dorham, Watkins, Blakey). Label Blue Note and year field 1955 match MB; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist.

album:in-the-land-of-hi-fi.notes: Prose is verbatim from the wiki page (recorded October 25-27, 1955, NYC; Adderley alternate takes on the Mercury compilation). EmArcy MG 36058, 1955, and all 5 keyTracks verified by embedded MBID; wiki personnel confirms Cannonball Adderley on the sessions.

album:sarah-vaughan-clifford-brown.notes: No MB evidence (search empty); all checks are against the stored wiki page, which is entity-correct ('Sarah Vaughan, reissued in 1991 as Sarah Vaughan with Clifford Brown'). Verified verbatim: 1955, EmArcy, only Vaughan-Brown collaboration, Vaughan's own favorite through 1980, Grammy Hall of Fame 1999. keyTracksVerdict 'ok' is based on the wiki track listing (all 4 present), since no MB tracklist exists. Editorial tail not flagged.

album:study-in-brown.notes: Prose is verbatim from the wiki page (Lands End by Harold Land, Sandu by Brown, George's Dilemma aka Ulcer Department, acclaimed Cherokee solo) plus an editorial tail ('before his tragic death', Morgan/Hubbard influence) that is uncontradicted context/opinion. EmArcy MG 36037, 1955 verified by MB; all 4 keyTracks on the MB tracklist.

album:telefunken-blues.notes: Recorded late 1954 / early 1955 and first released on Savoy -- verbatim from wiki, and the wiki session dates (Nov 1, 1954; Feb 7, 1955) agree. Savoy MG 12006, 1955 per embedded MBID; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. The 'bebop revolution' sentence is editorial era framing, not flagged.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: The three sessions were subsequently recompiled across The Eminent Jay Jay Johnson, Volume 1 (BLP 1505) and The Eminent Jay Jay Johnson, Volume 2 (BLP 1506).

album:the-eminent-jay-jay-johnson-vol-2.notes: Same entity-correct verbatim prose as the Vol. 1 item; the label fieldIssue is a fillable gap ('Unknown'), not a contradiction, and does not change the verdict class. Year field 1955 vs 'released on Blue Note in 1956': 1955 matches the documented June 6, 1955 final session whose material went to Volume 2 (the 10-inch 'Volume 2' was 1954, the 12-inch Volume 2 was 1956) -- material-year convention, so not flagged as contradiction, but field and prose visibly disagree. No MB evidence.

album:the-genius-of-art-tatum-9.notes: Series-level prose matches the wiki series page verbatim (Clef, Grammy Hall of Fame 1978, Pablo 7-CD box July 1991), and that page covers Volume 9; embedded MBID verifies #9 itself (Clef MG C-660, 1955, all 5 keyTracks). Typo worth fixing: 'Would You Lie to Take a Walk' -- MB carries the same string, so it matches evidence, but wiki's Volume 9 list gives 'Would You Like to Take a Walk?'. Wiki calls the series 1953-54 while #9's release date is 1955 (volume releases trailed the sessions) -- no contradiction with ours.

album:ellington-at-newport.notes: albumDNA is verbatim from the wiki page (Wein quote, 1001 Albums, studio re-creation) plus an editorial close. Year 1956 supported by wiki and by the MB candidate release group (1956-09). Label 'Columbia' is supported only indirectly ('Afterwards, Ellington once again had a contract with Columbia Records'). No tracklist in evidence; the keyTracks include announcer-segment titles ('Father Norman O'Connor Introduces Duke & The Orchestra...') that look like the 1999 'Ellington at Newport 1956' complete edition rather than the original LP -- unverifiable here, flag for curator. Wiki does confirm Star Spangled Banner, Black and Tan Fantasy and Tea for Two were performed. MB gave search candidates only, no full release doc.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: labels: [{name: "Blue Note", catalogNumber: "BLP 1543"}]

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Kenny Burrell is an album by guitarist Kenny Burrell recorded in 1957 and released on the Prestige label.
wrongClaims.problem: Our item per embedded MBID is the 1956 Blue Note album (BLP 1543); this sentence, the keyTracks and the stored wiki link all describe the different same-titled 1957 Prestige album.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Kenny Burrell"; date: "1956"; labels: Blue Note BLP 1543; releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1956"

album:kenny-burrell.notes: Classic same-name collision. The keyTracks (Don't Cry Baby, Drum Boogie, Strictly Confidential, All of You, Perception) are exactly the Prestige album's wiki tracklist, and none appear on the embedded MB Blue Note tracklist (Get Happy, But Not for Me, Mexico City, Moten Swing, Cheeta, Now See How You Are, Phinupi, How About You?). The prose even self-contradicts ('recorded in 1957 ... this 1956 session'). Year field 1956 matches the Blue Note album. Repair: decide the anchor -- the embedded MBID says Blue Note 1956 -- then rewrite prose, keyTracks and wiki link to that album consistently (or re-anchor everything to the Prestige album).

album:pithecanthropus-erectus.notes: Prose is verbatim from the wiki page (taught arrangements by ear). Atlantic 1237 verified; all 4 keyTracks equal the full MB tracklist. One evidence-internal month discrepancy: prose and wiki say released August 1956 while the embedded MB release says 1956-07; the year agrees everywhere and ours matches the entity-matched wiki page, so not flagged -- recommend no change without an external check.

album:saxophone-colossus.notes: Prose is verbatim from the wiki page (sixth studio album, recorded mono June 22, 1956, Weinstock/Van Gelder, Hackensack, Flanagan/Watkins/Roach quartet). Year field 1956: wiki explicitly documents that release dating is disputed (Mojo: autumn 1956; Billboard implies 1957) and the MB release group says 1957-04-27; the recording year 1956 is solid and one source supports a 1956 release, so not flagged -- curator should be aware. Prestige verified (LP 7079); all 4 keyTracks on the MB tracklist.

album:the-benny-goodman-story-vol-2.notes: Generated-style prose. Verified by the MB search hit (Decca Records DL 8253, 1956): year, label, and that 'Memories of You' and 'China Boy' are on the album; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Unverifiable substantive claims: 'documents a 1956 studio date' (no recording-date evidence in the pack; these film-soundtrack-related sessions are commonly dated 1955 -- background knowledge, not citable) and 'leading a tight big band ensemble' (ensemble format not in evidence; some numbers on this album are associated with small groups). No wiki page found by opensearch.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: The Sermon is an album of spirituals by American jazz pianist Hampton Hawes recorded in 1958, but not released on the Contemporary label until 1987.
wrongClaims.problem: Describes a different Hampton Hawes album (The Sermon, recorded 1958, released 1987); our item per embedded MBID is the 1956 trio album. The collision likely happened because our album contains a track titled 'The Sermon'.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Everybody Likes Hampton Hawes, Vol. 3: The Trio"; date: "1956"

album:the-trio-volume-3-everybody-likes-.notes: Fields are correct per embedded MBID (Contemporary C3523, 1956) and all 5 keyTracks are on the MB tracklist -- including the track 'The Sermon', which explains the wrong article match. The Sermon album's wiki tracklist (spirituals: Down by the Riverside, etc.) shares nothing with our album. The second prose sentence is generic filler ('cool jazz vocabulary of the hard bop period'). Stored wiki link points to The Sermon (Hampton Hawes album) -- wrong entity shown to users. Repair: rewrite prose for the 1956 trio album; replace the wiki link.

album:2-guitars.notes: First sentence verbatim from the wiki page; Prestige 7119, 1957 verified by embedded MBID; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Artist field credits Burrell alone while MB credits Burrell and Raney jointly (the prose names both) -- site primary-artist convention, not flagged. The context sentence 'Prestige under Bob Weinstock was known for its prolific output of blowing sessions' is scene-setting that cannot be checked from this item's evidence (it is consistent with Weinstock appearing as Prestige's producer elsewhere in the pack); uncontradicted.

album:at-carnegie-hall.notes: Prose is verbatim from the wiki page (recorded Carnegie Hall November 29, 1957; released September 27, 2005, by Blue Note -- label verified by wiki, and the MB candidate RG date 2005-09-27 agrees). Year field 1957 is the recording year; the first release was 2005, as our own prose states -- material-year convention as elsewhere on the site, not flagged as a contradiction, but a curator should confirm that convention is intended. MB returned a matching candidate only (no full release doc) and no tracklist exists in evidence, so the 4 keyTracks (Monk's Mood, Evidence, Crepuscule with Nellie, Nutty) are unverified.

album:birth-of-the-cool.notes: Prose lead is verbatim the Wikipedia lead. MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms Capitol Records, 1957-02, release-group secondaryType Compilation, and an 11-track listing including all 4 keyTracks; 'compiles eleven tracks ... three sessions during 1949 and 1950' matches both sources. 'Named and defined the cool jazz movement' and the West Coast/Third Stream influence sentence are editorial; wiki's 'considered seminal in the history of cool jazz' is consistent.

album:blues-and-vanilla.notes: MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms title, RCA Victor, 1957, the Jack Montrose + Red Norvo credit, and all 6 keyTracks (exact tracklist match). Unverifiable: 'recorded in 1956' (no recording date in evidence) and Montrose being a saxophonist leading a quintet. Nothing contradicts. Site's artist field shows only Red Norvo while MB credits 'Jack Montrose, Red Norvo' (Montrose first); the prose states the co-credit, so not flagged. No wikipedia evidence found.

album:brilliant-corners.notes: MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms title/artist/year/label (Riverside RLP 12-226, 1957) and all 4 keyTracks. The detailed prose claims are uncheckable against pack evidence: recorded 1956, Sonny Rollins and Max Roach personnel, 25 takes with the title track spliced from multiple attempts, celeste on the session, 'Pannonica' dedicated to Baroness Pannonica de Koenigswarter, Time magazine cover. No contradictions; no wikipedia evidence. Judge's background knowledge (not citable) finds these claims consistent with this album's standard history, so likely repair-low-priority.

album:dizzy-atmosphere.notes: Embedded MB and the stored wiki page agree: Specialty label, 1957 (RG first release 1957-02-01), Lee Morgan. Wiki personnel confirm Al Grey (trombone) and Billy Mitchell (tenor saxophone); 'members of Dizzy Gillespie's Orchestra' is verbatim from the wiki lead. All 6 keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly (take-suffixes included). MB also credits Wynton Kelly as co-artist; ours lists Morgan alone, but Morgan is a credited artist, so not flagged.

keyTracksUnmatched: And Her Tears Flowed Like Wine

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Like Someone in Love is a 1957 studio album by the American jazz singer Ella Fitzgerald, with a studio orchestra arranged and conducted by Frank DeVol
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA describes a different album (Like Someone in Love, a Verve-era LP arranged by Frank DeVol), not Ella and Her Fellas, which the authoritative embedded MB release shows is a 1957 Decca LP
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Ella and Her Fellas"; labels: Decca Records, DL 8477; date: 1957

wrongClaims.ourClaim: recorded at the same time as her albums with Louis Armstrong
wrongClaims.problem: Sentence is lifted verbatim from the Wikipedia lead of Like Someone in Love (Ella Fitzgerald album); the fact belongs to that album, not this one
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Like Someone in Love is a 1957 studio album by the American jazz singer Ella Fitzgerald, with a studio orchestra arranged and conducted by Frank DeVol. This album represents a fine example of Ella's singing from this period, recorded at the same time as her albums with Louis Armstrong.

album:ella-and-her-fellas.notes: Classic disambiguation substitution: prose and stored wikipedia link are both for Like Someone in Love (Ella Fitzgerald album); the item is Ella and Her Fellas (Decca DL 8477, 1957, per embedded MB). Title/artist/year/label fields are correct; the prose needs a full rewrite and the link must be replaced or dropped. 5 of 6 keyTracks are not on the authoritative MB tracklist (only 'Stone Cold Dead in the Market (He Had It Coming)' matches) - the keyTracks appear to come from yet another source and need rebuilding from the MB tracklist.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Mingus is an album by the jazz bassist and composer Charles Mingus. The album was recorded in October and November 1960 in New York and released in late 1961 on Nat Hentoff's Candid label.
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA is the Wikipedia lead of the 1961 Candid album 'Mingus', a different record; the item is Four Trombones (Debut, 1957), and the 1960/1961/Candid facts contradict the embedded MB release
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Four Trombones"; labels: Debut, DEB-126; date: 1957; artists: Kai Winding, J.J. Johnson, Bennie Green, Willie Dennis, John Lewis, Charlie Mingus

album:four-trombones.notes: Prose and stored wikipedia link both belong to 'Mingus (Charles Mingus album)' (Candid, recorded Oct-Nov 1960, released 1961) - wrong entity. The real Four Trombones is the 1957 Debut LP; year and label fields are correct and all 4 keyTracks match the embedded MB tracklist exactly. Note the site files this multi-leader album (Winding/Johnson/Green/Dennis with Lewis and Mingus) under Charles Mingus alone; Mingus is credited ('Charlie Mingus') so not flagged. Prose rewrite + link replacement needed.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Jay and Kai is an album by American jazz trombonists J. J. Johnson and Kai Winding featuring performances recorded in 1955 and 1956 for the Columbia label.

album:jay-and-kai.notes: Label field ships as the placeholder 'Unknown' while both our prose and the entity-matched wiki page say Columbia - a straightforward field fix; the prose itself is fine (it is the wiki lead verbatim). Year field 1957 matches wiki's '1957 studio album' description. No MusicBrainz evidence at all (search returned no candidates). Minor wrinkle a reviewer should know: the wiki session list includes one track recorded January 27, 1957, although the page's own lead (and our prose, copied from it) says 'recorded in 1955 and 1956' - the wiki is internally inconsistent, so not flagged against ours. keyTracks not stored (null).

album:jazz-by-sun-ra.notes: Prose is a condensed verbatim copy of the wiki lead; every claim confirmed: debut album, first-pressing label reading '07-12-56', Tom Wilson's Transition Records (MB: Transition, trlp 10, 1957), Delmark owner Bob Koester purchase in the mid-1960s, 1967 reissue. All 5 keyTracks on the embedded MB tracklist.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Louis Armstrong Meets Oscar Peterson is a 1959 studio album (recorded in 1957) by Louis Armstrong, accompanied by Oscar Peterson.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Louis Armstrong Meets Oscar Peterson is a 1959 studio album
wrongClaims.problem: Contradicts the item's own year field (1957) and the MB release-group first release date; the item is internally inconsistent - whichever source is right, either the prose year or the year field is wrong
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: releaseGroup firstReleaseDate: "1957"

album:louis-armstrong-meets-oscar-peters.notes: Internal inconsistency: year field says 1957, prose says '1959 studio album'. The two entity-matched evidence sources disagree with each other: MB release group gives firstReleaseDate 1957 (embedded method, though the embedded release itself is a 1985 German Verve CD reissue), while Wikipedia says 1959 release recorded in 1957. Judge's background knowledge (not citable) leans toward 1959 release / 1957 recording, which would make the year field the error - needs external confirmation before repair. 'That Old Feeling' and all other keyTracks are on the MB tracklist ('Blues in the Night' matches 'Blues in the Night (My Mama Done Tol' Me)'); 'vocal jazz output from 1957' is consistent with the recorded-in-1957 evidence.

album:mulligan-meets-monk.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim; wiki confirms Riverside 1957 original, numerous reissues, and the 1987 CD remaster with three additional alternate takes. Embedded MBID resolves to a 1982 Milestone reissue titled ''Round Midnight' (M-47067) but it sits in the Mulligan Meets Monk release group (first release 1957-10), so entity match holds at release-group level; ours.label Riverside refers to the original issue and is wiki-confirmed. All 5 keyTracks present on the reissue tracklist (case-insensitive). Duplicate of item 'mulligan-meets-monk-monk' filed under the other co-leader.

album:mulligan-meets-monk-monk.notes: Same album as item 'mulligan-meets-monk', filed under Thelonious Monk. Here the embedded MB release is the original Riverside RLP 1106 (US, 1957); all 5 keyTracks match its tracklist exactly, and the wiki page confirms every prose claim (Riverside 1957, 1987 CD remaster with three added alternate takes).

album:sonny-rollins-volume-2.notes: Thin prose, but everything checkable is confirmed by the embedded MB release: Blue Note BLP 1558, 1957, Sonny Rollins, and all 5 keyTracks including the two named in the prose ('Why Don't I', 'Wail March'). 'Hard bop' characterization is editorial. No wikipedia evidence; no URL stored.

album:such-sweet-thunder.notes: Wiki page (stored link) confirms both prose claims - released 1957, 'a twelve-part suite based on the works of William Shakespeare' - and all 4 keyTracks appear in its track listing. The one claim no evidence covers is the label field 'Columbia': MB returned only release-group searchCandidates with no release/label data (weak evidence; candidates do look like this album by 'Duke Ellington & His Orchestra'), and the wiki extract never names the original label. Nothing contradicts; only the label needs external confirmation.

album:the-clown.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim; wiki confirms Atlantic release, recording dates February 13 and March 12, 1957, and Jean Shepherd's improvised narration on the title track. All 4 keyTracks equal the embedded MB tracklist exactly. One month-level wrinkle: prose and wiki say 'released in September 1957' while the embedded MB release and release group say 1957-08; the two entity-matched sources disagree by one month, the displayed year field (1957) is right either way - recorded as a note, not flagged.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: It was first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra, and played a prominent part in the 1971 motion picture Play Misty for Me.
wrongClaims.problem: Sentence is lifted verbatim from the Erroll Garner biography page, where 'It' refers to Garner's composition 'Misty', not to this album; as written it misattributes the song's facts (1956 Mitch Miller recording, Play Misty for Me) to The Most Happy Piano. 'Misty' is not even on this album's MB tracklist.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: His instrumental ballad "Misty", his best-known composition, has become a jazz standard. It was first recorded in 1956 with Mitch Miller and his orchestra, and played a prominent part in the 1971 motion picture Play Misty for Me.

album:the-most-happy-piano.notes: MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms the album itself: Columbia CL 939, 1957, Erroll Garner, and all 5 keyTracks. First two prose sentences are fine ('cool jazz program' is editorial). The final sentence is a referent-swapped graft from the artist bio about the song 'Misty' - delete or rewrite it. Stored wikipediaUrl points to the Erroll Garner artist page, not an album article, so it is counted wrong_entity for this album item; if no album article exists, drop the link rather than keep the artist page.

album:thelonious-himself.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim and fully confirmed: 1957 Riverside release, Monk's fourth album for the label, solo piano except 'Monk's Mood' with John Coltrane (tenor sax) and Wilbur Ware (bass), second solo piano studio album and first made by an American label and distributed in the US. Embedded MB confirms Riverside RLP 12-235, 1957; all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist.

album:round-about-midnight.notes: Embedded MB confirms Columbia CL 949, released 1957-03-18, and all 5 keyTracks (punctuation-insensitive: 'Ah-Leu-Cha' = 'Ah‐Leu‐Cha', 'Tadd's Delight' = 'Tadd’s Delight'). Wikipedia (opensearch; no URL stored on the item) resolves to the correct album page and corroborates Columbia/March 1957 and both named tracks. Prose's 'A 1957 Columbia session' reads as loose dating of the release; actual recording sessions were Oct 1955 - Sept 1956 per wiki - phrasing, not flagged. Genre talk ('hard bop territory', 'cool jazz school') is editorial.

album:at-the-pershing.notes: Prose lead is verbatim from the stored wiki page and fully confirmed: January 16, 1958 at the Pershing Lounge of Chicago's Pershing Hotel, 43 tracks recorded / 8 selected, Argo LP-628, first release recorded for Argo, first live album. MB search (similarity 0.81) matched the fuller-titled 'Ahmad Jamal at the Pershing: But Not for Me' (Argo LP-628, 1958, Live) - same album; all 4 keyTracks on its tracklist. 'Influenced Miles Davis's conception of space in music' is supported in spirit by the wiki's 'influence on other jazz musicians such as Miles Davis'; the closing sentence is editorial.

album:blue-train.notes: MB search hit is a 2008 German 'Documents' 40-track compilation that merely shares the title (different release group, none of Blue Train's actual tracks) - discarded after entity-matching; it is not counter-evidence. All checking done against the entity-matched wiki article, which confirms every hard claim: Coltrane's only session as leader for Blue Note, recorded September 15, 1957 at Van Gelder's Hackensack NJ studio, the full sextet personnel (Lee Morgan, Curtis Fuller, Kenny Drew, Paul Chambers, Philly Joe Jones), January 1958 Blue Note release (year field 1958 ok), Francis Wolff cover photography, and 'Blue Train' (the single keyTrack) plus 'Moment's Notice' on the track listing. The 'would later evolve into Giant Steps' / 'Coltrane changes' sentences are critical interpretation, not flagged.

album:branching-out.notes: All album facts verified: embedded MB gives Riverside RLP 12-285, 1958, artists Nat Adderley / Johnny Griffin / The Three Sounds; wiki page (stored link resolves directly to the album article) confirms cornetist, Riverside, and the same lineup; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. The appended bio sentence - 'He was the younger brother of saxophonist Julian "Cannonball" Adderley, with whom he played for many years' - is covered by no pack evidence: unverifiable, not contradicted.

album:carmen-for-cool-ones.notes: Embedded MB (Decca DL 8738, 1958) and the stored wiki page both confirm; 'arranged and directed by cellist Fred Katz' is verbatim in the wiki lead and personnel (arranger, conductor, cello). All 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. The second sentence about McRae being 'considered one of the most influential jazz vocalists' with 'behind-the-beat phrasing' is reception/characterization in bio register - treated as editorial, not a checkable factual claim.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: labels: Verve, MG VS-6005-2

album:ella-fitzgerald-sings-the-irving-b.notes: Label field is wrong per both evidence sources: the embedded US MB release is on Verve (MG VS-6005-2) and Wikipedia says 'originally released on the Verve label in 1958: Verve MGV 4019-2'. 'His Master's Voice' may stem from a UK/EMI distribution pressing, but it is not the album's label. Everything else is verified: 1958, Paul Weston arranger/conductor, Irving Berlin songs, Songbook series, Grammy context on the wiki page; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist (RG first release 1958-07-04).

album:ellington-indigos-expanded-edition.notes: No MB evidence at all (search returned nothing). The stored wiki page is entity-matched and confirms 1958, Duke Ellington, and Columbia (original mono LP 'Columbia CL 1085'). But the wiki extract's track-listing sections are empty, so there is no complete tracklist: keyTracksVerdict no_evidence. Wiki prose does reference 'Mood Indigo', 'Autumn Leaves', and 'The Sky Fell Down' (mono-LP-only, omitted from CD) as album tracks - consistent with three of the five keyTracks, including the 'Mono Version' suffix on The Sky Fell Down. 'Solitude' and 'Where or When' (and the 'definitive readings of Solitude' claim) are uncheckable. The '(Expanded Edition)' title qualifier matches no evidence; the page documents multiple variant editions, so the exact edition/tracklist needs external confirmation.

album:going-for-myself.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim; both claims confirmed ('an album by Harry "Sweets" Edison and Lester Young, accompanied by Oscar Peterson'; 'one of Young's last studio recordings'). Embedded MB: Verve, RG first release 1958, credit Harry 'Sweets' Edison + Lester Young; all 5 keyTracks on the tracklist. Site's artist field shows Lester Young only while MB credits Edison first - the prose states the co-credit, so not flagged (same pattern as blues-and-vanilla). Detail a reviewer may care about: wiki personnel show Oscar Peterson only on the July 1957 Hollywood session; the Feb 1958 NY session has Lou Stein on piano - the wiki's own lead elides this, so ours is not flagged.

album:lady-in-satin.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim and fully confirmed: 1958 Columbia release, catalogue CL 1157 mono / CS 8048 stereo, penultimate completed album and last released in her lifetime, Last Recording recorded March 1959 and released just after her death, produced by Irving Townsend, engineered by Fred Plaut. Embedded MBID resolves to a 1980 Soviet Melodiya pressing of the Lady in Satin release group (RG first release 1958-06) with bilingual track titles; all 5 keyTracks match as substrings of the dual-language titles.

album:latin-kick.notes: Embedded MBID is authoritative and matches fully: Cal Tjader, Fantasy 3250, 1958; all 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist. The two prose track names also check out. wikiLinkVerdict: stored URL is the ARTIST page (Cal Tjader), not an album page — page is verified to be about the album's own artist, so this is likely an intentional fallback rather than a disambiguation collision; human should decide if acceptable. Prose quality: the final sentence ('was an American Latin Jazz musician, often described as the most successful non-Latino Latin musician.') is a subject-less fragment pasted verbatim from the artist's Wikipedia lede — factually correct per that page, but broken prose that needs a rewrite. 'Hard bop period' framing is era boilerplate / editorial, not flagged.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: It was released by Bethlehem Records in February 1959.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: It was released by Bethlehem Records in February 1959.
wrongClaims.problem: Contradicts the embedded MusicBrainz release date (authoritative per evidence rules) and is internally inconsistent with our own year field (1958).
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: date: 1958-06-24; releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: 1958-06-24

album:little-girl-blue.notes: Right entity throughout (artist, label, tracklist all match both sources; all 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist). The problem is the release date: our year field says 1958, our prose says February 1959, and the two evidence sources contradict each other (MB embedded: 1958-06-24; Wikipedia: February 1959, shortDescription '1959 debut studio album', with a detailed sourced narrative — announced in Billboard December 1958, released February 1959). At least one of our two stated dates is wrong whichever source is right. Wikipedia's account is detailed and cited while the MB date is a bare field, so the Feb 1959 / year=1959 reading looks more likely correct, but a human must pick the canonical year and make field and prose agree.

album:little-niles.notes: Embedded MBID confirms title, artist Randy Weston, United Artists (UAL-4011), 1958-11; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist, including 'Little Niles' and 'Pam's Waltz' named in the prose. No contradictions. Unverifiable substantive claims (no evidence available): Langston Hughes wrote the liner notes; album inspired by Weston's children Niles and Pamela who appear in the cover photo; all tracks are Weston originals; later reissued as part of a Blue Note compilation of the same title.

album:lou-takes-off.notes: Embedded MBID confirms title, Lou Donaldson, Blue Note BLP 1591, 1958; all 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly. No contradictions. Unverifiable substantive claims (no evidence available): recorded December 15, 1957; sextet personnel Donald Byrd, Curtis Fuller, Sonny Clark, Jamil Nasser, Art Taylor.

album:milestones.notes: Prose is the Wikipedia lede (minus the sextet parenthetical) and checks out: September 1958, Columbia, 'first great quintet'. All 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Caveat on label: the embedded MBID resolves to the UK Fontana pressing (TFL 5035, country GB), so MB's label field reads Fontana — this does NOT contradict ours.label='Columbia' because the entity-matched Wikipedia page states 'released in September 1958 by Columbia Records' (original US release). Not flagged; just note the embedded MBID points at a non-US pressing. Minor: prose says 'recorded with his first great quintet' without Wikipedia's clarification that it was augmented to a sextet with Cannonball Adderley — an omission, not a contradiction.

album:minor-move.notes: Prose is verbatim from the entity-matched Wikipedia page and fully consistent (recorded March 16, 1958; first leader date for Blue Note; unreleased until Japan 1980; 2000 CD; the Cuscuna 'Nutville' quote — note our prose truncates mid-quote, dropping the closing 'Curtis Fuller also confirms...' sentence and the closing quote mark). All 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist. Field caveats for editorial review (not errors under the evidence rules): the embedded MBID is a modern digital 'Expanded Edition' (country XW) on Capitol Records, which is where ours.label='Capitol Records' comes from — historically the album is a Blue Note recording first issued 1980 in Japan as Blue Note GXF 3072 per Wikipedia, so showing 'Capitol Records' is technically evidence-backed but historically misleading. Similarly year=1958 is the recording date (MB uses 1958-03-16 as release-group date) while Wikipedia's shortDescription calls it a '1980 studio album'. An archival-release labeling convention decision is needed; no claim contradicts its evidence source.

album:moanin.notes: MB search hit (similarity 0.95) is the original self-titled Blue Note BLP 4003 release whose release group is titled 'Moanin'' (1958) — solid entity match; year and label confirmed; all 4 keyTracks on the MB tracklist; prose mentions of 'Along Came Betty' and 'Blues March' also on tracklist. The opensearch Wikipedia page is 'Moaning (album)', a 2018 Sub Pop rock album — different entity, discarded (no stored URL, so no link penalty). Unverifiable substantive claims (no surviving evidence covers them): recorded October 30, 1958 at Rudy Van Gelder's studio; lineup Lee Morgan, Benny Golson, Bobby Timmons, Jymie Merritt; Golson composing credits; Timmons composing the title track. Remaining prose ('definitive hard bop album', gospel/call-and-response framing) is editorial.

album:somethin-else.notes: MB search resolved to a 2010 Not Now Music (FR) 12-track COMPILATION named 'Somethin' Else' (release-group firstReleaseDate 2010-04-15, secondaryTypes Compilation) — not the 1958 Blue Note album; discarded for fact-checking. All checking done against the entity-matched stored Wikipedia page ('1958 studio album by Cannonball Adderley'), which our prose quotes verbatim and which confirms: recorded March 9, 1958; released on Blue Note in August 1958; his only Blue Note album; Miles Davis on the session; Adderley then a member of Davis's group; recorded shortly after Milestones. Year and label fields consistent. keyTracks: no entity-matched MB tracklist, but the Wikipedia page's prose names all four ('Autumn Leaves', 'Love for Sale', the 'bluesy title track', 'One for Daddy-O') as album/session content — marked ok on that basis. Closing two sentences are editorial.

album:songs-for-distingu-lovers.notes: Embedded MBID confirms title, Billie Holiday, Verve MG V-8257, 1958; all 5 keyTracks on the MB tracklist (including the full 'One for My Baby (and One More for the Road)'). No contradictions. Unverifiable substantive claims (no evidence available): originally issued in both mono and stereo; recorded at Capitol Studios, Los Angeles, January 3-9, 1957; produced by Norman Granz.

album:sonny-rollins-and-the-big-brass.notes: Embedded MBID confirms title, Sonny Rollins, MetroJazz, 1958; all 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist. Stored Wikipedia page is the right album and confirms the prose verbatim: MetroJazz session, later reissued on Verve as 'Sonny Rollins/Brass - Sonny Rollins/Trio'; Rollins on tenor saxophone. The 'hard bop vocabulary of the hard bop period' sentence is clumsy era boilerplate (editorial), not a factual claim — note for prose cleanup only.

album:the-atomic-mr-basie.notes: Embedded MBID (original release titled 'Basie', Roulette R 52003, 1958-03; release group 'The Atomic Mr. Basie') and stored Wikipedia page both match. Prose is the Wikipedia lede verbatim and every claim checks: original title 'Basie', aka E=MC2, 1994 reissue 'The Complete Atomic Basie', 1958, Neal Hefti arrangements, 1001 Albums inclusion with the Will Fulford-Jones quote, number 411 in Larkin's All Time Top 1000 Albums (2000). All 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist.
