wrongClaims.ourClaim: Oscar Peterson and Roy Eldridge is an album by Oscar Peterson, on which he is accompanied by Roy Eldridge.
wrongClaims.problem: This sentence is the Wikipedia lede of a DIFFERENT album — the 1975 duo studio album 'Oscar Peterson and Roy Eldridge' — not the 1958 live album 'The Oscar Peterson Trio at Newport' that this item is titled after.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: shortDescription: "1975 studio album by Oscar Peterson & Roy Eldridge"

album:the-oscar-peterson-trio-at-newport.notes: Classic instance of the disambiguation disease, in mixed form. The embedded MBID matches the titled entity (The Oscar Peterson Trio at Newport, 1958, Verve UMV-2618 reissue catalog; credited to The Oscar Peterson Trio with Roy Eldridge, Sonny Stitt, Jo Jones) and all 5 keyTracks are on that MB tracklist — so title, year, and keyTracks belong to the real 1958 Newport album. But the prose lede and the stored Wikipedia URL belong to the 1975 'Oscar Peterson and Roy Eldridge' duo album, the artist field miscredits the album to 'Roy Eldridge' alone (MB primary credit is The Oscar Peterson Trio), and the label field 'Columbia' contradicts MB's Verve. Repair needs: rewrite prose for the 1958 Newport album, fix wikipediaUrl, set artist to The Oscar Peterson Trio (or Trio + guests), set label to Verve. The 'hard bop sensibility / cool jazz era' tail is editorial boilerplate.

album:abbey-is-blue.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0) confirms the album: Abbey Lincoln, Riverside RLP 1153, 1959; all 4 keyTracks on MB tracklist (Afro-Blue hyphen variant). No checkable claim contradicts evidence: the albumDNA's factual sentences are the Abbey Lincoln ARTIST Wikipedia lede verbatim (Anna Marie Wooldridge; vocalist/songwriter/actress; civil-rights activist from the 1960s) and verify against that page; the rest is editorial. Two issues for repair, neither a factual error: (1) the albumDNA contains zero album-specific content — it is artist-bio boilerplate pasted into an album slot and should be rewritten about the record itself; (2) wikiLinkVerdict: stored URL is the artist page, not an album page — verified to be the album's own artist, so likely an intentional fallback rather than a collision; human should decide if acceptable.

album:bags-flutes.notes: Embedded MBID confirms Atlantic SD 1294, 1959 release; stored Wikipedia page is the right album and confirms the prose verbatim ('recorded in 1957 and released on the Atlantic label'). All 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist. ours.year=1959 is the release year per authoritative MB; Wikipedia's shortDescription says '1957 studio album' (recording year) — not a contradiction of our field, but flag if the site wants consistent year semantics. MB credits the release jointly to Milt Jackson, Frank Wess, Bobby Jaspar while Wikipedia calls it an album by Milt Jackson — ours.artist='Milt Jackson' is supported by the entity-matched Wikipedia page, not flagged. 'Cool jazz movement/conversation' sentence is editorial era boilerplate.

album:ella-fitzgerald-sings-the-george-a.notes: Embedded MBID confirms Verve MG V-4025, 1959; stored Wikipedia page matches and the prose is its lede verbatim: box set, Nelson Riddle arrangements, produced by Norman Granz (Fitzgerald's manager and Verve founder), fifty-nine songs recorded over eight months in 1959. All 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist ('(I've Got) Beginner's Luck' matches MB's 'Beginner's Luck' under the substring rule and appears in full on the Wikipedia track list).

album:go-chambers.notes: Embedded MBID confirms Vee-Jay LP 1014, 1959; stored Wikipedia page matches and the prose is its lede verbatim: recorded in Chicago 1959, Vee-Jay; featured musicians Freddie Hubbard, Cannonball Adderley, Wynton Kelly, Philly Joe Jones (Wikipedia personnel confirms, with Jimmy Cobb on some tracks — the lede itself names Philly Joe, so no contradiction); 1997 double-CD reissue; 2000 Mosaic box 'The Complete Vee Jay Paul Chambers/Wynton Kelly Sessions 1959-61'. All 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: most tracks were first takes with minimal rehearsal
wrongClaims.problem: The 'first takes' half is contradicted: Wikipedia debunks the one-pass legend and says only one track ('Flamenco Sketches') yielded a complete take on the first attempt. The 'minimal rehearsal' half IS supported ('Davis... called for almost no rehearsal').
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: the persistent legend that the entire album was recorded in one pass is untrue. Only "Flamenco Sketches" yielded a complete take on

album:kind-of-blue.notes: Entity solid in both sources; year/label confirmed (Columbia, release group firstReleaseDate 1959-08-17; Wikipedia 'released on August 17, 1959, by Columbia Records'). Verified prose: 30th Street Studio, sessions March 2 and April 22, 1959; sextet Coltrane/Adderley/Evans with Wynton Kelly on 'Freddie Freeloader', Chambers, Cobb; Evans-Russell modal lineage; five million US copies (5x Platinum). The flagged claim's evidence quote is truncated mid-sentence in the pack extract ('...complete take on'), which is why confidence is moderate — the surrounding sentence explicitly debunks the first-take legend. 'Best-selling jazz album of all time' is not directly checkable from the evidence (only 5x Platinum is stated) — uncontradicted, left as a note. keyTracks: the MB search hit is an odd 3-track pressing (catalog GCB 60) listing only 'So What', 'Blue in Green', 'All Blues'; the other two keyTracks ('Freddie Freeloader', 'Flamenco Sketches') are explicitly confirmed as album tracks by the Wikipedia page, so keyTracksVerdict is ok.

fieldIssues.evidence: No such track on the album; the complete Wikipedia track listing has six tracks and none is titled 'Looking Ahead'
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: "Luyah! The Glorious Step" - 6:25 "African Violets" (Earl Griffith, Taylor) - 5:12 "Of What" - 8:18 "Wallering" - 5:22 "Toll" - 7:38 "Excursion on a Wobbly Rail" - 9:04

album:looking-ahead.notes: Stored Wikipedia page is the right album and the prose is its lede verbatim — all verified: second studio album, recorded for Contemporary in June 1958 (June 9, 1958), released the following year (1959), personnel Buell Neidlinger, Denis Charles, Earl Griffith. Year and label fields consistent. The one error: keyTrack 'Looking Ahead' does not exist on the album per the complete entity-matched Wikipedia track listing — the album title was apparently mistaken for a track title. Other three keyTracks (African Violets, Excursion on a Wobbly Rail, Toll) all confirmed. MB gave no confident release (searchCandidates only, similarity 0.67 — candidate 'Looking Ahead!' by The Cecil Taylor Quartet, 1959, is consistent but weak), hence mb entityMatch 'unsure'; the keyTracks check rests on the Wikipedia tracklist.

album:mingus-ah-um.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0) confirms Columbia CL 1370, 1959; stored Wikipedia page matches. Verified prose: released October 1959 by Columbia ('recorded in May 1959 and released that October'); first album for Columbia; the Latin-declension title explanation (verbatim); Grammy Hall of Fame 2013; ranked 380 on Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Albums (2020 edition — our prose omits the edition, not a contradiction). All 4 keyTracks on MB tracklist. Prose-corruption note for repair: 'The cover features a painting by S.' is truncated mid-name — Wikipedia reads 'a painting by S. Neil Fujita'. Not factually contradicting (the initial is right), but the sentence is broken and should be completed. Final two sentences are editorial.

album:nina-at-town-hall.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0) confirms Colpix CP 409, 1959, Live; stored Wikipedia page matches and the prose is its lede verbatim: first live album, released December 1959, third album of that year, second for Colpix, based on the September 12, 1959 Town Hall (New York) performance, with three tracks replaced by studio versions cut the following month. All 4 keyTracks on MB tracklist. Closing sentence is editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: artists: ["Dizzy Gillespie", "Sonny Stitt", "Sonny Rollins"]

album:sonny-side-up.notes: Right entity — title, year 1959, and all 4 keyTracks confirmed by the authoritative embedded MBID; stored Wikipedia page matches ('1959 album by Dizzy Gillespie, Sonny Stitt and Sonny Rollins') and the prose is its lede verbatim (recorded December 1957 in NYC; released 1959 on Norman Granz's newly launched Verve label; rhythm section Ray Bryant, Tommy Bryant, Charlie Persip). Two field errors: (1) label 'Columbia Records' contradicts MB (Verve MG V-8262), the Wikipedia page ('released in 1959 on... Verve label'), and our own prose; (2) artist credited solely to 'Sonny Rollins' while both sources credit Dizzy Gillespie (first-listed leader), Sonny Stitt, and Sonny Rollins jointly. Repair: label -> Verve; artist -> Dizzy Gillespie / Sonny Stitt / Sonny Rollins (or at minimum lead with Gillespie).

album:the-shape-of-jazz-to-come.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0) confirms Ornette Coleman, Atlantic 1317, 1959 (release group 1959-10); all 4 keyTracks on the MB tracklist. No contradictions. Unverifiable substantive claims (no Wikipedia evidence; MB carries no personnel/recording detail): recorded May 1959 at Radio Recorders in Hollywood; quartet personnel Don Cherry (cornet), Charlie Haden (bass), Billy Higgins (drums); pianoless-quartet framing; 'harmolodic' terminology. Influence/legacy sentences are editorial.

album:thelonious-alone-in-san-francisco.notes: Embedded MBID confirms Riverside RLP 12-312, 1959; stored Wikipedia page matches and confirms the factual prose verbatim ('third solo album, recorded in 1959'). All 5 keyTracks on MB tracklist. Editorial caveat for prose review (not a factual claim): describing a solo-piano record as 'a 1959 hard bop setting... of the hard bop era' is era-bucket boilerplate that fits this album poorly — Wikipedia documents an unaccompanied solo session at Fugazi Hall, October 21-22, 1959.

album:time-out.notes: Entity solid: MB search (0.91) confirms The Dave Brubeck Quartet, Columbia, 1959, with all 4 keyTracks on the tracklist; stored Wikipedia page matches. Verified: unusual time signatures; 9/8 'Blue Rondo a la Turk'; 5/4 'Take Five' composed by Desmond (the only track not by Brubeck); cool/West Coast character ('a subtle fusion of cool and West Coast influences'); Joe Morello on drums (Take Five 'was supposed to be a Joe Morello drum solo'); first jazz album to sell one million copies. Unverifiable substantive claims, no contradictions: Eugene Wright on bass (not in the extract); album cover by Neil Fujita (not mentioned on this page — the pack's Mingus Ah Um page credits Fujita for THAT cover only, not usable here); recording year 1959 as such (page gives studio and release year, recording date not explicit in extract).

album:blues-and-roots.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0) confirms Charles Mingus, Atlantic 1305, released 1960-04-04; stored Wikipedia page matches and confirms the prose verbatim: recorded 1959 (February 4, 1959 per the page), released on Atlantic in 1960, reissued on CD by both Atlantic and Rhino. All 4 keyTracks on both MB and Wikipedia tracklists. Closing two sentences are editorial.

album:booker-little.notes: Embedded-MBID MB release (Time Records 52011, 1960) and the stored Wikipedia album page both match: title, sole artist credit Booker Little, year, label. All five keyTracks are on the MB tracklist. Prose first sentence is verbatim the Wikipedia lede; 'hard bop output from 1960' is editorial framing consistent with the April/August 1960 session dates in the wiki personnel section.

fieldIssues.evidence: Cannonball Adderley, Ray Brown, Wes Montgomery (MB embedded-MBID artist credit, authoritative)
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: artists: ["Cannonball Adderley", "Ray Brown", "Wes Montgomery"]

album:cannonball-adderley-and-the-poll-w.notes: Embedded-MBID release confirms title/year/label and all five keyTracks. The artist field names only Wes Montgomery, but the authoritative credit lists Cannonball Adderley first (with Ray Brown and Montgomery), and our own prose says 'an album by jazz saxophonist Julian "Cannonball" Adderley' -- the field contradicts both. Possibly a deliberate catalog filing under Montgomery (artist-centric site); human should decide how to repair. Personnel claims Victor Feldman and Louis Hayes, and the 'cool jazz' framing, are unverifiable from pack evidence (no personnel list, no wiki page) but not contradicted.

album:charles-mingus-presents-charles-mi.notes: Prose is verbatim the Wikipedia lede (Candid, recorded October 1960, released January 1961, Dolphy/Curson/Richmond quartet, Showplace residency). All four keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly. Year field 1960 matches the MB release-group first release date (1960) and the recording year, while prose and Wikipedia say released January 1961 -- the two evidence sources disagree with each other; not flagged. The MB release fetched is a 1987 CD reissue of the same release group.

album:free-form-harriott.notes: Entity solid: MB search match (Joe Harriott Quintet, Jazzland JLP 949S) and stored wiki page agree. KeyTrack 'Free Form' is on neither tracklist -- both MB and Wikipedia list the same eight tracks (Formation, Coda, Abstract, Impression, Parallel, Straight Lines, Calypso, Tempo); 'Free Form' is the album title, not a track, possibly a corruption of 'Formation'. Other three keyTracks match. Year field 1960 is the recording year (wiki: 'recorded in England in 1960'); first release was 1961 per MB and the wiki short description -- noted, not separately flagged. Prose claims (Jamaican-born alto saxophonist, parallel to Ornette Coleman) are consistent with the wiki page; 'independently developed free jazz at the same time' is editorial-historical, supported in spirit by AllMusic's 'Comparable to Ornette Coleman's recordings of the period'.

album:giant-steps.notes: Prose is near-verbatim Wikipedia (February 1960 Atlantic release, National Recording Registry 2004, gold record 2018 / 500,000 copies). 'First album as sole leader' is consistent with wiki ('first album as leader', with a prior January 1959 Atlantic date led by Milt Jackson). MB date 1960-01 vs wiki February 1960 is source-level wobble; ours follows wiki. All four keyTracks on the MB tracklist. 'Sheets of sound' and harmonic-challenge statements are supported by the wiki text.

album:gillespiana.notes: MB search match (similarity 1.0) confirms title/artist/year/label (Verve V6-8394, 1960) and all five keyTracks exactly. The substantive claim that Lalo Schifrin wrote the compositions and arrangements cannot be checked against pack evidence (no Wikipedia page; MB carries no composer credits). 'Adding to the recorded documentation of the free jazz era' is odd genre framing for a 1960 Gillespie big-band suite but is editorial, not a checkable claim.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: the Jazz Epistles recorded the first album by a black South African band, Jazz Epistle, Verse 1

album:township-jive.notes: No MB evidence (search returned nothing). The stored wiki link is the BAND article (The Jazz Epistles), not an album article -- not a disambiguation collision, but strictly not a page about the album; flagged unsure. Most of the prose is verbatim from that article and matches (members incl. Dollar Brand/Moeketsi/Gwangwa/Masekela/Gertze/Mabuza-or-Ntshoko, Jazz at the Odin in Sophiatown, recording shortly before the 1960 Sharpeville massacre). The article names the album 'Jazz Epistle, Verse 1' (singular Epistle), contradicting our title form 'Jazz Epistles: Verse 1' -- single casual mention, so confidence is moderate. Unverifiable from pack: Gallo label, 1960 release year (article says recorded 1959, gives no release date), 'the only album' claim, and all four keyTracks (no tracklist in any evidence).

wrongClaims.ourClaim: I've Got the World on a String is a 1960 album by Louis Armstrong, arranged by Russell Garcia. It was recorded on the same day as Armstrong's 1958 album Louis Under the Stars; the previous day he had finished recording Ella and Louis Again with Ella Fitzgerald.
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA describes a different Armstrong album (I've Got the World on a String, the Russell Garcia orchestral date) -- not Louie and the Dukes of Dixieland, the Dukes of Dixieland collaboration this item actually is
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Louie and the Dukes of Dixieland"; artists: ["Louis Armstrong", "The Dukes of Dixieland"]; releaseGroup firstReleaseDate: "1960"

album:louie-and-the-dukes-of-dixieland.notes: Classic disambiguation disease. Embedded-MBID MB release confirms the real album (Armstrong with The Dukes of Dixieland, RG first release 1960) and all five keyTracks match its tracklist, so title/artist/year/keyTracks fields are fine. But the prose and the stored Wikipedia link both belong to I've Got the World on a String (whose wiki personnel shows a Russell Garcia orchestra, no Dukes of Dixieland). Label field 'Audio Fidelity' is unverifiable from the pack (MB shows only a High Definition Tape Transfers reissue) -- plausible for the 1960 original but needs sourcing during repair. Prose and wikipediaUrl both need replacement.

album:mingus-dynasty.notes: Prose is verbatim Wikipedia (recorded 1959, released May 1960 on Columbia, Grammy Hall of Fame 1999, title alluding to Mingus's partially Chinese ancestry). MB embedded MBID confirms Columbia CS 8236, 1960. All four keyTracks match after stripping the '(unedited version)' suffixes, which are themselves grounded in the wiki note that tracks 1, 3, 4 and 5 were later released unedited.

album:portrait-in-jazz.notes: Prose first sentences are verbatim Wikipedia (fifth studio album, first of two studio albums with LaFaro and Motian, mono/stereo editions with different 'Autumn Leaves' takes). MB search match (similarity 1.0) confirms Riverside RLP 12-315, 1960; all four keyTracks on the tracklist. 'Established the template for modern jazz piano trio playing' is editorial, consistent with the wiki 'blueprint' quote.

album:sketches-of-spain.notes: Prose is verbatim the Wikipedia lede (released July 18, 1960 by Columbia; recorded November 1959-March 1960 at the 30th Street Studio; Rodrigo's Concierto de Aranjuez 1939; de Falla's El amor brujo 1914-1915; third stream). MB embedded MBID confirms Columbia CL 1480; MB dates the release 1960-08 vs wiki's July 18, 1960 -- source wobble only, ours follows wiki. All five keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly.

fieldIssues.evidence: Red Garland (leader); MB credit: The Red Garland Quintet, John Coltrane, Donald Byrd
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Soul Junction is an album by the jazz pianist and composer Red Garland.

album:soul-junction.notes: This is Red Garland's album: wiki lede names Garland as leader and MB embedded-MBID credit lists The Red Garland Quintet first, with Coltrane and Byrd as featured names. Filing it under artist 'Donald Byrd' contradicts the leader credit, though Byrd is genuinely in the credit and on the date -- possibly a deliberate artist-centric catalog choice; flag for human. Prose itself never claims Byrd led ('features trumpeter Donald Byrd' is accurate). Title/year/label/keyTracks all check (Prestige P-7181, RG first release 1960; wiki: recorded November 15, 1957, released 1960 -- 'a hard bop outing from 1960' reads as release year). The Byrd-influenced-Herbie-Hancock claim is unverifiable from pack evidence. Note: the same album appears again in this pack as soul-junction-coltrane.

fieldIssues.evidence: Red Garland (leader); MB credit: The Red Garland Quintet, John Coltrane, Donald Byrd
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Soul Junction is an album by the jazz pianist and composer Red Garland.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Lush Life is a studio album by the jazz musician John Coltrane. It was released in early 1961 by Prestige Records.
wrongClaims.problem: The albumDNA describes a different album entirely -- Coltrane's Lush Life -- while this item is Soul Junction
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Soul Junction is an album by the jazz pianist and composer Red Garland. It was released in 1960 through Prestige Records.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: It was assembled from previously unissued tracks from three recording sessions at Van Gelder Studio in Hackensack, New Jersey in 1957 and 1958.
wrongClaims.problem: Soul Junction comes from a single session, November 15, 1957, per the entity-matched wiki page
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: It features tracks recorded on November 15, 1957, the same day the pieces for All Mornin' Long were recorded, with the same lineup.

album:soul-junction-coltrane.notes: Title, year, label and all five keyTracks match the real Soul Junction (MB embedded MBID, Prestige P-7181, RG first release 1960), and the stored wiki link points to the correct Soul Junction article -- but the prose was pasted from Coltrane's Lush Life (early-1961 release assembled from 1957-58 sessions). Artist field 'John Coltrane' also misstates the leader (Red Garland; Coltrane is a credited sideman). Duplicate entry of the same album as pack item soul-junction, there filed under Donald Byrd. Prose needs replacement; artist filing and the duplicate need a human decision.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Holiday Soul is an album of Christmas music by the American jazz pianist Bobby Timmons, recorded in 1964 and released on the Prestige label.
wrongClaims.problem: The albumDNA describes a different Timmons album (Holiday Soul, 1964 Christmas record on Prestige); this item is Soul Time, a 1960 Riverside album
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Soul Time"; date: "1960-11"; labels: [{"name": "Riverside", "catalogNumber": "RS 9334"}]

album:soul-time.notes: MB embedded MBID confirms Soul Time (Bobby Timmons, Riverside RS 9334, November 1960) and all five keyTracks are on its tracklist, so the fields are right. But the prose and the stored Wikipedia link both belong to Holiday Soul (1964, Prestige) -- the disambiguation disease. The 'cool jazz movement' sentence is template filler inappropriate for either record, but editorial. Prose and wikipediaUrl both need replacement.

album:the-centaur-and-the-phoenix.notes: MB search match (similarity 1.0; Riverside RLP 337, 1960) confirms title/artist/year/label. Its tracklist is Revelation, Apathy, Ev'ry Day (I Fall in Love), The Centaur and the Phoenix, Iqbal, Summer Song, The Philanthropist -- keyTracks 'The Plum Blossom' and 'First Gymnopedie' are not on it. 'The Plum Blossom' is the opening track of Lateef's Eastern Sounds per that item's evidence in this same pack, so this looks like cross-album contamination. Prose claims about oboe/flute/tenor plus exotic instruments are unverifiable (no personnel in evidence) but not contradicted; the world-jazz pioneer framing is editorial.

album:this-here-is-bobby-timmons.notes: MB embedded MBID confirms title/artist/label and November 1960 release (Riverside RLP 12-317); all five keyTracks are on the tracklist. No contradictions. Unverifiable from pack evidence: 'his first album as sole leader' and the claim that Jenkins, Jordan and Timmons (1957) was a co-led date with John Jenkins and Clifford Jordan (no Wikipedia page available; MB carries no such context).

album:we-insist.notes: Prose is verbatim Wikipedia (released January 1961 through Candid; suite begun 1959 with lyricist Oscar Brown for the planned 1963 Emancipation Proclamation centennial; sit-in cover; Penguin Guide crown plus Core Collection). All four keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Year field 1960 vs first release January 1961 (MB and wiki body): Wikipedia's own short description calls it a '1960 studio album' (recorded 1960), so the field is evidence-supported and not flagged. 'One of the first jazz albums to directly address civil rights' is editorial-historical, consistent with the evidence.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Trumpeter Don Ellis's hard bop sensibility is on display in this 1961 session
wrongClaims.problem: Calls the session 1961; the recording was made in 1960 (1961 is the release year). The prose's own first sentence states this correctly, so the appended template sentence contradicts both the evidence and itself
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: How Time Passes is the debut album by trumpeter Don Ellis recorded in 1960 and released on the Candid label.

album:how-time-passes.notes: Entity solid: MB embedded MBID (Candid CJS 9004, 1961) and stored wiki page agree; all five keyTracks match; first prose sentence is verbatim the wiki lede; year field 1961 matches the release. The 'hard bop sensibility ... document of the hard bop era' framing sits oddly against the evidence (Yanow: 'stretching the boundaries of bop-based jazz and experimenting a bit with time and tempo') but genre labels are editorial -- only the '1961 session' date claim is flagged.

album:live-at-the-five-spot.notes: No MB evidence; judged against the stored Wikipedia article, which covers both Five Spot volumes and matches the prose nearly verbatim (recorded the night of 16 July 1961 at the end of the two-week residency with Booker Little; only night recorded; engineer Rudy Van Gelder; Vol. 1 released December 1961 by Prestige). Item is Vol. 1, but keyTrack 'Aggression' is on Volume two per the wiki tracklists (Volume one: Fire Waltz, Bee Vamp, The Prophet; Volume two: Aggression, Like Someone in Love, ...). KeyTracks matching used the wiki tracklist since MB returned nothing. The prose's pair-of-albums framing is the wiki lede -- acceptable context, though slightly odd under a Vol. 1-only item; year and label fields are correct for Vol. 1.

album:buhaina.notes: Prose is verbatim Wikipedia (recorded November 28 and December 18, 1961 at Van Gelder Studio; produced by Alfred Lion; released by Blue Note in July 1963 after Blakey went to Riverside in late 1962). All four keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Year field 1961 is the recording year; first release is 1962 per MB but July 1963 per Wikipedia -- the two evidence sources contradict each other on release year, and ours matches the documented recording year, so noted for human review rather than flagged as error. Shorter-stature remark is editorial.

album:eastern-sounds.notes: Prose is verbatim Wikipedia (released April 1962 through Prestige's Moodsville imprint; recorded September 1961; quartet with Barry Harris; xun on the opening track). All four keyTracks on the MB tracklist (Love Theme from 'Spartacus' matches with punctuation differences). Year field 1961 matches the recording year and the MB release-group date (1961-09-05, which appears to be the session date); wiki gives the release as April 1962 -- noted, not flagged. Label field Moodsville is confirmed by wiki; the MB release fetched is a modern Craft Recordings reissue.

album:blue-in-green.notes: Content is Explorations and checks out: prose verbatim Wikipedia (second and final studio album with the LaFaro/Motian trio, Riverside, 1961); MB search match similarity 1.0 (RLP-351, 1961); all four keyTracks on the tracklist. Trio-interplay sentences are editorial. One cosmetic oddity for maintainers: the item id is 'blue-in-green' (a tune title) and does not reflect the album title -- harmless but confusing.

album:face-to-face.notes: Entity solid: MB search match similarity 1.0 (Blue Note BLP 4068, 1961) and stored wiki page agree; wiki confirms debut album and the personnel named in our prose (Grant Green guitar, Fred Jackson tenor sax, plus Ben Dixon). KeyTrack 'Swingin' for Bumsy' is on neither the MB nor the wiki tracklist (both open with 'Swingin' at Sugar Ray's', and the CD bonus takes are alternates of existing tracks) -- likely a corrupted or confabulated title. 'Somethin'' matched 'Somethin' Strange' as a substring. Unverifiable but not contradicted: forename 'Roosevelt' and the 'only a handful of albums' career claim.

album:free-jazz.notes: No MB evidence. Stored wiki page matches the album and the core prose is verbatim from it (released September 1961 by Atlantic; recorded December 21, 1960 at A&R Studios; sole outtake 'First Take' later on the 1971 compilation Twins). No tracklist appears in the extract, but wiki confirms the title track was 'split into two sections for each side of the LP', consistent with the Part 1/Part 2 keyTracks -- still no_evidence by the letter of the rule. Unverifiable: 'The album is named after the then-nascent free jazz movement' (background knowledge, not citable, suggests the naming influence is usually described in the opposite direction -- the movement taking its name partly from this album; pack evidence is silent) and the claim of influence extending to rock, punk, and experimental music (wiki only documents influence on later free jazz recordings such as Ascension and Machine Gun).

fieldIssues.evidence: Pim Jacobs, Rita Reys, Kenny Clarke (MB embedded-MBID artist credit, authoritative)
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: artists: ["Pim Jacobs", "Rita Reys", "Kenny Clarke"]

wrongClaims.ourClaim: drummer Kenny Clarke leads a hard bop session recorded for Philips in 1961
wrongClaims.problem: Presents Clarke as the leader; the authoritative artist credit lists Pim Jacobs first and Rita Reys second, with Clarke third -- nothing in the evidence supports Clarke as leader
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: artists: ["Pim Jacobs", "Rita Reys", "Kenny Clarke"]

album:jazz-pictures-at-an-exibition.notes: Embedded-MBID release is the same album as ours (same sic spelling 'Exibition', Philips, release group first release 1961; the fetched pressing is a 1989 Japan reissue) and all five keyTracks match the tracklist. But the credit makes this a Pim Jacobs / Rita Reys date with Clarke as the third-billed name; our artist field and 'Clarke leads' sentence overstate his role. Background knowledge, not citable: Rita Reys was a vocalist and Pim Jacobs a pianist, which would make 'hard bop session' framing doubly questionable -- pack evidence cannot confirm, so genre framing stays unflagged. Year and label fields are consistent with the evidence.

album:midnight-special.notes: MB embedded release (Blue Note BST 84078, 1961, Jimmy Smith) and stored wiki page both match our album. Prose mirrors the wiki lead: recorded 1960, released on Blue Note, same session as Back at the Chicken Shack (1960) - all confirmed. All 5 keyTracks on the MB/wiki tracklist.

album:my-favorite-things.notes: MB embedded (Atlantic SD 1361, 1961) and wiki page match. Prose claims all confirmed by the wiki extract: released March 1961 on Atlantic, first album with Coltrane on soprano saxophone, edited title track a 1961 radio hit, major commercial success. Ours says 'first released album' where wiki says 'first album' - consistent. All 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist.

album:out-front.notes: MB embedded (Candid CJM 8027, 1961-10) and wiki page match. Prose first sentence is the wiki lead verbatim; 'We Speak' and 'Strength and Sanity' confirmed on the tracklist; 'hard bop context' / 'interpretive range' are editorial. All 5 keyTracks on the 7-track album.

album:percussion-bitter-sweet.notes: MB embedded confirms Max Roach, 1961, impulse! AS-8, and all 5 keyTracks. No wikipedia evidence (no stored URL; opensearch returned nothing). The Booker Little claims - that this was his penultimate recording and that he died of uremia in early October 1961 - cannot be checked against pack evidence (no personnel data anywhere in the item). Right entity, no contradictions.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Captures clarinetist Phil Woods during the hard bop period
wrongClaims.problem: Wrong instrument for Woods on this album: the wiki lead calls him a saxophonist and the personnel list credits him on alto saxophone only; our own first sentence also says 'saxophonist Phil Woods'
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Rights of Swing is an album led by saxophonist Phil Woods ... Personnel: Phil Woods - alto saxophone

album:rights-of-swing.notes: Everything else checks out: MB embedded (Candid CJS 9016, 1961) and wiki match, and all 5 keyTracks match exactly. 'Clarinetist' looks like a template fill-in error. Woods may have played clarinet elsewhere in his career (background knowledge, not pack evidence), but the evidence for this album shows alto saxophone only.

wrongClaims.problem: Leader is Oliver Nelson, not Dolphy (MB bills 'Oliver Nelson Sextet' first, with Dolphy and Richard Williams as featured artists; wiki personnel has Nelson on tenor/alto, Dolphy as sideman on bass clarinet/alto); original label was New Jazz, not Prestige
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Screamin' the Blues is an album by American saxophonist Oliver Nelson, originally released in 1961 on New Jazz Records.

album:screamin-the-blues.notes: Same album, wrong leader: prose and the artist field attribute the album to Eric Dolphy, but both sources show it as an Oliver Nelson date with Dolphy as sideman. MB embedded doc is the 1991 OJC reissue (OJCCD-080-2) crediting Oliver Nelson Sextet / Eric Dolphy / Richard Williams; release-group first release 1961-01, so year 1961 is fine. All 5 keyTracks match the tracklist. On label: New Jazz was a Prestige-family imprint (the Far Cry wiki lead in this same pack calls New Jazz 'a subsidiary of the Prestige label'), but the dataset elsewhere uses 'New Jazz' as its own label value (the Where? item), so this field should read New Jazz. album:shirley-scott-trio.notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Prestige 7240, 1961) and wiki agree on a six-track album: Señor Blues, Moon Ray/Moonray, Sister Sadie, Doodlin', The Preacher, Strollin'. 'Nica's Dream' is not on it (it is a well-known Horace Silver composition - background knowledge, which may explain the slip - but it is absent from both tracklists). The other 3 keyTracks match. Year 1961 matches MB; wiki shortDescription says '1962 studio album' while its extract says recorded 1961 - sources differ on release year, ours sides with MB. Also note the item id 'shirley-scott-trio' vs title 'Shirley Scott Plays Horace Silver'.

album:shirleys-sounds.notes: MB embedded (Prestige PRLP 7195, 1961) and wiki match. Prose mirrors the wiki lead: recorded mainly 1958 for Prestige, not released until 1961 as PRLP 7195 - catalog number confirmed by MB too. All 5 keyTracks on the 8-track album. The era-template sentence ('reconnecting jazz with blues and gospel...') is editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: "African Lady" (Langston Hughes, Randy Weston) — 3:46

album:straight-ahead.notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Candid CJM 8015, 1961) and wiki agree; prose lead matches the wiki verbatim. KeyTrack 'African Woman' should be 'African Lady' per both the MB and wiki tracklists; the other three keyTracks match. 'Part of the cultural moment that included We Insist!' is a contextual claim not checkable against pack evidence (We Insist! appears nowhere in the evidence), and the closing sentence is editorial.

album:sunday-at-the-village-vanguard.notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Riverside RLP 376, 1961, Live release-group) and wiki match. The 'redefined the piano trio format' material is editorial.

album:the-honeydripper.notes: Stored wiki page is about our album and confirms 1961, Prestige, and Grant Green on guitar (also Jimmy Forrest on tenor). MB found only a release-group candidate ('Brother' Jack McDuff, 1961) with no release/tracklist - weak per spec - so the tracklist check rests on the wiki's complete six-track listing, which lacks 'Whistle While You Work' and 'He's a Real Gone Guy'. Those two likely belong to another McDuff record, not determinable from pack evidence. The Jimmy Smith comparison, 'signature album', and soul-jazz/jazz-funk influence claims are editorial, not flagged.

album:thelonious-monk-with-john-coltrane.notes: MB embedded (Jazzland JLP 946S, 1961) and wiki match. Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: 1961 album on Jazzland (a Riverside subsidiary), material recorded four years earlier when Monk worked extensively with Coltrane - the 1957 session dates in the extract bear out 'four years earlier'. album:together.notes: Prose lead is the wiki text verbatim and checks out (co-led by Philly Joe Jones and Elvin Jones, Atlantic, recorded 1961, with Mitchell/Fuller/Mobley/Kelly/Chambers). But the ENTIRE keyTracks list is wrong: the authoritative embedded-MBID tracklist (Le Roi, Beau-Ty, Brown Sugar) and the identical wiki tracklist show a three-track album; none of our six titles is on it - they look imported from a different Elvin Jones album, not identifiable from pack evidence. The trailing sentence 'His brothers Hank and Thad were also celebrated jazz musicians...' is a dangling artist-bio fragment about Elvin Jones, unverifiable here. Release year: ours 1961 = MB embedded 1961; wiki shortDescription says '1964 studio album' - sources conflict, noted for the repair pass.

album:waltz-for-debby.notes: Classic disambiguation-collision case (the known disease). The stored URL resolves to the 'Waltz for Debby' disambiguation page, and the prose is that page's text plus two appended editorial sentences; it never describes the album itself. The catalog entry is salvageable: the album is MB-verified (Bill Evans Trio, Riverside RLP-9399, Live, first release 1962) and all 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. Repairs: rewrite the prose about the 1962 live album, set year to 1962 (ours says 1961; MB and the disambig entry both say 1962 - 1961 is the recording year per background knowledge, not citable), and repoint the wiki link to the album article ('Waltz for Debby (1962 album)' per the disambiguation listing).

album:where.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: Ron Carter's debut, recorded 1961 at Van Gelder Studio, New Jazz label, 'Some reissues of the album appear under Eric Dolphy's name.' MB embedded (New Jazz NJ/NJLP 8265, 1961) co-credits Ron Carter / Eric Dolphy / Mal Waldron, and all 5 keyTracks match. The artist field says only 'Eric Dolphy' even though the album is canonically Carter's - defensible given the wiki's reissue-crediting note and the MB co-credit, so not flagged as a contradiction, but worth an editorial look: our own prose calls it Carter's debut while the site files it under Dolphy.

album:modern-sounds.notes: Stored wiki page matches: 1962 Carmen McRae tribute to Billie Holiday on Columbia; the prose claims are confirmed (including 'died two years previously', which mirrors the wiki text). MB returned no candidates at all, so the tracklist check rests on the wiki's complete 14-track listing: 'Good Morning Heartache' is absent; Lover Man, God Bless the Child, and Them There Eyes match. Separate flag for the repair pass: the item id 'modern-sounds' does not correspond to this album's title - looks like a leftover id from a different record; check the catalog row.

album:dear-lonely-hearts.notes: MB embedded confirms Nat King Cole, 1962, Capitol ST-1838, and all 5 keyTracks. No wikipedia evidence (no stored URL; opensearch empty). Unverifiable substantive claims: arranged by Belford Hendricks; LP peaked at number 24 on the Billboard albums chart; title track at number two for two weeks on Easy Listening and number 13 on the Hot 100. No contradictions found.

album:far-cry.notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; New Jazz NJ 8270, 1962) and wiki match. Prose mirrors the wiki lead: third album, released 1962 on New Jazz, quintet co-led with Booker Little, Byard/Carter/Haynes returning, recorded the same day Dolphy took part in Ornette Coleman's Free Jazz session, the day after Jazz Abstractions - all confirmed. album:go.notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Blue Note BLP 4112, 1962) and wiki match. Prose mirrors the wiki page: Sonny Clark/Butch Warren/Billy Higgins, recorded August 27 1962, released the same year on Blue Note, the Ira Gitler liner-note quote, Rudy Van Gelder at Englewood Cliffs. album:hub-tones.notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Blue Note BLP 4115, 1962) and wiki match. Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: recorded October 10 1962, BLP 4115/BST 84115, Spaulding/Hancock/Workman/Jarvis, Reid Miles cover design, Francis Wolff photography. All 4 keyTracks match. One discrepancy: wiki shortDescription says '1963 studio album' while MB says first release 1962 (= our year field); sources conflict on release year and ours sides with MB - noted for human review, not flagged as error.

album:let-freedom-ring.notes: MB match is confident (search, artist similarity 1.0, Blue Note BST 84106) and says first release April 1963, contradicting our year 1962; all 4 keyTracks match its tracklist, so it is clearly the same album. 1962 may be the recording year, but the dataset elsewhere uses release year (e.g. Shirley's Sounds: recorded 1958, year 1961).
