fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: It was recorded on September 17, 1962, and released in February 1963 by United Artists Jazz.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: released in February 1963 by United Artists Jazz
wrongClaims.problem: Contradicts the embedded-MBID MB release date, which the pipeline treats as authoritative for date; note this prose sentence is itself verbatim from the entity-matched wiki page, so the two evidence sources conflict with each other
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: date: "1962"; releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1962" (United Artists Jazz UAJS 15017)

album:money-jungle-mingus.notes: Right album; the evidence sources split on release date (MB embedded: 1962; wiki: recorded September 17 1962, released February 1963). Ours is internally inconsistent - year field 1962 but prose says 'released in February 1963' - so one of the two is wrong whichever source is right; repair must reconcile (fieldIssues documents year-vs-wiki, wrongClaims documents prose-vs-MB). Everything else checks: Ellington/Mingus/Roach trio, United Artists Jazz, all 5 keyTracks on both tracklists, stored wiki link correct. Also: the artist field lists only Charles Mingus while both sources bill Duke Ellington first (with Mingus and Roach co-credited; MB has 'Charlie Mingus'); the prose attributes the album correctly to Ellington, so this is selective credit of a co-billed artist rather than a contradiction - flag for editorial review.

album:nancy-wilson-cannonball-adderley.notes: MB embedded confirms the album (Nancy Wilson / Cannonball Adderley, Capitol ST 1657, 1962-09) and all 5 keyTracks. The stored wikipedia link is wrong: it points to "Somethin' Else (Cannonball Adderley album)", the 1958 Blue Note record with Miles Davis - a different album entirely. Discarded for fact-checking; the site is showing users a wrong link - fix to the Nancy Wilson/Cannonball Adderley album article. With only MB usable, several substantive prose claims are unverifiable: Nat Adderley on cornet and Joe Zawinul on piano, Wilson's 'easy-going third horn' quote, and the claim it reached the pop charts. No contradictions found. Minor: artist field lists only Cannonball Adderley while MB co-credits Nancy Wilson (the title itself names both).

album:ready-for-freddie.notes: MB embedded (Blue Note BST 84085, 1962-05) and wiki match. Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: released on Blue Note in 1962 as BLP 4085/BST 84085, 2003 CD remaster with two alternate takes, performances by Hubbard, Bernard McKinney, Wayne Shorter, McCoy Tyner, Art Davis and Elvin Jones - all confirmed. All 5 keyTracks match.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: featuring performances recorded and released on the Blue Note label in 1961
wrongClaims.problem: Release year contradicts MB: the release-group's first release is 1962 (which also matches our own year field, 1962); recording in 1961 may well be right, but 'released ... in 1961' conflicts with the authoritative embedded-MBID evidence
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1962"

album:stop-and-listen.notes: MB embedded doc is a 1999 Japanese reissue (Blue Note TOCJ-9143) of our album; its release-group gives first release 1962, agreeing with our year field but contradicting the prose's 'recorded and released... in 1961' - the prose contradicts our own year field too. All 5 keyTracks are on the reissue tracklist. Unverifiable from pack: 'second album', the '1941 Harry Warren song' attribution for 'At Last', and the Etta James R&B/Hot 100 chart-timing claim. No wikipedia evidence (no stored URL; opensearch empty).

wrongClaims.ourClaim: The bluesy track "Watermelon Man" made it to the Top 100 of the singles charts
wrongClaims.problem: Ours drops the word 'almost', flipping the claim: per the evidence the track did NOT reach the Top 100, it only came close.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: The bluesy track "Watermelon Man" almost made it into the Top 100 of the singles charts, and went on to become a jazz standard.

album:takin-off.notes: All other claims (1962 debut on Blue Note; Dexter Gordon, Freddie Hubbard, Butch Warren, Billy Higgins; hard bop; 1973 Head Hunters funk remake of Watermelon Man) match the entity-matched Wikipedia page verbatim, and MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms year, label, and all four keyTracks. Prose is a near-copy of the Wikipedia lead with 'almost' lost in editing. Repair: restore 'almost' or rephrase the chart sentence. Closing sentences are editorial.

album:the-bridge.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (recorded 1962; first release after the three-year sabbatical; first RCA Victor album; Jim Hall, Bob Cranshaw, Ben Riley). MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms 1962, RCA Victor, and all four keyTracks. 'One of jazz's great comebacks' framing is editorial but consistent with the page's hiatus narrative.

album:the-newest-sound-around.notes: No MusicBrainz match (empty search candidates) and no Wikipedia page in the pack; nothing is checkable, including year, label, keyTracks, and the prose claims (Blake/Lee duo debut, third stream blend). Background knowledge finds the description consistent with the real 1962 RCA Victor album, but that is not citable evidence. No wikipediaUrl is stored, so the site at least ships no link that could be wrong.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Verve Records, Inc.

album:think-well-of-me.notes: Label field ships the placeholder 'Unknown' while the authoritative embedded-MBID release says Verve Records, Inc. (V-8465) and the Wikipedia page says 'released by the Verve label' -- the item's own prose even names Verve, so this is an internal inconsistency with an easy repair. Everything else matches: 1962, Willard Robison songbook, Brookmeyer/Russ Case arrangements (verbatim from the Wikipedia lead); all six keyTracks are on the MB tracklist. 'Documenting the breadth of the swing period' is vague editorial filler, not flagged.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: The Bridge is a studio album by jazz saxophonist Sonny Rollins, recorded in 1962. It was Rollins's first release following a three-year sabbatical and was his first album for RCA Victor.
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA describes a different album (The Bridge). This item is What's New? per the authoritative embedded MBID, which matches our title, year, label, and all five keyTracks.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: What's New?; labels: RCA Victor (LSP-2572); date: 1962

album:whats-new.notes: Same-artist collision: albumDNA is the Wikipedia lead for The Bridge, and wikipediaUrl points at The Bridge (Sonny Rollins album) -- the wrong page for this item. The catalog fields and keyTracks correctly describe What's New? (embedded MBID, RCA Victor, 1962; tracklist matches exactly: If Ever I Would Leave You, Jungoso, Bluesongo, The Night Has a Thousand Eyes, Brownskin Girl). The site has a separate the-bridge item legitimately carrying this same prose. Repair: rewrite albumDNA for What's New? and replace the wikipedia link with the What's New? (Sonny Rollins album) page after verifying it exists.

album:back-at-the-chicken-shack.notes: Prose first half matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (recorded 1960, released 1963 on Blue Note, same session as Midnight Special, cited in 1001 Albums You Must Hear Before You Die); MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms 1963, Blue Note, and all four keyTracks. The influence sentence (Jack McDuff, Dr. Lonnie Smith) is editorial legacy hyperbole, not treated as a checkable claim.

album:blue-bash.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim and correctly credits both leaders (Burrell guitar, Smith organ, 1963, Verve). Embedded MBID confirms 1963-10, Verve V6-8553, and all five keyTracks. Data-model note, not flagged as error: the artist field lists only Jimmy Smith while the authoritative MB credit is Kenny Burrell + Jimmy Smith; the site files dual-credit albums once per leader (paired item blue-bash-burrell exists).

album:blue-bash-burrell.notes: Twin of blue-bash filed under Kenny Burrell, who is the first-listed artist in the authoritative embedded-MBID credit (Kenny Burrell + Jimmy Smith). Identical prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim; year, label, and all five keyTracks confirmed by MB.

album:boss-guitar.notes: Core facts confirmed: embedded MBID resolves to a Boss Guitar CD reissue (Riverside RCD-9459-2, 1989) whose release group dates the original to 1963; all five keyTracks on the tracklist, plus two alternate takes -- consistent with the reissue-with-alternate-takes claim. Unverifiable from the pack: that the reissue label was specifically Original Jazz Classics, and that all tracks appear on Wes Montgomery: The Complete Riverside Recordings. No Wikipedia evidence and no stored URL.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: firstReleaseDate: 1962

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers is a studio album by Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers, released on September 1, 1961, through Impulse! Records. Expanding to a sextet for the first time, it was the group's final recording with Bobby Timmons, who would be replaced by Cedar Walton.
wrongClaims.problem: The prose's factual skeleton (September 1, 1961 release, Impulse! Records, first sextet, final Timmons recording) belongs to the 1961 album 'Art Blakey and the Jazz Messengers' (a.k.a. Alamode), not to our album Caravan, which MB resolves to Riverside, 1962.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: Caravan; labels: Riverside (RM 438); date: 1962

album:caravan.notes: Same-artist collision. The stored wiki URL is the 1961 Impulse! album's page (its extract supplies the colliding facts and a completely different tracklist: A la Mode, Invitation, Circus...), so it was discarded for fact-checking and flagged as wrong_entity. Our item is Caravan per MB search (similarity 1.0) whose tracklist matches all four keyTracks (Caravan, Sweet 'n' Sour, Skylark exact; 'In the Wee Small Hours' is a substring of 'In the Wee Small Hours of the Morning'). Year field 1963 contradicts MB's 1962 (search method, strong but not infallible) -- verify Caravan's real first-release date during repair. Correct wiki target is the Caravan (Art Blakey album) page. The closing 'standards into hard bop vehicles' sentence is editorial and could survive a rewrite.

album:conversations.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (1963 on the FM label; Vee-Jay reissue as The Eric Dolphy Memorial Album the following year; 2018 Musical Prophet reissue on Resonance). Embedded MBID resolves to a 1998 Jazz World reissue CD -- valid for the tracklist (all four keyTracks present) and the release group's first date (1963-09); the original FM-label claim rests on the entity-matched Wikipedia page, which states it explicitly. Label field 'FM' consistent with that.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: A 1963 big band date for impulse! that documents Duke Ellington's ongoing work in the idiom.
wrongClaims.problem: The session is an eight-piece small group, not a big band: the Wikipedia personnel section enumerates exactly eight musicians.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote:
Duke Ellington – piano
Coleman Hawkins – tenor saxophone
Johnny Hodges – alto saxophone
Harry Carney – baritone saxophone, bass clarinet
Lawrence Brown – trombone
Ray Nance – cornet, violin
Aaron Bell – double bass
Sam Woodyard – drums

album:duke-ellington-meets-coleman-hawki.notes: First sentence (recorded August 18, 1962; released February 1963 by Impulse!) matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim; embedded MBID confirms 1963-02, impulse! AS 26, and all five keyTracks. Caveat on the flag: the contradiction is by enumeration (an octet is not a big band), not an explicit statement -- a human reviewer may judge the template sentence's wording differently, hence the lowered confidence. Artist field lists only Ellington while the authoritative credit is Ellington + Hawkins; site files dual credits per leader (paired -hawkins item exists), noted only.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: A big band recording from the hard bop period
wrongClaims.problem: The session is an eight-piece small group, not a big band: the Wikipedia personnel section enumerates exactly eight musicians.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote:
Duke Ellington – piano
Coleman Hawkins – tenor saxophone
Johnny Hodges – alto saxophone
Harry Carney – baritone saxophone, bass clarinet
Lawrence Brown – trombone
Ray Nance – cornet, violin
Aaron Bell – double bass
Sam Woodyard – drums

album:duke-ellington-meets-coleman-hawki.notes: Twin entry filed under Coleman Hawkins (authoritative MB credit includes both leaders). First sentence matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim; MB confirms date, label, and all five keyTracks. Same caveat as the Ellington twin: 'big band' is contradicted by the enumerated octet personnel, an inference a human may wish to confirm. 'Hard bop period' read as era framing and 'reconnecting jazz with blues and gospel' as genre boilerplate -- not flagged.

album:midnight-blue.notes: Checkable claims confirmed: Ray Barretto conga; Stanley Turrentine tenor on roughly half the tracks (wiki: absent on tracks 3, 4, 6, 9 of 9 listed); Blue Note 1963; MB matches all four keyTracks. Unverifiable: "'Chitlins con Carne' became a jazz standard" -- a concrete legacy claim absent from the evidence (background knowledge finds it plausible, but that is not citable). Borderline call: everything else checks out, and 'most celebrated', 'definitive mood album', and the influence-on-guitarists line are treated as editorial.

album:money-jungle.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (recorded September 17, 1962; released February 1963 by United Artists Jazz; all but one composition by Ellington; four of seven first recorded here; CD releases added eight tracks). Year: MB search evidence says 1962 while the entity-matched Wikipedia page explicitly says released February 1963 -- ours follows Wikipedia; conflict noted, not flagged. Label 'United Artists' vs 'United Artists Jazz' treated as parent-brand shorthand. keyTrack 'Fleurette Africaine' appears in MB as 'Les Fleurs Africaines (African Flower)' and is confirmed by name in the Wikipedia text, so counted as matched. Artist field lists Ellington alone; credit is 'Ellington with Mingus and Roach' -- site convention, noted only. 'Avant-garde'/'generational clash' closing lines are editorial, loosely supported by the page's generational-gap and mid-session-argument discussion.

album:monks-dream.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (Columbia, January 1963; first Columbia album after the five-year Riverside period). MB returned only a search candidate without a release doc (weak evidence), but the candidate (Monk's Dream, The Thelonious Monk Quartet, 1963) is consistent with our item. No MB tracklist; all four keyTracks are named as session recordings in the Wikipedia recording-dates passage (Bolivar Blues Oct 31; Body and Soul and Bright Mississippi Nov 1; Monk's Dream Nov 2), so keyTracks ruled ok on wiki evidence. 'Commercial breakthrough' line is editorial.

album:no-room-for-squares.notes: Fields confirmed by MB (search, similarity 1.0): Hank Mobley, 1963, Blue Note BLP 4149; all four keyTracks on the tracklist. Unverifiable: the personnel claims -- Lee Morgan on trumpet, 'either Andrew Hill or Herbie Hancock on piano' -- the MB doc carries no personnel and there is no Wikipedia evidence (consistent with background knowledge of the album's two sessions, but not citable). The rest ('finest hour', 'middleweight' comparisons to Rollins/Coltrane) is editorial. No wikipediaUrl stored.

album:one-step-beyond.notes: First five sentences match the Wikipedia lead verbatim (first of two albums swapping piano for Hutcherson's vibes; Moncur, Khan, Tony Williams; hard bop leaning post-bop/free). MB (similarity 1.0) confirms Blue Note and the exact four keyTracks. Unverifiable: 'Williams, just 17' (age not in evidence) and that his drumming 'would transform Miles's second quintet' (membership not in evidence); 'Introduced Grachan Moncur III to the jazz world' read as promotional hyperbole. Year conflict noted, not flagged: wiki short description says '1964 studio album' while the MB release and release group say 1963, matching ours -- conflicting evidence; worth a human check of the original BLP/BST 4137 release date.

album:our-man-in-paris.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead near-verbatim (Paris recording; expatriates Bud Powell and Kenny Clarke; native Parisian Pierre Michelot; The Three Bosses playing together since Powell's 1959 move). MB (similarity 1.0) confirms 1963, Blue Note, and all four keyTracks. 'Influenced countless tenor players including Joe Henderson and Michael Brecker' treated as editorial legacy framing, not a checkable factual claim.

album:sassy-swings-the-tivoli.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (1963 live album with her trio, produced by Quincy Jones, recorded at Tivoli Gardens Copenhagen over four days in July 1963). MB confirms 1963, Mercury, a Live release group, and all four keyTracks. Closing praise is editorial.

album:stan-getz-with-guest-artist-laurin.notes: Prose first sentence matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (recorded 1963, issued on Verve as V/V6-8665); the embedded MBID -- authoritative for date and label per the spec -- gives 1963-03, Verve V6-8665, and matches all five keyTracks. CAUTION for human review: the Wikipedia short description reads '1966 studio album', conflicting with the MB date; background knowledge also leans toward a 1963 recording shelved until a 1966 release, but that is not citable. Ruled clean because the year field matches the spec-designated authoritative source; the 1966 signal should be checked before trusting year=1963. 'Cool jazz period' filler treated as era framing.

album:the-black-saint.notes: Nearly all prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (recorded January 20, 1963; released July by Impulse!; single continuous composition partially written as a ballet, four tracks and six movements; canonical stature). MB confirms label, year, and the three keyTracks (colon-vs-dash punctuation only). Unverifiable nit: ours says the liner notes were 'written by Mingus's psychiatrist'; the evidence says 'liner notes written by Mingus and his then-psychotherapist, Edmund Pollock' -- joint authorship, and 'psychotherapist' rather than 'psychiatrist'. Overlapping but not identical terms, so not ruled a hard contradiction. Repair suggestion: 'co-written by Mingus and his psychotherapist'.

album:the-latin-bit.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim (recorded 1962, released on Blue Note; loose Latin concept; Quebec, Clark, Acea, Marshall, Bobo, Masseaux, Valdes). Embedded MBID confirms 1963-04, Blue Note BLP 4111, and all five keyTracks. Fine print in the wiki personnel section (Quebec and Clark appear only on the September 1962 session tracks issued as CD bonuses; Bobo credited on drums) refines but does not contradict the lead the prose copies.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: which was released in 1964 by Folkways Records

album:black-christ-of-the-andes.notes: Entity confirmed: the stored page 'Mary Lou Williams (album)' is explicitly 'alternatively titled Black Christ of the Andes', so our title field's use of the alternate title is fine, and wikiLink is correct. The label field 'Saba' contradicts both the evidence and the item's own prose, which says 'released in 1964 by Folkways Records' (background knowledge suggests a later European SABA issue exists and may be the field's source -- not citable; repair should decide which release the entry represents). No MB evidence; the wiki extract omits the track listing, so keyTracks are uncheckable ('St. Martin de Porres' is at least confirmed as the title-track hymn). Also unverifiable: 'Pioneered the jazz mass genre later explored by Duke Ellington and Dave Brubeck.' Conversion/Vatican-II context matches the page.

album:black-fire.notes: Prose matches the Wikipedia lead verbatim, including the Stephen Thomas Erlewine AllMusic quote (Blue Note debut, 1964; Philly Joe Jones scheduled but replaced by Roy Haynes; Joe Henderson and Richard Davis in the band). MB (similarity 1.0) confirms 1964, Blue Note BLP 4151, and all four keyTracks. 'Third path' closing lines are editorial.

album:blue-flames.notes: All prose claims confirmed by the stored wiki page (Turrentine collaboration, recorded 1964, Prestige; shortDescription '1964 studio album by Shirley Scott and Stanley Turrentine'). MB match is a 1995 German OJC CD reissue that carries the original Prestige PR 7338 catalog number; its 1995 release/release-group dates are reissue artifacts, not counter-evidence on the 1964 year. Tracklist matches 5/5. MB credits Scott and Turrentine jointly; site artist field lists Scott alone (first-credited; the prose names both).

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes were an American soul and R&B vocal group. One of the most popular Philadelphia soul groups of the 1970s
wrongClaims.problem: Our item is a 1964 South African jazz album credited to The Blue Notes on Gallo; this sentence and the bulk of the blurb describe the American Philadelphia soul group, a different identifiable entity.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes were an American soul and R&B vocal group. One of the most popular Philadelphia soul groups of the 1970s, the group's repertoire included soul, R&B, doo-wop, and disco.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: the group is most noted for several hits on Gamble and Huff's Philadelphia International label between 1972 and 1976, although they performed and recorded until Melvin's death in 1997
wrongClaims.problem: These facts (Philadelphia International 1972-1976, Teddy Pendergrass, Melvin's death 1997) belong to the American soul group, not to the South African jazz group behind a 1964 Gallo LP.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: the group is most noted for several hits on Gamble and Huff's Philadelphia International label between 1972 and 1976, although they performed and recorded until Melvin's death in 1997

album:blue-notes-legacy.notes: Classic title-search disambiguation collision: most of the albumDNA is the Wikipedia lead for Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes, and the stored wiki link also points to that page. The intended entity is evidently the South African jazz group The Blue Notes (Gallo label, keyTracks like Kippie / Township Bop / Ke Tla Moya, closing sentences about apartheid exile and the London jazz scene) - no evidence for them in the pack (MB search returned nothing), so those closing claims and the keyTracks are unverifiable. Repair needs sources on the South African Blue Notes and the 'Blue Notes in Concert' Gallo release.

album:crescent.notes: All prose claims and fields confirmed by both sources: July 1964 release on Impulse!, Tyner/Garrison/Jones personnel, original Coltrane compositions (wiki); impulse! label and RG first release 1964 (embedded MBID; the specific MB release entry is a 1987 reissue). MB credit 'John Coltrane Quartet' vs ours 'John Coltrane' is a credit variant, not an error. 5/5 keyTracks match.

album:destination-out.notes: Prose confirmed by wiki (recorded 1963, Blue Note, second McLean album with Hutcherson and Moncur, Ridley/Haynes rhythm section). Year and label confirmed by MB (1964-10, Blue Note BLP 4165). 4/4 keyTracks match. Closing sentences about McLean's transition are editorial.

album:empyrean-isles.notes: Core facts confirmed by wiki (fourth studio album, Blue Note November 1964, Hubbard/Carter/Williams) and MB (1964, BST 84175); 4/4 keyTracks on MB list. Wiki's body notes Hubbard played cornet on this session while the wiki lead (like our prose) still calls him 'trumpeter' - not a contradiction. ''Cantaloupe Island' became a hip-hop staple decades later' is legacy color not checkable against this evidence.

album:evolution.notes: Prose verbatim-matches the wiki lead (debut as leader, recorded 1963, full personnel, the two related McLean albums) and 4/4 keyTracks match. The year field is disputed between the evidence sources: ours says 1964 and the wiki shortDescription says '1964 studio album by Grachan Moncur III', but the MB search match (original US Blue Note BST 84153 entry) dates both the release and the release group 1963. Recorded-1963 is agreed by everyone; only the original release year is split, so flagged for human review rather than asserted as error. 'Influenced Roswell Rudd and later avant-garde trombonists' is uncheckable legacy color. Minor typo in prose: 'A overlooked masterpiece'.

album:getz-gilberto.notes: MB found no matching release (candidates are the different live albums Getz/Gilberto #2 and Getz/Gilberto '76), so corroboration is wiki-only - and the DNA is nearly verbatim the stored wiki lead, so verification is partly circular. The page is solidly about our entity (title, both artists, 1964, Verve) and confirms Jobim's involvement, March 1964 release, Astrud on 'Garota de Ipanema' and 'Corcovado', Olga Albizu artwork, and the Record of the Year Grammy. No MB tracklist to check keyTracks; wiki text does mention Desafinado, Corcovado and Garota de Ipanema as tracks. Genre-fusion and world-music sentences are editorial.

keyTracksUnmatched: On the Sunny Side of the Street
When the Saints Go Marching In
Cut Off My Legs and Call Me Shorty
album:hello-dolly.notes: Prose is the wiki lead verbatim and fully confirmed (All Stars lineup, recording dates Dec 3 1963 / Apr 18 1964 in NYC, Kapp 1964, topped Billboard Top LPs with 74-week stay). The error is in keyTracks: 5 of 6 are not on the album per the authoritative embedded-MBID tracklist (Hello, Dolly! / It's Been a Long, Long Time / A Lot of Livin' to Do / A Kiss to Build a Dream On / Someday / Hey, Look Me Over / I Still Get Jealous / Moon River / Be My Life's Companion / Blueberry Hill / You Are Woman, I Am Man / Jeepers Creepers). Only 'Hello Dolly' matches; the rest look like generic Armstrong-repertoire filler. Repair: replace keyTracks with actual album tracks.

album:judgment.notes: Prose matches the wiki lead (recorded and released 1964 on Blue Note; Elvin Jones, Richard Davis, Bobby Hutcherson). MB confirms 1964-09, Blue Note BST 84159. 4/4 keyTracks match. Closing sentences are editorial.

album:mingus-mingus-mingus-mingus-mingus.notes: Release date January 9, 1964 confirmed by wiki; impulse! (AS 54), 1964, US confirmed by embedded MBID. 5/5 keyTracks match (apostrophe variant in 'Better Get Hit in Yo' Soul'). 'From the hard bop period...' framing is editorial, not a factual claim.

album:night-dreamer.notes: All claims confirmed by wiki (fourth album, released November 1964 by Blue Note, quintet with Morgan/Tyner/Workman/Jones, six Shorter originals); MB confirms 1964, BST 84173. 4/4 keyTracks match. 'His compositions became jazz standards' is legacy color.

album:out-to-lunch.notes: All claims confirmed: only Blue Note recording as leader, BLP 4163 / BST 84163 (MB shows BLP 4163, 1964-08), quintet personnel (Hubbard/Hutcherson/Davis/Williams), Reid Miles cover with the seven-handed-clock 'Will Be Back' sign. Ours omits that the issue was posthumous - an omission, not an error. 4/4 keyTracks match.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Joseph Arthurlin Harriott was a Jamaican jazz musician and composer, whose principal instrument was the alto saxophone.
wrongClaims.problem: The entire albumDNA is a person's biography (the Joe Harriott wiki lead) on an album item; it says nothing about the album itself. Spec tell: content about 'a person when the item is an album'.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: shortDescription: Jamaican jazz musician and composer

album:poetry-and-jazz-in-concert-record-.notes: Softer variant of the disease: the bio is of the album's actual featured saxophonist, not an unrelated entity, but the prose still describes a person rather than the album. The album's own fields check out against the authoritative embedded-MBID release: Argo ZDA 26, GB, 1964, and both 'Poetry and Jazz' tracks match. MB credits five artists (Adrian Mitchell, Dannie Abse, The Michael Garrick Quintet, Joe Harriott, Shake Keane); the site's artist field lists only Harriott - a curatorial simplification worth review. The stored wiki link goes to the artist page, not an album page (possibly a deliberate fallback, but it is not about our entity). Repair: write album-specific prose about this poetry-and-jazz concert document.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: The album was rereleased on CD with two bonus tracks from the same session.
wrongClaims.problem: The page's track-listing note (corroborated by the personnel section crediting Sam Jones on tracks 7-8 only) says the two CD bonus tracks were recorded June 13, 1960 - a different session three years before the 1963 album session.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: NOTE: "Lonesome Road" and "Smith Walk" are bonus tracks on a CD reissue that were recorded on June 13, 1960, featuring the same lineup plus Sam Jones on bass.

album:prayer-meetin.notes: Everything else checks out: recorded 1963, Blue Note, year 1964 (MB embedded BLP 4164, 1964-06), 5/5 keyTracks match. Caveat on the flagged claim: the wiki page is internally inconsistent - its own lead contains the identical 'two bonus tracks from the same session' sentence our prose copied, while its dated, personnel-corroborated note contradicts it; the specific note is the better evidence. Repair: drop or correct 'from the same session'. MB and wiki credit Stanley Turrentine alongside Smith; ours lists Smith alone, consistent with the wiki shortDescription's primary credit ('by Jimmy Smith with Stanley Turrentine').

album:search-for-the-new-land.notes: Prose verbatim-matches the wiki lead and is internally consistent (recorded 1964, shelved two years, issued as 84169 - i.e., a 1966 release). The year field says 1964: contradicted by the wiki shortDescription ('1966 studio album by Lee Morgan') and by the shelved-two-years account in both wiki and our own prose, but supported by the pack's MB search match (release date and RG firstReleaseDate both 1964 - possibly erroneous MB data). The catalog's convention elsewhere is release year, which would make 1964 wrong; since the pack evidence is split, flagged for human review rather than asserted as error. 4/4 keyTracks match.

album:the-most-important-jazz-album-of-1.notes: Recorded 1964 and Colpix label confirmed by wiki; release group first release 1964 per embedded MBID (the MB release entry itself is a 2003 reissue with an empty label list - artifact, not contradiction). 5/5 keyTracks on the MB list (ours 'Walking' = MB 'Walking'; wiki spells it 'Walkin''). 'A cool jazz entry... documenting the breadth of the free jazz period' is era-grouping editorial. Wiki personnel has Baker on flugelhorn for this date while the lead (like ours) says 'trumpeter/vocalist' - no contradiction.

album:the-sidewinder.notes: All claims confirmed: 1964, Van Gelder Studio Englewood Cliffs, BLP 4157 (mono) / BST 84157 (stereo) - MB shows BLP 4157, 1964-07. 4/4 keyTracks match. Legacy sentences ('influenced countless soul jazz recordings') are editorial color.

album:witches-devils.notes: Embedded MBID confirms the title history the prose describes: original Danish Debut release titled 'Spirits' (DEB 146, DK, 1964) inside release group 'Witches & Devils'. 4/4 keyTracks match. Unchecked substantive claims: recorded in New York City, and the later Freedom-label reissue (the RG title is consistent with the alias but no Freedom release appears in evidence). Nothing is contradicted. No wiki URL stored and none found.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: date: 1966; releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: 1966

album:a-caddy-for-daddy.notes: MB (search method, exact artist match) dates both the release and the release group 1966 vs site year 1965; nothing in evidence supports 1965. If the site intended the recording year the field may be deliberate, but no recording date is in evidence and the catalog's convention elsewhere is release year. keyTrack 'Carl's Ballade' is not on the MB tracklist (A Caddy for Daddy / The Morning After / Venus Di Mildew / Ace Duece Trey / 3rd Time Around); 'Third Time Around' was accepted as a spelling variant of '3rd Time Around'. Personnel claims (Lee Morgan, Curtis Fuller) are unverifiable - no personnel evidence. Title track and 'The Morning After' are confirmed real tracks. No wiki evidence.

album:a-love-supreme.notes: Nearly everything is confirmed by wiki: single-session recording December 9, 1964 at Van Gelder Studio (Englewood Cliffs, NJ), classic quartet personnel, four-part suite, the 'a love supreme' chant in Acknowledgement, Coltrane 'playing' the liner-notes poem on 'Psalm'; year/label confirmed by MB (impulse! A 77, RG first release 1965-01). keyTracks all match as substrings of the MB side titles. The unverified substantive claim: 'selling over one million copies' - wiki says about 500,000 by 1970 and 'ranks among Coltrane's best-selling albums'; not contradicted (later sales could exceed 1M) but not supported by the pack evidence. 'Inspiring musicians from Carlos Santana to Kendrick Lamar' is likewise uncheckable legacy color.

album:behind-the-8-ball.notes: Embedded MBID confirms artist (hyphen variant 'Baby-Face Willette'), Argo 749, June 1965; 5/5 keyTracks match. Unchecked substantive claims: 'final album', 'recorded in late 1964', 'cut in Chicago' - no evidence either way. The stored wiki link points to 'Face to Face (Baby Face Willette album)', his 1961 Blue Note debut - a different album, so the site ships a wrong link on this page; per entity-matching rules that page was discarded for fact-checking (it incidentally corroborates that Willette recorded for Blue Note earlier, but cannot verify claims about this album). Repair: fix or remove the wikipediaUrl.

album:blue-note-4000.notes: No MB release or search candidates, no wiki page - nothing checkable. Red flags for human review (background knowledge, not citable evidence): 'Blue Note 4000' resembles the label's BLP 4000 catalog-number series rather than an album title, and the three named 'selections' (Open Sesame, Hub-Tones, Ready for Freddie) are the titles of three separate Hubbard albums from 1960-62, not tracks. The entry looks fabricated or synthetic; recommend a manual source hunt or removal from the catalog.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: built around the irresistible groove of the title track, which became a Blue Note classic
wrongClaims.problem: The album has no track titled 'Dippin'' per the MB tracklist; the opener is 'The Dip'. The prose's 'title track' does not exist on the record.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: tracks: The Dip, Recado Bossa Nova, The Break Through, The Vamp, I See Your Face Before Me, Ballin'

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Contains Mobley's most recognizable composition in the title track, a boogaloo-tinged burner
wrongClaims.problem: Same issue: no title track exists; the claim presumably intends 'The Dip'.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: tracks: The Dip, Recado Bossa Nova, The Break Through, The Vamp, I See Your Face Before Me, Ballin'

album:dippin.notes: MB (search, exact artist match) confirms year (1965-06-18) and Blue Note BLP 4209. keyTrack 'Dippin'' matches nothing on the tracklist (ours also lists 'The Dip' separately, which does match, along with Recado Bossa Nova and The Break Through). Personnel claims (Lee Morgan, Harold Mabern Jr.) are unverifiable - no personnel evidence. Repair: retitle the 'title track' references to 'The Dip' and fix keyTracks. No wiki evidence.

album:duke-ellington-fletcher-henderson-.notes: Embedded MBID confirms title, Society SOC 982, GB, 1965, and all 5 keyTracks. Two concerns for human review: (1) the authoritative credit is three co-equal artists (Duke Ellington, Fletcher Henderson, Artie Shaw) while the site's artist field picks Henderson alone - curatorial simplification; (2) 'A big band outing from 1965... features pianist Fletcher Henderson recording for Society' implies 1965 recordings, but this is almost certainly a budget-label compilation of older swing-era material - background knowledge says Henderson died in 1952 and could not have recorded in 1965, but the release group carries no Compilation secondaryType and no recording dates are in evidence, so per the rules this stays unverifiable rather than a proven anachronism. Recommend reframing the prose as a compilation.

album:esp.notes: All prose confirmed verbatim by wiki: recorded January 20-22, 1965, released August 16, 1965 by Columbia, first release of the second great quintet (Davis/Shorter/Hancock/Carter/Williams), named after Shorter's tune, telepathy quote. The embedded-MBID release is a 1972 Japanese CBS/Sony reissue (RG first release 1965) - its label does not contradict Columbia as the original label, which wiki confirms. 5/5 keyTracks match.

album:fire-music.notes: MB search match (similarity 1.0) and stored wiki page both confirm Archie Shepp, Fire Music, Impulse! 1965 (AS 86). Prose mirrors the wiki extract; personnel (Curson, Orange, Brown, Johnson, Chambers), Malcolm X dedication, and Los Olvidados film homage all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist ('Malcolm, Malcolm—Semper Malcolm' matches MB 'Malcolm, Malcolm - Semper Malcolm', punctuation variant). Trailing sentences ('merged Black nationalist politics...', 'most politically outspoken') are editorial, not flagged.

album:fuchsia-swing-song.notes: Entity confirmed by both sources (Sam Rivers, Blue Note BST-84184). Prose mirrors wiki: debut album, recorded 1964, 1995 CD reissue, 2003 Connoisseur Series with four alternate takes - all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Year wrinkle: ours year=1965 matches wiki shortDescription '1965 studio album by Sam Rivers'; MB release/release-group date says 1964, which equals the recording year stated by both prose and wiki, so the MB date looks like recording-year slop (a recurring MB pattern in this pack). Not flagged as error; human may want to pin the exact release date. 'Influenced the loft jazz movement' tail is editorial.

album:i-put-a-spell-on-you.notes: Embedded MBID (authoritative) confirms Nina Simone, Philips PHS 600-172, 1965. All 5 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Every prose claim (recorded 1964-65 NYC, released by Philips 1965, Billboard 200 #99, UK Albums #18, title track R&B #23 / UK Singles #28) verified verbatim against the stored wiki page.

album:idle-moments.notes: Entity confirmed (Grant Green, Blue Note BLP 4154). Prose mirrors wiki: recorded 1963, released 1965, personnel Henderson/Hutcherson/Pearson/Cranshaw/Harewood all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist (exact). Year: ours 1965 is supported by the wiki extract's explicit month-level statement 'released in both stereo and mono in February 1965'; MB release/RG date says 1964 (bare year, search-method) - wiki's explicit statement preferred, MB date noted as likely erroneous. Trailing 'spacious and meditative' sentences are editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Inner Urge is an album by the jazz saxophonist Joe Henderson, released in 1966 via Blue Note Records, his fourth recorded as a leader.

album:inner-urge.notes: Right entity throughout; the year FIELD is wrong. The item is internally inconsistent: its own albumDNA prose says 'released in 1966' (matching the wiki page) while the year field says 1965, which matches no evidence. MB RG firstReleaseDate '1964-11-30' equals the recording date stated by prose and wiki (recorded at Van Gelder Studio November 30, 1964), so it is a recording date, not a release date; the MB-matched release is the 2004 RVG edition of the correct release group. All 4 keyTracks match MB (RVG suffixes ignored) and the wiki tracklist. Personnel (Tyner, Jones, Cranshaw) verified. Fix: year 1965 -> 1966.

album:juju.notes: Entity confirmed (Wayne Shorter, Blue Note BST 84182). Prose mirrors wiki: fifth album, released July 1965, rhythm section Tyner/Workman/Jones with the Coltrane connection - all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Year: ours 1965 supported by wiki's explicit 'released in July 1965 by Blue Note Records'; MB release/RG says 1964 (bare year, search method) - wiki's month-level statement preferred, MB date noted as likely recording-year slop. Trailing 'signature style' sentences are editorial.

album:maiden-voyage.notes: Entity confirmed (Herbie Hancock, Blue Note BLP 4195, MB date 1965-05-17). Prose mirrors wiki: fifth album, recorded by Rudy Van Gelder March 17 1965, catalog BLP 4195/BST 84195, personnel Coleman/Hubbard/Carter/Williams, oceanic concept, Grammy Hall of Fame 1999 - all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Closing 'expanded jazz's emotional palette' sentences are editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Matador is an album by American jazz guitarist Grant Green.
It contains performances recorded in 1964 but not released on the Japanese Blue Note label until 1979.

album:music-matador.notes: The prose, artist field, and 3 of 4 keyTracks all identify this item as Grant Green's 'Matador' (recorded 1964, first released 1979 in Japan), matching the stored wiki page - so the entity behind the content is consistent, but two fields are wrong: title 'Music Matador' matches no evidence, and year 1965 contradicts both the wiki and the item's own prose (recorded 1964 / released 1979). MB search found nothing (likely searched the corrupted title), so no MB doc to match. keyTracks checked against the wiki tracklist (Matador, My Favorite Things, Green Jeans, Bedouin, Wives and Lovers): 'Green's Greenery' is unmatched; closest real title is 'Green Jeans'. Editorial tail calls it 'Latin-jazz fusion' while the wiki's AllMusic quote calls it 'a cool-toned, straight-ahead modal workout' - characterization conflict worth an editorial rewrite, not counted as a factual error. Background hypothesis (NOT citable evidence): 'Music Matador' is a known Eric Dolphy/Prince Lasha title, a plausible source of the title contamination.

album:pastel-blues.notes: Entity confirmed (Nina Simone, Philips PHS 600-187, 1965). Release date October 1, 1965 by Philips verified against wiki ('released on October 1, 1965, by Philips Records'; ours has a minor 'in/on' grammar slip). All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. 'Sinnerman became a anthem...' is editorial (and contains a typo 'a anthem' - cosmetic only).

album:point-of-departure.notes: Entity confirmed (Andrew Hill, Blue Note BST 84167, MB date 1965-04). Prose mirrors wiki: recorded 1964 / released 1965, sextet with Dolphy, Henderson, Dorham, Davis, Williams - all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Trailing sentences are editorial.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Goodbye is an album by vibraphonist Milt Jackson recorded in 1973 and released on the CTI label.
wrongClaims.problem: This sentence describes a different album entirely (Goodbye, Milt Jackson, CTI, 1973). Our item is the 1965 Verve LP 'Ray Brown / Milt Jackson' (embedded MBID, authoritative: title 'Ray Brown / Milt Jackson', date 1965, label Verve V6-8615).
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: title: "Ray Brown / Milt Jackson"; date: "1965"; labels: Verve, V6-8615

album:ray-brown-milt-jackson.notes: Classic disambiguation-collision case. The structured fields (title, year 1965, label Verve) and all 5 keyTracks match the authoritative embedded-MBID release exactly, so the catalog entry itself is the real 1965 Verve album. But the albumDNA's factual skeleton is imported from 'Goodbye' (Milt Jackson, CTI, 1973), and the stored wikipediaUrl points to that wrong album's page (Goodbye (Milt Jackson album) - confirmed by the wiki evidence to be about the 1973 CTI record). Repair: rewrite albumDNA for the actual Ray Brown / Milt Jackson LP and replace or drop the wikipediaUrl. Minor: artist field credits only 'Milt Jackson' while MB credits 'Ray Brown, Milt Jackson' jointly (site convention for co-led dates, cf. the Ella/Duke duplicate entries). The second prose sentence's 'big band sensibility... hard bop era' is generic template text, unverifiable.

album:solo-monk-monk.notes: Embedded MBID (authoritative) confirms Thelonious Monk, Columbia CS9149, 1965. Prose mirrors wiki verbatim ('fifth studio album... recorded for Columbia Records, and his eighth overall for that label', AllMusic Jurek quote, Japan SACD). All 5 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Editorial caution for repair-writer: the appended template sentence 'A hard bop entry from 1965... documenting the breadth of the hard bop period' mischaracterizes a solo-piano standards record (evidence: 'The album is composed of solo piano work by Monk'); genre labels are editorial so not flagged as factual error, but this boilerplate (same template as the ray-brown-milt-jackson tail) deserves a rewrite.

album:some-other-stuff.notes: Entity confirmed (Grachan Moncur III, Blue Note BST 84177, MB date 1965-02). Prose mirrors wiki: second album, recorded July 6 1964, released early the following year, quintet with Shorter/Hancock/McBee/Williams - all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Trailing sentences are editorial.

album:song-for-my-father.notes: Right entity: stored wiki page confirms the 1965 Horace Silver Quintet album on Blue Note; prose first half mirrors wiki verbatim (Brazil trip, cover photo of John Tavares Silver, Cape Verde liner-note quote - all verified). All 4 keyTracks verified against the wiki tracklist (MB returned no resolved release, only a score-100 candidate credited to 'The Horace Silver Quintet', RG date 1964-12 - weak evidence; its 1964-12 vs wiki's '1965 album' left as an open source conflict). UNVERIFIABLE because of the substantive claim that Steely Dan borrowed the title track's bass intro for 'Rikki Don't Lose That Number' and that Donald Fagen disputed this as 'a generic Brazilian bass line' - none of this is in the pack evidence. Widely repeated lore, but it needs a citable source before it can stand.

album:soul-sauce.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0) confirms Cal Tjader, Soul Sauce, Verve, 1965 - fields all check. Wikipedia evidence is an opensearch miss (page about 'Mexican Institute of Sound', a different entity) - discarded; no wiki URL is stored so no link issue. keyTracks: 'Soul Sauce (Guachi Guaro)', 'Tanya', 'Somewhere in the Night' match MB; 'Maramoor' fails the mechanical substring match against MB's 'Maramor Mambo' but is almost certainly the same tune (one-letter spelling variant plus truncation), so this is listed as unmatched per the rules without treating it as a track-not-on-album error; repair-writer should normalize the spelling against a primary source. UNVERIFIABLE because the substantive claim that the title track is 'Dizzy Gillespie-penned' has no composer-credit evidence in the pack; 'biggest commercial success', 'most popular Latin jazz artist of the 1960s', and 'established the boogaloo groove' are editorial.

album:spiritual-unity.notes: Entity confirmed (MB: Albert Ayler Trio, ESP-Disk' 1002, 1965; ours credits 'Albert Ayler' - the wiki body itself says 'studio album by American free jazz saxophonist Albert Ayler', so the credit variant is fine). Prose mirrors wiki: recorded July 10 1964 NYC, Peacock/Murray, first album recorded for Bernard Stollman's ESP-Disk, 'shockingly different', AllMusic 5-star 'landmark recording' quote - all verified. All 4 keyTracks in MB tracklist. Cosmetic: the final AllMusic quotation is left unclosed and the editorial tail ('Influenced countless free jazz musicians and inspired John Coltrane's late period explorations', an interpretive influence claim) is fused into it - punctuation repair recommended.

album:the-heliocentric-worlds-of-sun-ra-.notes: Prose is the wiki page's opening verbatim ('1965 album... Sun Ra and his Solar Arkestra' back-cover quote) - verified. Year 1965 confirmed by wiki and MB release-group firstReleaseDate. All 5 keyTracks present in both the MB tracklist and the wiki tracklist. Data-hygiene flag: the embedded MBID resolves to 'Cosmic Equation', a 1990 German BOOTLEG CD (Magic Music label) inside the correct release group - it is our album's content, but the site should repoint the MBID to an original/official ESP release. Label field 'ESP-Disk'' is not directly stated by the matched release (which is the bootleg); the wiki supports it indirectly (catalog 'ESP 1014-2', 'Adapted from the ESP Sleeve notes'). No contradiction.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: A Sign of the Times is an album by American jazz guitarist Joe Pass that was released in 1965 by World Pacific Records.

album:a-sign-of-the-times.notes: Right entity (Joe Pass, World Pacific; embedded MBID resolves to a 2010 Japanese CD reissue inside the correct release group). The release YEAR cannot be settled from the pack: the wiki page says 1965 twice (body + shortDescription) while the MB release group says firstReleaseDate 1966; our year field (1966) matches MB, our prose ('released in 1965') matches wiki - the item contradicts itself, and whichever year is right, either the field or the prose needs fixing. No in-pack recording date exists to explain either value, so this is logged as a conflict, not a factual_error. Observation for the human reviewer (NOT citable from this pack): the covered repertoire ('A Sign of the Times' was 'a hit for Petula Clark' per wiki; 'Nowhere Man') is mid-60s pop whose release timing could settle the anachronism question - recommend checking primary sources; suspicion leans 1966. All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist; remaining prose claims (orchestrated pop hits, Petula Clark hit, 2002 Euphoria CD reissue with Simplicity) verified against wiki.

album:ascension.notes: Entity confirmed (John Coltrane, impulse! AS 95). Prose mirrors wiki verbatim: released through Impulse! February 1966, watershed framing, AllMusic quote, recorded Van Gelder Studio June 1965 - all verified. Both keyTracks in MB tracklist. Year: ours 1966 supported by wiki's explicit 'released through Impulse! Records in February 1966'; MB release/RG says 1965, which equals the recording year stated by the wiki (recorded June 1965) - the recurring MB recording-year-as-release-date pattern; not flagged. Closing 'pushed free jazz to its limits' sentences are editorial.

album:complete-communion.notes: Entity confirmed (Don Cherry, Blue Note BST 84226, 1966). Prose claims verified against wiki: 1966 album, debut as bandleader, first release on Blue Note. Both suite-form keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly. 'Pointed toward the world music direction... influenced the AACM and European free jazz scenes' is editorial/interpretive, not flagged.

album:ella-at-dukes-place.notes: Embedded MBID (authoritative) confirms the Fitzgerald/Ellington Verve album, V-4070, release date 1966; all 5 keyTracks in its tracklist. Prose mirrors the stored wiki page (second studio Fitzgerald-Ellington album after the 1957 songbook; Cote D'Azur live double recorded 1966; 1967 Grammy nomination for Best Female Pop Vocal Performance - all verified). Year wrinkle: our year FIELD (1966) matches the authoritative MB date, while our prose (verbatim wiki) calls it 'a 1965 studio album' - the wiki labels it by recording year (recorded October 18-20, 1965 per its own text; it also calls V6-4070 'the 1965 Verve LP'). Both values trace to entity-matched evidence; recommend harmonizing prose to 'recorded 1965, released 1966'. Trivial: wiki says second '(and last)' studio album; ours drops the parenthetical.

album:ella-at-dukes-place-ellington.notes: MB-only evidence (no wikipedia page found; none stored). Embedded MBID resolves to the 1996 CD reissue inside the correct release group (RG firstReleaseDate 1966, artists Ella Fitzgerald & Duke Ellington, Verve) - confirms ours: 1966 Verve release, and both named tracks plus all 5 keyTracks are in the tracklist. 'Duke Ellington's piano artistry is on full display' and 'steeped in jazz' are editorial framing (MB carries no instrument credits); no contradictions. This item duplicates 'ella-at-dukes-place' under the co-credited artist (site convention); artist field is half of MB's joint credit.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: release date: "1967"; releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1967"

album:globe-unity.notes: MB search (similarity 1.0; title, SABA label and Schlippenbach/Globe Unity Orchestra credit all align) is the only evidence doc - no wikipedia page found, none stored. It dates the release 1967 in both the release date and the release-group firstReleaseDate, contradicting our year 1966 (plausibly the recording year, but the pack contains no recording date to confirm that). Both keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly. Unverifiable side claims, noted not flagged: 'assembled fourteen musicians' (no personnel in evidence) and 'German pianist' (nationality/instrument not in evidence); 'founded the European free jazz big band tradition' etc. is editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Happenings is an album by the jazz vibraphonist Bobby Hutcherson, released in 1967 on the Blue Note label.

album:happenings.notes: Right entity; the year field is wrong. BOTH evidence docs agree on 1967: the stored wiki page ('released in 1967', shortDescription '1967 studio album') and MB (release date 1967, RG firstReleaseDate 1967, Blue Note BST-84231). Ours says 1966 (likely the recording year, but no recording date appears in the pack to confirm). Personnel claims (Hancock piano, Cranshaw bass, Chambers drums) verified against wiki. All 5 keyTracks in MB tracklist. 'Masterpiece', 'most important vibraphonist since Milt Jackson', 'touchstone for modern post-bop' are editorial. Fix: year 1966 -> 1967.

album:here-and-there.notes: Embedded MBID (authoritative) confirms Eric Dolphy, Prestige PR 7382, 1966; all 4 keyTracks in its tracklist. Prose claims verified verbatim against the stored wiki page: released 1966 as PRLP 7382, three separate recording dates, Waldron's 'Status Seeking' and the solo bass-clarinet 'God Bless the Child' recorded July 16 1961 at the Five Spot tied to the At the Five Spot recordings. Data-quality flag (not a factual error): the albumDNA is TRUNCATED mid-sentence - it ends with '"April Fool" and "G.W.' - the copied wiki sentence was cut off; repair-writer should complete or trim it. Minor: wiki shortDescription calls it a live album (it mixes live and studio material per the text); ours calls it 'a jazz album', no conflict.

album:indo-jazz-suite.notes: MB returned only a search candidate (no release resolved): 'Indo-Jazz Suite' by 'Joe Harriott and John Mayer Double Quintet', firstReleaseDate 1966 — weak evidence, but it does corroborate the title, the 1966 year, John Mayer's involvement, and the 'double quintet' concept. No wikipedia evidence at all. Unchecked substantive claims: label Columbia; Mayer being an Indian violinist; 'first serious attempt to fuse jazz improvisation with Indian classical music'; 'Predated the Indo-jazz experiments of John McLaughlin and others by several years'; the five-jazz-plus-five-Indian-classical personnel split. No contradictions found.

album:inside-basie-outside.notes: Embedded-MBID release (authoritative) confirms title, 1966, label VSP (VSP-12), and all five keyTracks; the three tracks named in the prose are all on the MB tracklist. MB credits 'Count Basie Orchestra' vs ours 'Count Basie' — normal credit variant, not an error. The 'pianist' descriptor for Basie is not stated in the evidence (trivial). No wikipedia evidence available; no URL stored.

album:meditations.notes: Prose matches the stored wikipedia page (confirmed to be our album) nearly verbatim: 1966, 'spiritual follow-up to A Love Supreme', Coltrane and Pharoah Sanders both on tenor, last recording with the classic quartet (Garrison/Jones/Tyner), next group Sanders/Ali/Garrison/Alice. MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms 1966, impulse! A 9110, and all four keyTracks on the tracklist ('Compassion' is the only album track not listed as a key track — fine). Closing influence sentences are editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Mercy, Mercy, Mercy! Live at "The Club" is a 1967 live in-studio album by The Cannonball Adderley Quintet

wrongClaims.ourClaim: is a 1967 live in-studio album
wrongClaims.problem: prose release year contradicts the MB release (search method, similarity 0.93), which dates the Capitol ST 2663 release and the release-group first release to 1966
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: date: 1966; firstReleaseDate: 1966

album:mercy-mercy-mercy.notes: The certain defect is internal: the year field says 1966 while the prose says 1967 — one of them is wrong regardless of which source is right. Evidence is split: MB says 1966, the stored wikipedia page (clearly our album) says 1967 throughout (Grammy in 1967, 1967 R&B albums list). Repair needs an external source to settle the year and align field+prose. Everything else checks out: the Grammy claim and 2021 Grammy Hall of Fame are verbatim from wiki; 'crossover hit' is supported by wiki (No. 11 on the Hot 100); all four keyTracks are on the MB tracklist; label Capitol matches. Ours.title drops the 'Live at "The Club"' subtitle — cosmetic only.

album:mode-for-joe.notes: Prose is verbatim from the stored wikipedia page (our album): fifth studio album, recorded and released 1966, full personnel (Morgan/Fuller/Hutcherson/Walton/Carter/Chambers), last Blue Note leader date until The State of the Tenor, Vols. 1 & 2. All three keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Caveat: the MB search matched a 2013 Music Matters audiophile reissue (its release-group firstReleaseDate is 2013), so MB date fields describe the reissue, not the original; the 1966 claim rests on wikipedia, which states it explicitly.

album:music-on-my-mind.notes: Embedded-MBID release (authoritative) confirms 1966, MPS 20628, artist credit, and all five keyTracks. The stored wikipedia URL points to the ARTIST page (Willie 'the Lion' Smith), not an album page — wrong link target for an album item, though it is the album's own artist (benign as collisions go; fix or null it). The prose's bio sentence (full name 'William Henry Joseph Bonaparte Bertholf Smith', nickname 'the Lion', 'American jazz and stride pianist') is verbatim-verified against that artist page. Rewrite candidate, not a factual error: 'A early jazz entry from 1966... documenting the breadth of the free jazz period' is era-template boilerplate (wiki calls Smith a stride pianist; nothing in evidence ties this album to free jazz) plus a grammar error ('A early').

fieldIssues.evidence: no such track on the 1966 ESP-Disk release; 'Don Cherry' is a credited performer on the release, so a personnel name likely leaked into keyTracks
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: tracks: "Dons Dawn", "AY", "ITT"; artists: Albert Ayler, Don Cherry, John Tchicai, Roswell Rudd, Gary Peacock, Sonny Murray

album:new-york-eye-and-ear.notes: Prose first sentence is verbatim from the stored wikipedia page (our album): recorded July 1964 by an augmented Ayler group as the soundtrack to Michael Snow's film — consistent with the MB release-group secondaryType 'Soundtrack'. KeyTracks 'AY' and 'ITT' match; 'Don Cherry' does not match anything on the MB tracklist — it could conceivably be a reissue track title, but no evidence in the pack supports that, and the name matches a credited sideman. Year evidence is split: ours 1966 agrees with the MB release and first-release date (1966), while the wiki shortDescription says '1965 studio album' (the extract itself gives no release year) — noted for human review, not charged against ours.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: labels: [{"name": "Atlantic", "catalogNumber": "1460"}]

album:sayin-somethin.notes: The label field ships 'Unknown' while the embedded-MBID release (authoritative) says Atlantic 1460 — and our own prose already says 'released on the Atlantic label'. Fix the field to Atlantic. Year 1966 and all six keyTracks verified against MB. Unverifiable prose claims (no personnel in evidence): the four-orchestra-plus-four-quintet split and sidemen Joe Henderson, Herbie Hancock, Bob Cranshaw, Roy McCurdy. 'From the free jazz era... within the bebop conversation of the time' is internally muddled era boilerplate — rewrite candidate, not an evidence contradiction.

album:sound-roscoe-mitchell.notes: MB search matched the WRONG release: 'Sound Songs' (1997, Delmark), a different Roscoe Mitchell album — discarded for fact-checking, so there is no MB evidence for 'Sound'. Verification rests on the stored wikipedia page, which IS our album: 1966 debut on Delmark; personnel includes Lester Bowie and Malachi Favors (six musicians total, consistent with 'Sextet'); the original-LP track listing covers all three keyTracks (Ornette / The Little Suite / Sound), so keyTracks are marked ok on wiki evidence. Unchecked substantive claims: 'The first recording by the AACM' and 'founding document of the AACM' — the wiki extract never mentions the AACM; the claim is widely repeated per background knowledge, but that is not citable here. 'Spawned the Art Ensemble of Chicago' is looser than the wiki passage (AEC recordings 'expanded upon' Sound's concepts). The silence/space characterization is supported by the Penguin/AllMusic quotes in the wiki extract.

album:speak-no-evil.notes: Prose is verbatim from the stored wikipedia page (our album): sixth album, released June 1966 by Blue Note, hard bop + modal, personnel Hubbard/Hancock/Carter/Elvin Jones, cover photo of first wife Teruko (Irene) Nakagami met 1961. All four keyTracks on the MB tracklist. Year conflict noted: the MB search release dates BLP 4194 and the release-group first release to 1965, while wiki states 'initially released on LP in 1966' (and 'rec. 1964, rel. 1966' in its external links). Ours consistently says 1966 and sides with the more detailed wiki account — flagged for human review, not charged as an error against ours.

album:tequila.notes: First prose sentence is verbatim from the stored wikipedia page (our album): released 1966. The embedded-MBID match is a 1999 CD reissue, but its release-group firstReleaseDate is 1966 and the label is Verve — consistent with ours; all five keyTracks are on the tracklist. Rewrite candidate (not factual): 'cool jazz work... a 1966 session reflecting the free jazz period' is era boilerplate at odds with wiki's own characterization ('mainly a Latin-flavored album' with Claus Ogerman strings) — genre framing is editorial, so not flagged as an error.

album:unit-structures.notes: First sentence is verbatim from the stored wikipedia page (our album): released October 1966 by Blue Note. MB (search, similarity 1.0) confirms 1966, Blue Note BST 84237; both keyTracks on the tracklist ('Unit Structure/As of a Now/Section' matches MB's 'Unit Structure / As of a Now / Section' up to spacing). Closing sentences are editorial.

album:ammmusic.notes: MB (search, similarity 1.0) verifies 1967, Elektra EUK 256, GB release, and both keyTracks. Wikipedia opensearch fetched 'ABC Music' (a 2002 Stereolab compilation) — different entity, discarded; no URL is stored, so no link issue. Unchecked substantive claims: members Keith Rowe (guitar) and Eddie Prevost (percussion); 'London-based'; 'influenced Brian Eno, Cornelius Cardew'. Background-knowledge caution (from judge memory, NOT citable evidence): Cornelius Cardew was an AMM member who performs on AMMMusic, so 'influenced... Cardew' looks garbled — repair should re-source that influence sentence. The no-melody/no-rhythm and 'foundational document' characterizations are editorial.

One substantive claim is uncheckable against pack evidence and forces this verdict: 'Became the first jazz album to go platinum' -- wiki gives Billboard peak #13 and National Recording Registry but says nothing about platinum certification. '15-minute Chameleon' duration and Us3/A Tribe Called Quest sampling also unverifiable. No contradictions found; borderline-clean item.

album:gateway.notes: The albumDNA prose verbatim-matches the entity-correct stored Wikipedia page ('1976 studio album by Gateway', jazz trio John Abercrombie/Dave Holland/Jack DeJohnette, recorded March 1975, released on ECM the following year) -- prose, year, and label are verified. But the MB search hit is a different entity: a 2015 self-released album by another band named Gateway, and our keyTracks are exactly that album's first six tracks in order. Tracks first released 2015-07-23 cannot be key tracks of a 1976 album, so the keyTracks field was populated from the wrong entity. The real 1976 album's tracklist is not in the pack (the wiki extract's track-listing section is empty), so correct keyTracks must be re-sourced.

No entity-matched tracklist exists in the pack, so per the rules this cannot be flagged as a proven mismatch, but the keyTracks almost certainly came from the wrong entity and need re-sourcing.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Pool is an album by John Zorn featuring his early "game piece" composition of the same name which was first released on vinyl on Parachute Records in 1980 as a double album including the composition "Hockey".

album:school.notes: Disease case. The CATALOG FIELDS are correct for School: MB embedded_mbid confirms title 'School', 1978, Parachute P004/6, credited Eugene Chadbourne + John Zorn, and all 5 keyTracks (Solitude/Duet/The Return of Romance/The Shreeve/The Fling) match the MB tracklist. BUT the albumDNA prose and the stored wikipediaUrl are entirely about Zorn's later album 'Pool' (1980) -- the prose's factual skeleton (Pool, 1980 double LP, 'Hockey', Tzadik CD reissue, Parachute Years Box Set) belongs to a different entity. Repair-writer: rewrite DNA from School's facts and replace the wiki link (current page is 'Pool (John Zorn album)').

album:spirits-rejoice-moholo.notes: Right album confirmed: wiki artist page (Louis Moholo) discography lists 'Louis Moholo; Spirits Rejoice!; Ogun: OG520 (1978)' -- matches ours (Ogun, 1978). MB evidence is the 2006 Septet/Octet CD compilation 'Bra Louis - Bra Tebs / Spirits Rejoice!' (secondaryType Compilation), not the 1978 original, hence MB entity 'unsure' -- but all 6 keyTracks (Sonke, Lakutshona Ilanga / Ntyilo-Ntyilo, Unisone, B My Dear, Maybe of Cause, Utshaka) match that compilation tracklist exactly. UNVERIFIABLE / FLAG: the albumDNA names three 'selections' that the keyTracks and evidence do not support -- (a) a track literally titled 'Spirits Rejoice!' is not in the MB tracklist (the album is named that, but no such track appears); (b) 'You Ain't Gonna Know Me 'Cos You Think You Know Me' IS in the MB tracklist (ok); (c) 'Spiritual Knowledge and Grace' appears in the same wiki discography as a SEPARATE 2011 album (Ogun OGCD035), not a track here, and is absent from the MB tracklist. Per the absence-is-not-contradiction rule these are unverifiable rather than proven-wrong, but (a) and (c) look like editorial conflations a repair-writer should fix. wikipediaUrl is the correct artist's bio page, not an album page, hence wikiLink 'unsure'.

album:third-plane.notes: The album is correctly identified (Third Plane, the Carter/Hancock/Williams trio date) -- MB embedded_mbid credits 'Ron Carter', wiki says 'album by jazz bassist Ron Carter', and all 5 keyTracks match. The albumDNA itself correctly states 'album by jazz bassist Ron Carter ... features Carter, Herbie Hancock and Tony Williams', so only the catalog artist field is wrong. Label note: field 'JVC' matches MB (1978 Japanese pressing); DNA 'Milestone label ... 1977' matches the wiki (US original) -- both legitimate, original Milestone 1977 / JVC reissue 1978. wikipediaUrl is the correct album page.

album:saudades.notes: Chimera entry. The albumDNA's opening is verbatim from the Wikipedia article for 'Saudades', the 1980 ECM album BY NANA VASCONCELOS (Gismonti appears only as featured guitarist; wiki personnel: 'Egberto Gismonti - guitar'). Yet the site credits the album to Egberto Gismonti, dates it 1981, and lists keyTracks none of which appear on that album's wiki tracklist ('O Berimbau', 'Vozes (Saudades)', 'Ondas (No olhos de Petronila)', 'Cego aderaldo', 'Dado'); the four listed titles look like Gismonti repertoire from his own albums (background impression, not pack-verified). No MB evidence at all (no release, no candidates). Two repair paths: (a) if the entry means the Vasconcelos album, fix artist, year, and keyTracks; (b) if it means a Gismonti album, then title, prose, and wiki link are all wrong. wikiLinkVerdict left unsure because the stored page is correct for reading (a) and wrong for reading (b). keyTracks were compared against the wiki tracklist since MB had none.

All 5 keyTracks (Trumbology, Clarinet Marmalade, Singin' the Blues, Ostrich Walk, Riverboat Shuffle) match the MB tracklist; the three tracks named in the DNA all exist. DNA call 'an early jazz recording' is accurate for this 1991 compilation of 1920s recordings. ours.artist 'Eddie Lang' is editorially a minor credit (the set is principally Bix/Trumbauer) but Lang IS a credited artist, so not a factual error. MB releaseGroup secondaryType 'Soundtrack' is anomalous metadata, irrelevant to the claims. No wiki evidence/URL.

album:black-and-blues.notes: MB embedded_mbid confirms James Blood Ulmer, 1991 (recorded 1990), DIW. DNA verbatim from matched wiki page ('recorded in 1990 ... released on the Japanese DIW label'). All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist. Remaining DNA is templated editorial.

album:commodore-classics-in-swing-colema.notes: MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms Coleman Hawkins, 1991, Commodore Records, with all 5 keyTracks matching the MB tracklist; the two tracks named in the DNA ('Smack', 'Smack (alt take)') both exist. This is a 1991 reissue of 1940s Commodore sessions; the templated 'early jazz / contemporary era' phrasing is loose editorial but contains no contradicted factual claim. No wiki evidence/URL.

album:faith-moves.notes: MB search match (sim 1.0) confirms a Nicky Skopelitis / Sonny Sharrock collaboration, 1991, CMP Records; all 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. 'free jazz context' is editorial. ours.artist credits Sharrock only; the album is a duo, but naming a co-credited leader is legitimate, not a mismatch.

album:in-private.notes: Despite 'In Private' being cited in the judge brief as a known disease example (Dusty Springfield content substituted for Art Tatum), THIS item's MB embedded_mbid is authoritative and confirms the real entity: Art Tatum, 1991, Fresh Sound Records (FSRCD 368), with all 5 keyTracks (Wrap Your Troubles in Dreams, Sittin' an' a Rockin', You're Drivin' Me Crazy, Tenderly, Over the Rainbow) matching the MB tracklist exactly. The DNA correctly describes an Art Tatum solo-piano Fresh Sound 1991 reissue and names two of those tracks. The only unverifiable claim is 'originally released on Pablo Records in 1976' (MB shows only the 1991 Fresh Sound release; not in evidence, but not contradicted). Entity is strongly confirmed, no contradictions, so clean; flagging the Pablo-1976 origin as unverified background. No wiki page (opensearch null).

album:the-little-big-band-real-life.notes: MB embedded_mbid confirms title, Phil Woods, 1991, Chesky Records (JD47); all 5 keyTracks present. albumDNA calls Woods 'clarinetist' — wiki confirms he was an alto saxophonist AND clarinetist, so consistent. album:the-power-of-cool.notes: MB embedded_mbid confirms title, Donald Harrison, 1991, CTI; all 5 keyTracks present. albumDNA ends with a broken bio fragment ('is an American jazz saxophonist and the Big Chief of The Congo Square Nation...') copied from the wiki lead with the subject dropped — sloppy prose but factually about the correct Donald Harrison (wiki confirms). wrongClaims.ourClaim: A 1997 Tudor date, Blue Rondo finds Charlie Parker in a bebop setting.
wrongClaims.problem: The embedded-MBID release is not a Charlie Parker bebop album; it is a 1997 Tudor various-artists clarinet recital. Parker (d. 1955) appears only as one composer credit among many (Debussy, Poulenc, Honegger, Stravinsky, Brubeck, etc.); the performers are clarinettist Karl-Heinz Steffens, pianist David Gazarov and others. Framing it as Parker 'in a bebop setting' belongs to a different kind of entity.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: artists: Brubeck, Debussy, Donato, Gilberto, Evans, Jones, Honegger, Parker, Poulenc, Stravinsky, Karl-Heinz Steffens, David Gazarov, Martin Zehn, Keith Copeland, Chris Lachotta; tracks include 'Premiere rhapsodie', 'Clarinet Sonatina, H.42', 'Clarinet Sonata, FP184'

album:blue-rondo.notes: Items 'blue-rondo' (Charlie Parker) and 'blue-rondo-brubeck' (Dave Brubeck) both point to the SAME release mbid 505e34e7-b2da-4d89-92a9-72e2d9d1997d, a Tudor classical/jazz clarinet recital. The keyTracks listed here (Blue Rondo a la Turk, Premiere rhapsodie, Waltz for Debby, two Clarinet Sonatina movements) DO appear on that release, so keyTracks technically match; but the artist attribution to Charlie Parker and the 'bebop setting' description misrepresent a multi-composer clarinet album as a Parker bebop date. Classed entity_mismatch on the prose's factual skeleton (Parker/bebop) vs the actual release.

That claim is clearly inapplicable to a 2000 Duoline reissue, but no pack evidence contradicts it for THIS release (the Wiki passage actually asserts it for the same-titled Verve album), so per 'absence is not contradiction / never fill gaps from own knowledge' I mark it unverifiable rather than factual_error. wiki link is to a real same-titled album page but a different release than the Duoline comp = unsure. No wikipedia evidence (opensearch returned nothing). Repair note: confirm tracklist against the original Flying Dutchman pressing rather than the 2002 reissue.

album:concept-of-freedom.notes: The release itself is correctly identified (title 'Concept of Freedom', label hatOLOGY, year 2005, and all 5 keyTracks -- the long 'Comp. No. 257 ... / Freedom No. 1, 4 & 6 From the Sacred Concert No. 2' titles -- match MB exactly), so this is factual_error rather than entity_mismatch. The error is the artist attribution/characterization: MB lists Anthony Braxton FIRST. The track titles use Braxton's composition-numbering system ('Comp. No. 257'); hatOLOGY is a Braxton label home. This is a 2005 avant-garde Braxton work, not a Duke Ellington swing/big-band date. The 'swing vein'/'big band' genre language is editorially uncheckable on its own (no genre field in evidence) but the leader attribution is the concrete, evidence-backed error. No wikipedia evidence (opensearch returned nothing).

No clear single-leader field is contradicted and Stenson is a genuine principal, so not flagged; also the verbatim Trio sentence describes a DIFFERENT lineup (Jormin/Falt) than plays on Parish, which is slightly misleading though literally true per wiki.

All four keyTracks match the MB tracklist ('Lift Off' matches 'Lift Off / Mic Check' by substring; Afro Blue, Cherish the Day, Letter to Hermione present). MB (search, 1.0) matches title/artist/label.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: He performed in the United States and Europe and made many recordings with Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday, Johnny Hodges, and others.
wrongClaims.problem: This sentence is lifted verbatim from the Ben Webster Wikipedia biography (the wrong page stored for this item) and grafted into a Duke Ellington compilation blurb. Attributed to this Ellington album it is incoherent ('made many recordings with Duke Ellington' for an Ellington release) — it is biographical text about saxophonist Ben Webster, not about our album.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Benjamin Francis Webster (March 27, 1909 - September 20, 1973) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist. He performed in the United States and Europe and made many recordings with Duke Ellington, Billie Holiday, Johnny Hodges, and others.

album:blue-note-jazz-inspiration.notes: The album-level facts are correct: MB (embedded) confirms title/artist/year/label (Duke Ellington, 'Blue Note Jazz Inspiration', Blue Note, 2012) and all five keyTracks (Satin Doll, Flamingo, Prelude to a Kiss, Just a Sittin' and a Rockin', Black and Tan Fantasy) match its tracklist — all Ellington tunes. The corruption is a grafted Ben Webster bio sentence plus the stored wikipediaUrl pointing to the Ben Webster page (wrong entity). The 'definitive hard bop label' line is also off for an Ellington big-band compilation but is editorial. Treated as factual_error (right album, one misattributed/grafted claim) rather than full entity_mismatch since the prose's skeleton — big band, Ellington, the tracklist — is Ellington's. Repair: drop the Webster sentence and fix the wiki link.

album:grews-tune.notes: MB (embedded) confirms title/artist/year/label (Mulgrew Miller with Kluvers Big Band, 'Grew's Tune', Stunt Records, 2012) and all five keyTracks match the tracklist. Wikipedia is the entity-matched Mulgrew Miller artist page; the closing sentence about McCoy Tyner's harmonic freedom is verbatim from it (correct artist). Two named tracks ('Thinking Out Loud', 'Return Trip') both present. 'Jazz fusion' is a loose/debatable genre tag for what is a straight-ahead big-band date, but genre labels are editorial, not factual claims, so not flagged.

album:mouvements.notes: Embedded-MBID release matches on artist, year (2012), label (Universal Music Classics & Jazz France) and full tracklist. keyTracks Jay/Mouvements/Maybe Tomorrow/Alpha 01-10-10/Oleo all present. Wiki stored_url is the Biréli Lagrène artist page (correct entity); the prose sentence 'He often performs in swing, jazz fusion, and post-bop styles' is verbatim from that page.

album:renaissance.notes: Right entity (Marcus Miller, Renaissance, 2012). Our label field 'Victor' matches the MB Japanese release (cat VICJ-61665); the albumDNA's 'Concord Jazz' / 'May 28, 2012' is the US release and is quoted verbatim from the matching wiki page. Both labels are genuine for this album, so no contradiction. All keyTracks present in MB tracklist; personnel names (Dr. John, Kris Bowers, etc.) verbatim from wiki.

album:sunrise.notes: Embedded-MBID release credited to 'Masabumi Kikuchi Trio' on ECM (ECM 2096), 2012, tracklist matches; all keyTracks present. Wiki stored_url is the Masabumi Kikuchi artist page (correct entity); the sidemen sentence (Sonny Rollins, Miles Davis, McCoy Tyner, Elvin Jones, Gary Peacock, Paul Motian, Gil Evans, Toru Takemitsu) is verbatim from that page.

album:the-nows.notes: Embedded-MBID release (Clean Feed, 2012-09) credits Ken Vandermark with Paul Lytton, Nate Wooley, Ikue Mori; all five keyTracks present in tracklist. Wiki stored_url is the Ken Vandermark discography page (correct entity); albumDNA is the generic discography-page description quoted verbatim, no specific factual claim about this album beyond what evidence supports.

album:burn.notes: Right entity (Sons of Kemet, Burn, 2013, Naim Records). MB date 2013-09-08 vs prose '9 September 2013' consistent; all keyTracks present. albumDNA incl. the 2013 MOBO 'Best Jazz Act' claim is verbatim from the matching wiki album page.

The albumDNA is overwhelmingly editorial (panoramic sound quilting, family Mississippi roots) — opinion, not flaggable; the checkable skeleton (Matana Roberts, Coin Coin Chapter Two, alto saxophonist, second installment) is consistent with evidence.

album:for-the-love-of-abbey.notes: Embedded-MBID release (Marc Cary, Motema, 2013-06-11) matches; all keyTracks present. Wiki stored_url is the matching album page; albumDNA ('solo piano album by Marc Cary ... recorded in 2012 ... released by Motema Music') is verbatim from it.

album:money-jungle-provocative-in-blue.notes: Album-level content is solidly correct: Terri Lyne Carrington, Concord Jazz, 2013-02-25, all keyTracks present, and the 2013 Grammy (Best Jazz Instrumental Album) claim is verbatim in the matching album wiki. The only issue is the trailing biographical sentence 'She toured with each of Hancock's musical configurations between 1997 and 2007', a specific dated claim absent from both the album wiki and MB — unverifiable against the pack evidence (not contradicted).

album:winter-morning-walks.notes: Embedded-MBID release confirms artist Maria Schneider, title, label ArtistShare (AS0121), 2013-03-01, and all five keyTracks.
